topics.title: NLP Tasks and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT742 - NATURAL LANGAUGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** NLP Tasks and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Natural Language Processing (NLP) Tasks** are well-defined computational problems that operate on human language data (text or speech) and produce structured linguistic or semantic outputs. Each task represents a specific subtask of language understanding or generation, while **NLP Applications** are the real-world systems that combine multiple tasks to solve practical problems.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 1), NLP tasks form the foundational building blocks that any intelligent language system must perform. The major task families are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core NLP Task Categories (KTU 2024 Module 1)**
> 1. **Syntax-level tasks** — Tokenization, POS Tagging, Parsing, Chunking
> 2. **Semantic tasks** — Named Entity Recognition (NER), Word Sense Disambiguation, Semantic Role Labeling
> 3. **Discourse tasks** — Coreference Resolution, Discourse Parsing
> 4. **Pragmatic / Application-level tasks** — Sentiment Analysis, Machine Translation, Question Answering, Text Summarization, Text Classification, Speech Recognition, Chatbots, Text Generation

**Application** refers to a deployed end-user system that integrates one or more NLP tasks to serve a domain-specific purpose such as healthcare, finance, legal, customer service, or social media analytics.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **multilingual librarian** who has just received a huge pile of letters written in dozens of languages by hundreds of people. Before you can file, translate, summarize, or answer questions from these letters, you must perform a series of small jobs:

1. **Break each letter into words** (Tokenization).
2. **Identify which words are names, places, dates** (NER).
3. **Understand the subject and emotion** (Sentiment Analysis).
4. **Translate important letters into another language** (Machine Translation).
5. **Write a short summary of each letter** (Summarization).
6. **Reply to specific questions about the letters** (Question Answering).

Each of these jobs is a **Task**. When you combine them into a service like "Google Translate" or "Alexa", you have built an **Application**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tasks = individual language-understanding subtasks. Applications = complete real-world systems built by combining tasks.**

The **linguistic levels of analysis** that drive these tasks are: **Phonetics/Phonology → Morphology → Lexicon → Syntax → Semantics → Pragmatics → Discourse**. Each NLP task typically targets one or more of these levels.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Systematic Taxonomy of NLP Tasks

#### A. Syntax-Level Tasks

- **Tokenization:** Splitting raw text into minimal units (words, subwords, characters).
  * *Why:* Computers cannot directly process continuous character streams. Tokenization provides discrete units for downstream analysis.
  * *How:* Uses whitespace, rule-based regex, or learned subword models (BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece).

- **Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging:** Assigns grammatical categories (noun, verb, adjective) to each token.
  * *Why:* Disambiguates word function; essential input for parsers and translators.
  * *How:* HMM, CRF, BiLSTM, Transformer-based taggers.

- **Parsing (Constituency & Dependency):** Builds tree structures showing grammatical relationships.
  * *Why:* Reveals who did what to whom — critical for question answering and relation extraction.
  * *How:* CKY algorithm, transition-based parsers, neural parsers.

- **Chunking / Shallow Parsing:** Identifies phrase boundaries (NP, VP) without full hierarchical trees.

#### B. Semantic-Level Tasks

- **Named Entity Recognition (NER):** Detects and classifies proper nouns into categories such as **Person, Organization, Location, Date, Monetary value**.
- **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD):** Selects the correct meaning of a polysemous word based on context (e.g., "bank" → river bank vs. financial bank).
- **Semantic Role Labeling (SRL):** Identifies predicate-argument structure (who did what, to whom, when, where, why).
- **Coreference Resolution:** Links different expressions that refer to the same entity (e.g., "Priya said **she** was tired" — "she" = "Priya").

#### C. Discourse & Pragmatic Tasks

- **Discourse Analysis:** Understands relations between sentences (elaboration, contrast, cause).
- **Pragmatics / Intent Classification:** Interprets the speaker's actual goal, including sarcasm and indirectness.

#### D. Application-Level Tasks

- **Text Classification:** Assigns predefined labels (spam/ham, topic, urgency).
- **Sentiment Analysis:** Determines polarity (positive/negative/neutral) and intensity.
- **Machine Translation (MT):** Converts text from source to target language.
- **Question Answering (QA):** Returns an answer given a question and context.
- **Text Summarization:** Produces a shorter version of a document — *extractive* (selects sentences) or *abstractive* (generates new sentences).
- **Natural Language Generation (NLG):** Produces coherent natural language from structured data.
- **Speech Recognition (ASR) & Text-to-Speech (TTS):** Bridge acoustic and symbolic language.
- **Information Extraction (IE):** Pulls structured facts (relations, events) from unstructured text.
- **Chatbots / Dialogue Systems:** Task-oriented or open-conversational agents.
- **Spell Checking, Autocorrect, Autocomplete:** Language-model driven writing aids.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Definition** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|---|
| Token Frequency | $f(w) = \text{count of token } w$ | Vocabulary building |
| N-gram Probability (MLE) | $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \dfrac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}$ | Language modelling, autocomplete |
| Perplexity | $PP(W) = P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)^{-1/n}$ | Evaluating LMs (lower is better) |
| TF-IDF | $tf\text{-}idf(t,d) = tf(t,d) \cdot \log \dfrac{N}{df(t)}$ | Information retrieval, classification |
| Cosine Similarity | $\text{sim}(A,B) = \dfrac{A \cdot B}{\Vert A \Vert \cdot \Vert B \Vert}$ | Document similarity, retrieval |
| Accuracy | $Acc = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Classification evaluation |
| Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Quality of positive predictions |
| Recall | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Coverage of true positives |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = \dfrac{2PR}{P + R}$ | Balanced single metric |
| BLEU Score | $BLEU = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | Machine translation evaluation |
| ROUGE (ROUGE-N) | $ROUGE\text{-}N = \dfrac{\sum_{S} \sum_{\text{gram}_n \in S} \text{Count}_{match}(\text{gram}_n)}{\sum_{S} \sum_{\text{gram}_n \in S} \text{Count}(\text{gram}_n)}$ | Summarization evaluation |
| Edit Distance (Levenshtein) | $ED(i,j) = \min\{ED(i-1,j)+1,\; ED(i,j-1)+1,\; ED(i-1,j-1)+\delta\}$ | Spell correction, fuzzy matching |
| Cross-Entropy Loss | $L = -\sum_{i} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Training classifiers & LMs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conventions:** TP = True Positive, TN = True Negative, FP = False Positive, FN = False Negative, BP = Brevity Penalty in BLEU, $p_n$ = n-gram precision, $w_n$ = weight (usually $\frac{1}{N}$).

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

NLP applications power virtually every modern digital interaction:

- **Healthcare:** Clinical NLP extracts diagnoses, medications, and symptoms from electronic health records (e.g., IBM Watson Health, Google Med-PaLM).
- **Finance:** Sentiment analysis on news and earnings calls drives algorithmic trading; NER extracts entities from filings for KYC/AML compliance.
- **Legal:** Contract review, e-discovery, and case-law retrieval using text classification and NER (e.g., Luminance, ROSS Intelligence).
- **Customer Service:** Chatbots, ticket classification, and email routing reduce human workload.
- **Search & IR:** Google, Bing, and Elasticsearch rely on tokenization, ranking, semantic embeddings, and QA.
- **Accessibility:** Real-time captioning, screen readers, sign-language avatars.
- **Cybersecurity:** Phishing email detection, threat-intelligence extraction from dark-web text.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner expects students to **map an application to its underlying tasks** and vice versa. A high-yield answer always names both the application domain AND the constituent NLP tasks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough: How an NLP Pipeline Combines Tasks

Consider a real-world system: **"Customer Review Insights Dashboard"** for an e-commerce platform.

**Step 1 — Text Ingestion & Tokenization**
Raw review text is split into tokens. Example: *"The phone's battery dies fast!"* → `["The", "phone", "'s", "battery", "dies", "fast", "!"]`

**Step 2 — Lowercasing & Normalization**
Tokens are lowercased; punctuation removed or separated. Reduces vocabulary size.

**Step 3 — Stop-word Removal (optional)**
Common words like "the", "is" are removed depending on downstream need.

**Step 4 — POS Tagging**
Tags each token with grammatical role. Example: `dies → VBZ (verb, 3rd person singular present)`.

**Step 5 — Named Entity Recognition**
Extracts product names, brand names, locations. Example: `phone → PRODUCT`.

**Step 6 — Sentiment / Polarity Classification**
Assigns sentiment label. Example: *"dies fast"* → **NEGATIVE**, intensity = high.

**Step 7 — Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA)**
Identifies *which feature* the sentiment targets. Example: aspect = `battery`, sentiment = negative.

**Step 8 — Aggregation & Visualization**
Counts positives/negatives per product; generates textual summary via **NLG** (e.g., *"78% of reviews for Phone X mention battery negatively"*).

This single application combines **at least 6 tasks**: Tokenization → POS Tagging → NER → Sentiment Analysis → ABSA → NLG.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Applications to Tasks

| **Application** | **Constituent NLP Tasks** | **Linguistic Levels** | **Industry Domain** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Google Translate | Tokenization, MT, POS Tagging (implicit), Language Detection | Syntax, Semantics, Pragmatics | Travel, Localization |
| Alexa / Siri | ASR, Intent Classification, NER, Dialogue Management, TTS, NLG | Phonology → Pragmatics | Consumer Electronics |
| Spam Filter | Tokenization, Text Classification | Lexicon, Semantics | Email Security |
| ChatGPT / LLM Chatbot | LM, NLU, NLG, QA, Summarization | All levels | General Purpose |
| News Summarization App | Tokenization, Extractive/Abstractive Summarization, NER | Semantics, Discourse | Media, Journalism |
| Medical Coding Assistant | NER, RE, Text Classification, SRL | Semantics, Pragmatics | Healthcare |
| Stock Sentiment Monitor | Sentiment Analysis, NER (companies/tickers), Time-Series Tagging | Semantics, Pragmatics | Finance |
| Plagiarism Detector | Tokenization, N-gram Overlap, Cosine Similarity, Paraphrase Detection | Lexicon, Semantics | Education |
| Grammar Checker (Grammarly) | POS Tagging, Parsing, Spell Correction, Style Transfer | Morphology, Syntax | Education, Writing |
| Resume Screener | Text Classification, NER (skills), Embeddings | Lexicon, Semantics | HR / Recruitment |
| Legal Contract Review | NER, RE, Clause Classification, Coreference Resolution | Syntax, Semantics, Discourse | Legal |
| Social Media Trend Detection | Topic Modeling, NER, Sentiment, Event Extraction | Semantics, Discourse | Marketing, PR |

---

### 3.3 Worked Derivation: BLEU Score Step-by-Step

BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) is the standard metric for machine translation evaluation. The KTU examiner frequently asks for its derivation.

**Step 1 — Compute n-gram precisions $p_n$.**
For each candidate translation $C$ and reference $R$:

$$
p_n = \dfrac{\sum_{\text{gram}_n \in C} \min\!\left(\text{Count}_C(\text{gram}_n),\; \text{MaxCount}_{R}(\text{gram}_n)\right)}{\sum_{\text{gram}_n \in C} \text{Count}_C(\text{gram}_n)}
$$

> *Logic:* The numerator clips the candidate's n-gram count by the maximum count of that n-gram in any reference. This prevents gaming the score via repeated words.

**Step 2 — Combine n-gram precisions using geometric mean.**

$$
\log p_{\text{combined}} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n
$$

Typically $N = 4$ and $w_n = \frac{1}{4}$.

**Step 3 — Apply Brevity Penalty (BP) to penalize too-short translations.**

$$
BP = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } c > r \\ e^{1 - r/c} & \text{if } c \le r \end{cases}
$$

where $c$ = candidate length, $r$ = closest reference length.

**Step 4 — Final BLEU score.**

$$
BLEU = BP \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Derivation: Perplexity of a Language Model

Given a sentence $W = w_1 w_2 \dots w_n$, perplexity measures how well a probability distribution predicts the sample.

**Step 1 — Joint probability via chain rule.**

$$
P(W) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1})
$$

**Step 2 — Average log-probability.**

$$
\log P(W) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})
$$

**Step 3 — Normalize by sentence length.**

$$
\frac{1}{n} \log P(W) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})
$$

**Step 4 — Take the exponential to obtain perplexity.**

$$
PP(W) = P(W)^{-1/n} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})\right)
$$

> *Interpretation:* Lower perplexity ⇒ the model is less "surprised" by the text ⇒ better language model. A perplexity of 100 means the model is as confused as if at each step it had to choose uniformly among 100 words.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Derivation: Edit Distance (Levenshtein)

Used in **spell correction, fuzzy matching, ASR post-processing**.

**Recurrence relation:**

$$
ED(i, j) = \begin{cases}
i & \text{if } j = 0 \\
j & \text{if } i = 0 \\
ED(i-1, j-1) & \text{if } w_i = v_j \\
1 + \min \begin{cases} ED(i-1, j) \\ ED(i, j-1) \\ ED(i-1, j-1) \end{cases} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Operations accounted for:** insertion ($ED(i, j-1)$), deletion ($ED(i-1, j)$), substitution ($ED(i-1, j-1)$). Dynamic programming fills an $(m+1) \times (n+1)$ table in $O(mn)$ time.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.2 Generic NLP Application Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Raw Text or Audio Input] --> P2[Preprocessing Tokenization Normalization]
    P2 --> P3[Syntactic Analysis POS Parsing]
    P3 --> P4[Semantic Analysis NER SRL WSD]
    P4 --> P5[Discourse Analysis Coreference]
    P5 --> P6[Pragmatic Analysis Intent Emotion]
    P6 --> P7[Application Layer Task Execution]
    P7 --> P8[Output Structured Result or Generated Text]

    subgraph Foundation_Models
        M1[Statistical Models HMM CRF SVM]
        M2[Neural Models RNN LSTM Transformer]
        M3[Pre Trained Language Models BERT GPT]
    end

    P2 -.uses.-> Foundation_Models
    P3 -.uses.-> Foundation_Models
    P4 -.uses.-> Foundation_Models
    P5 -.uses.-> Foundation_Models
    P6 -.uses.-> Foundation_Models
    P7 -.uses.-> Foundation_Models
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Differentiate between an NLP task and an NLP application. Give one example of each.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
An **NLP task** is a well-defined computational subtask performed on natural language data, e.g., Named Entity Recognition (NER), POS Tagging, Sentiment Analysis. An **NLP application** is a real-world deployed system that combines one or more NLP tasks to serve a domain-specific purpose. For example, NER (task) is used inside a Resume Parser (application); Sentiment Analysis (task) is used inside a Brand Monitoring Dashboard (application). Tasks are the building blocks; applications are the end-user products that integrate them.

---

**Q2. List any six major NLP tasks and the linguistic level each one operates on.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Tokenization** — Lexical/Morphological level
2. **POS Tagging** — Morpho-syntactic level
3. **Parsing** — Syntactic level
4. **Named Entity Recognition** — Semantic level
5. **Coreference Resolution** — Discourse level
6. **Sentiment Analysis / Intent Classification** — Pragmatic level

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the major categories of NLP tasks with suitable examples. Discuss how syntax-level, semantic-level, and application-level tasks differ. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe a real-world NLP application (e.g., Virtual Assistant) and list all constituent NLP tasks involved in its pipeline. *(7 Marks)*

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Major Categories of NLP Tasks**

NLP tasks are classified based on the linguistic level they target:

- **Syntax-level tasks** focus on the structural arrangement of words. Examples: *Tokenization* (splitting text into words/subwords), *POS Tagging* (labeling grammatical roles), *Parsing* (building constituency/dependency trees), *Chunking* (identifying phrase boundaries).

- **Semantic-level tasks** focus on meaning. Examples: *NER* (detecting persons, places, organizations), *WSD* (selecting the correct sense of a word), *SRL* (identifying who did what to whom), *Coreference Resolution* (linking pronouns/mentions).

- **Application-level tasks** are end-user problems combining multiple lower-level tasks. Examples: *Sentiment Analysis*, *Machine Translation*, *Question Answering*, *Text Summarization*, *Chatbots*, *NLG*.

The progression mirrors linguistic levels: **Phonology → Morphology → Syntax → Semantics → Pragmatics → Discourse**, with application tasks drawing from all underlying levels.

**(b) Real-World Application: Virtual Assistant (e.g., Alexa)**

The pipeline of a virtual assistant is shown in the Mermaid block diagram below:

```mermaid
graph TD
    V1[User Voice Input] --> V2[Speech Recognition ASR Converts to Text]
    V2 --> V3[Tokenization and Normalization]
    V3 --> V4[Intent Classification and NER]
    V4 --> V5[Dialogue State Tracking]
    V5 --> V6[Task Execution Query Database or Call API]
    V6 --> V7[Natural Language Generation NLG]
    V7 --> V8[Text to Speech TTS]
    V8 --> V9[Audio Response to User]
```

**Constituent NLP tasks used:**
1. **ASR (Speech Recognition)** — Acoustic-to-text conversion
2. **Tokenization** — Splitting recognized text
3. **POS Tagging** — Understanding grammar
4. **NER** — Extracting entities (songs, places, contacts)
5. **Intent Classification** — Determining user goal (play, set alarm, call)
6. **Dialogue Management** — Maintaining conversational context (Coreference + Discourse)
7. **Information Retrieval / QA** — Fetching answers from knowledge base
8. **NLG** — Generating textual reply
9. **TTS** — Converting reply to speech

Thus a single application integrates **at least 8 NLP tasks** spanning all linguistic levels.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the following NLP tasks with a one-line example for each: (i) Named Entity Recognition, (ii) Sentiment Analysis, (iii) Machine Translation, (iv) Text Summarization, (v) Question Answering, (vi) Speech Recognition, (vii) Coreference Resolution. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Discuss any five real-world applications of NLP and explain which NLP tasks each application uses. *(7 Marks)*

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Definition + Example for each task:**

| **Task** | **Definition** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|
| (i) **Named Entity Recognition** | Detects and classifies named entities in text into predefined categories. | *"Apple was founded by Steve Jobs in California."* → `Apple = ORG`, `Steve Jobs = PERSON`, `California = LOC` |
| (ii) **Sentiment Analysis** | Determines the polarity (positive/negative/neutral) and intensity of an opinion. | *"The movie was absolutely fantastic!"* → **Positive (intensity: high)** |
| (iii) **Machine Translation** | Automatically converts text from a source language to a target language. | *"How are you?"* (English) → *"Comment ça va?"* (French) |
| (iv) **Text Summarization** | Produces a shorter version of a document preserving key information. | A 500-word news article → a 60-word headline-style summary |
| (v) **Question Answering** | Returns a precise answer to a natural-language question given a context or knowledge base. | *Q: "Who wrote Hamlet?"* → **A: "William Shakespeare"** |
| (vi) **Speech Recognition (ASR)** | Converts spoken audio into written text. | User's voice saying *"Set alarm for 7 AM"* → text `"set alarm for 7 am"` |
| (vii) **Coreference Resolution** | Identifies all expressions in a text that refer to the same entity. | *"Maria went to the market. She bought apples."* → `She → Maria` |

**(b) Five Real-World NLP Applications:**

1. **Google Translate** — Uses *Tokenization, Language Detection, POS Tagging, MT, Named Entity Recognition, Language Modelling*. Domain: cross-language communication.

2. **Spam Email Filter (Gmail)** — Uses *Tokenization, Text Classification, NER (sender/URL detection), N-gram Models, Embeddings*. Domain: cybersecurity and email management.

3. **Virtual Assistant (Siri/Alexa)** — Uses *ASR, Intent Classification, NER, Dialogue Management, QA, NLG, TTS*. Domain: consumer electronics and smart homes.

4. **Clinical Decision Support (e.g., IBM Watson Health)** — Uses *NER (drugs, diseases, symptoms), RE, Text Classification, SRL, NLG*. Domain: healthcare and medical records.

5. **News Aggregation & Summarization (Inshorts, Google News)** — Uses *Tokenization, Extractive/Abstractive Summarization, NER, Topic Modeling, Text Classification*. Domain: media and journalism.

Each application demonstrates how **multiple NLP tasks combine** to deliver a usable product, reinforcing that NLP is fundamentally a **task-composition engineering discipline**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Conflating task with application:** Saying "Sentiment Analysis is used in Twitter" without naming the application (e.g., "Brand Sentiment Dashboard") loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Skipping the linguistic level:** Each task must be tagged to its linguistic level (Syntax / Semantics / Pragmatics / Discourse). KTU examiners award marks explicitly for this.
> 3. **Omitting evaluation metrics:** When asked about MT or Summarization, students must mention **BLEU** and **ROUGE** respectively, with their formulas.
> 4. **No diagram for 7-mark sub-parts:** The KTU 2024 scheme mandates a diagram/figure for any 7-mark question; absence leads to a 1–2 mark penalty.
> 5. **Vague definitions:** "NER finds names" is too vague. The correct phrasing is: *"NER detects and classifies named entities (person, organization, location, date, monetary value) in unstructured text into predefined categories."*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Task vs Application:** Task = single subtask; Application = integrated real-world system.
- ✅ **Seven core task families:** Syntax, Semantic, Discourse, Pragmatic, and four major application-level groups.
- ✅ **Linguistic levels (memorize order):** Phonology → Morphology → Lexicon → Syntax → Semantics → Pragmatics → Discourse.
- ✅ **Must-know tasks:** Tokenization, POS Tagging, Parsing, NER, WSD, SRL, Coreference, Sentiment Analysis, MT, QA, Summarization, NLG, ASR, TTS, Chatbots.
- ✅ **Must-know applications:** Search engines, Virtual Assistants, Healthcare NLP, Finance (sentiment/trading), Legal review, Customer service chatbots, Spam detection, Accessibility tools, Recommendation systems, Social media monitoring.
- ✅ **Must-know formulas:** Perplexity, BLEU, ROUGE, TF-IDF, Cosine Similarity, Edit Distance, Accuracy/Precision/Recall/F1.
- ✅ **NER categories (memorize):** Person, Organization, Location, Date, Time, Money, Percent, Facility.
- ✅ **Summarization types:** Extractive (selects sentences) vs. Abstractive (generates new sentences).
- ✅ **MT metrics:** BLEU (precision-based) and chrF; **Summarization metrics:** ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, ROUGE-L (recall-based).
- ✅ **Pipeline order for an application:** ASR → Tokenization → POS → Parsing → NER/SRL/WSD → Discourse → Intent/Sentiment → Application logic → NLG → TTS.
- ✅ **High-yield mapping:** Always map **Application → Tasks → Linguistic Levels** in answers.
- ✅ **Pre-trained models to know:** BERT, GPT, T5, RoBERTa, Whisper (ASR), NLLB (MT), BART (Summarization).
- ✅ **Industry use cases to mention:** Healthcare (clinical NLP), Finance (KYC/AML), Legal (e-discovery), Education (essay scoring), Accessibility (live captioning).
- ✅ **Examiner loves:** Tables mapping applications to tasks, neat Mermaid/hand-drawn diagrams, real industry examples (Alexa, Google Translate, Grammarly, IBM Watson).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Language Building Blocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT742 - NATURAL LANGAUGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Language Building Blocks # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are Language Building Blocks? In Natural Language Processing (NLP), **Language Building Blocks** refer to the hierarchical linguistic levels that constitute any human language. Just as a building is constructed from bricks, mortar, and beams, human language is constructed from layered units — starting from the smallest sound units and rising to the complex meanings of full conversations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Aligned):** > Language building blocks are the **fundamental structural and functional units of human language**, analyzed across multiple levels — **phonetics, phonology, morphology, lexicon, syntax, semantics, pragmatics, and discourse** — which collectively enable computational systems to process, understand, and generate natural language. The seven canonical building blocks (sometimes called the *levels of linguistic analysis*) are: 1. **Phonetics** – The physical production and perception of speech sounds. 2. **Phonology** – The abstract system of sound patterns in a language. 3. **Morphology** – The structure and formation of words from smaller meaning units. 4. **Lexicon** – The repository of word meanings and vocabulary. 5. **Syntax** – The rules governing sentence structure. 6. **Semantics** – The literal meaning of words and sentences. 7. **Pragmatics & Discourse** – Meaning in context and across sentences. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In PERAT742 Module 1, students must identify and explain the role of each language level in NLP pipelines — from raw speech/text ingestion up to contextual interpretation. This forms the foundational base for all later modules (parsing, semantic role labeling, transformers, etc.). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Think of language like a layered cake 🎂:** Imagine you are building a multi-tier cake that represents a single spoken or written sentence: - The **bottom layer (smallest)** is *Phonetics* — the actual sugar crystals and the sizzling sound when the oven door opens. These are the raw acoustic vibrations. - The **second layer** is *Phonology* — the recipe's rule that says "sugar is grouped and ordered" (e.g., English allows "st" but not "ts" at the start of words). - The **third layer** is *Morphology* — small cake pieces (morphemes) like "un-", "happiness", "-ed" that get assembled. - The **fourth layer** is the *Lexicon* — your personal dictionary of all cake flavors you know. - The **fifth layer** is *Syntax* — the recipe's instructions about the order of assembly (you can't ice the cake before baking it). - The **sixth layer** is *Semantics* — the meaning: "This is a chocolate cake." - The **top layer** is *Pragmatics/Discourse* — when a friend says "The cake smells great," they really mean "Please give me a slice!" — context and intent. > **Just as a baker must master every layer to produce a perfect cake, an NLP engineer must understand every linguistic level to build intelligent language systems.** --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used at Each Level | Level | Standard Metric / Constant | Notation | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | Phonetics | **Formant frequencies (F1, F2, F3)** | $F_1, F_2, F_3$ in Hz | Identify vowels uniquely | | Phonology | **IPA (International Phonetic Alphabet)** set | 107 symbols | Universal sound notation | | Morphology | **Average morphemes per word** | 1.5 – 2.5 morphemes/word | Measure word complexity | | Lexicon | **Vocabulary size** | 20,000 – 35,000 base words (English) | Measure language richness | | Syntax | **Average sentence length** | 15 – 20 words/sentence | Syntactic complexity measure | | Semantics | **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD) accuracy** | 65% – 95% | Measure semantic processing | | Pragmatics | **Coreference resolution F1 score** | 75% – 90% | Measure discourse coherence | > [!NOTE] > The **bold** values above are standard empirical benchmarks used in NLP literature and KTU-expected reference points. --- ## 1.4 Why is This Important for NLP? Every NLP task maps to one or more language levels: - **Speech Recognition** → Phonetics + Phonology - **Tokenization & Lemmatization** → Morphology - **Spell-checkers & Autocomplete** → Lexicon - **Grammar checking & Parsing** → Syntax - **Machine Translation & QA Systems** → Semantics - **Chatbots & Sentiment Analysis** → Pragmatics Understanding these blocks is the **prerequisite for designing any modern NLP pipeline**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Seven Levels — Detailed Breakdown ### 🔹 Level 1: Phonetics Phonetics is the **scientific study of the physical properties of human speech sounds** — how they are produced (articulatory), transmitted (acoustic), and perceived (auditory). **Three Branches of Phonetics:** 1. **Articulatory Phonetics** — Studies how speech organs (tongue, lips, vocal cords) produce sounds. 2. **Acoustic Phonetics** — Studies the physical properties (frequency, amplitude) of the sound waves. 3. **Auditory Phonetics** — Studies how the human ear and brain perceive sounds. **Key Concept: Formants** Formants are **concentrations of acoustic energy** at specific frequencies in the speech spectrum. Each vowel is uniquely identified by its first three formants (F1, F2, F3). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why it matters for NLP:** Speech-to-text systems (e.g., Whisper, Google Speech-to-Text) convert raw audio waveforms into phoneme sequences using formant analysis and acoustic models like HMMs (Hidden Markov Models) or CTC (Connectionist Temporal Classification). --- ### 🔹 Level 2: Phonology Phonology studies the **abstract, systematic organization of sounds in a particular language**. It deals with *which* sounds are meaningful (phonemes) and *how* they combine. **Key Concepts:** - **Phoneme**: The smallest unit of sound that can distinguish meaning. Example: "bat" vs "pat" — /b/ and /p/ are different phonemes. - **Allophone**: Different physical realizations of the same phoneme. Example: The /p/ in "pin" (aspirated, $p^h$) and "spin" (unaspirated, p). - **Phonotactics**: Rules governing allowed sound combinations in a language. **Why it matters for NLP:** Phonological rules help in **text normalization**, **grapheme-to-phoneme (G2P) conversion**, and **speech synthesis (TTS)**. --- ### 🔹 Level 3: Morphology Morphology is the study of the **internal structure of words** and the smallest meaningful units called **morphemes**. **Types of Morphemes:** 1. **Free Morpheme** — Can stand alone (e.g., *cat*, *run*, *happy*). 2. **Bound Morpheme** — Cannot stand alone (e.g., *-ing*, *un-*, *-ly*, *-ed*). **Two Morphological Processes:** - **Inflection**: Grammatical modification without changing word class (e.g., *run → running, runs, ran*). - **Derivation**: Creates a new word, often changing word class (e.g., *happy (adj) → happiness (noun)*). **Key Formula — Morpheme-per-Word Ratio:** $$MPW = \frac{\text{Total Morphemes in Corpus}}{\text{Total Words in Corpus}}$$ > [!NOTE] > A high MPW indicates a **morphologically rich language** like Turkish (MPW ≈ 2.5) or Finnish, while English has a relatively low MPW ≈ 1.5. --- ### 🔹 Level 4: Lexicon (Words / Vocabulary) The lexicon is the **mental dictionary of a language** — a structured repository of all known words, their meanings, pronunciations, parts of speech, and relationships. **Lexical Categories (Open Class Words):** - **Nouns** (boy, honesty) - **Verbs** (run, think) - **Adjectives** (red, beautiful) - **Adverbs** (quickly, very) **Closed Class Words (Function Words):** - Prepositions, conjunctions, determiners, pronouns, auxiliary verbs. **Lexical Relations:** - **Synonymy** — words with similar meanings (big/large). - **Antonymy** — opposites (hot/cold). - **Hyponymy** — is-a relationship (rose ⊂ flower). - **Homonymy** — same spelling/sound, different meaning (bank: river bank / financial bank). - **Polysemy** — one word with multiple related meanings (head: body part / head of department). --- ### 🔹 Level 5: Syntax Syntax governs the **arrangement of words into well-formed sentences** and the rules defining grammatical structure. **Key Concepts:** - **Grammar** — The set of rules defining a language's structure. - **Constituency** — Words group into phrases (NP, VP, PP). - **Grammatical Relations** — Subject, Object, Predicate. **Two Formal Approaches:** 1. **Constituency Grammar (CFGs — Context-Free Grammars):** $$S \rightarrow NP \;\; VP$$ $$NP \rightarrow Det \;\; N$$ $$VP \rightarrow V \;\; NP$$ 2. **Dependency Grammar:** Words are linked by binary asymmetric relations (head-dependent). --- ### 🔹 Level 6: Semantics Semantics is the study of **literal meaning** — what words, phrases, and sentences mean independent of context. **Key Sub-areas:** - **Lexical Semantics** — Meaning of individual words. - **Compositional Semantics** — How word meanings combine to form sentence meaning (via the **Principle of Compositionality**). - **Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD)** — Choosing the correct meaning of a polysemous word based on context. **Principle of Compositionality (Frege's Principle):** > *The meaning of a complex expression is determined by the meanings of its constituent parts and the rules used to combine them.* Formally: $$[[S]] = f([[W_1]], [[W_2]], \ldots, [[W_n]])$$ where $[[ \cdot ]]$ denotes semantic interpretation, and $f$ is the composition function. --- ### 🔹 Level 7: Pragmatics & Discourse **Pragmatics** studies **meaning in use** — how context affects interpretation. **Discourse** extends this to the meaning of connected sentences and texts. **Key Concepts:** - **Speech Acts** (Austin, Searle): *Locutionary, Illocutionary, Perlocutionary* acts. - **Implicature** (Grice): What is meant beyond what is said. - **Reference & Anaphora Resolution**: Identifying what pronouns refer to (*"John bought a phone. **He** liked it."* → *He* = John, *it* = phone). - **Coherence vs. Cohesion**: Coherence = logical unity; Cohesion = linguistic surface connections. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation | Unit / Domain | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Morpheme-per-Word Ratio | $MPW = \frac{M_{total}}{W_{total}}$ | Scalar | morphemes/word | | 2 | Phoneme Inventory Size | $\vert P \vert$ | Set Cardinality | phonemes | | 3 | Perplexity (Language Model) | $PP(W) = P(w_1 w_2 \ldots w_N)^{-\frac{1}{N}}$ | Probability-based | unitless | | 4 | Constituency Rule (CFG) | $S \rightarrow NP \;\; VP$ | Grammar Rule | Symbolic | | 5 | Compositional Semantics | $[[S]] = f([[w_1]], \ldots, [[w_n]])$ | Function | Semantic Value | | 6 | WSD Accuracy | $Acc = \frac{\#\text{correct senses}}{\#\text{total instances}}$ | Ratio | % (0–100) | | 7 | BLEU Score (N-gram overlap) | $BLEU = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | Geometric mean of n-gram precisions × brevity penalty | 0 to 1 | | 8 | F1 Score (Coreference) | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean | 0 to 1 | | 9 | Speech Sampling Rate (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Frequency | Hz | | 10 | Average Sentence Length | $ASL = \frac{\text{Total Words}}{\text{Total Sentences}}$ | Ratio | words/sentence | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the **backbone of NLP evaluation metrics** used in real-world projects at companies like Google, Microsoft, and Amazon. KTU exam questions frequently test your ability to apply BLEU, Perplexity, and F1 in context. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Google Translate** uses all 7 levels — phonetics for speech input, syntax for parsing source language, semantics for translation, pragmatics for cultural adaptation. - **Apple Siri / Alexa** combines phonetics + phonology for ASR (Automatic Speech Recognition) and pragmatics for intent classification. - **Search Engines (Google, Bing)** rely on lexical semantics (TF-IDF, embeddings) and pragmatics (query understanding). - **Healthcare NLP (e.g., IBM Watson Health)** uses morphology + syntax for medical entity extraction from clinical records. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Morpheme-per-Word Ratio (MPW) **Problem Context:** A corpus contains **120,000 words** and the morphological analyzer identifies **264,000 morphemes**. Compute the MPW. **Step-by-Step Derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Identify given values.} \\ &\text{Total Words} = W_{total} = 120{,}000 \\ &\text{Total Morphemes} = M_{total} = 264{,}000 \\ \\ \text{Step 2: Apply the MPW formula.} \\ &MPW = \frac{M_{total}}{W_{total}} \\ \\ \text{Step 3: Substitute numerical values.} \\ &MPW = \frac{264{,}000}{120{,}000} \\ \\ \text{Step 4: Simplify the fraction.} \\ &MPW = \frac{264}{120} = \frac{22}{10} = 2.2 \\ \\ \text{Step 5: Interpret the result.} \\ &\text{An MPW of } 2.2 \text{ indicates a morphologically moderately rich language.} \\ &\text{English: } MPW \approx 1.5 \\ &\text{Turkish: } MPW \approx 2.5 \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $MPW = 2.2$ morphemes per word. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Perplexity of a Language Model **Problem Context:** A unigram language model assigns probability $P(\text{“the cat sat”}) = 0.000125$ for a 3-word sentence. Compute the perplexity. **Step-by-Step Derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: State the Perplexity formula.} \\ &PP(W) = P(w_1 w_2 \ldots w_N)^{-\frac{1}{N}} \\ \\ \text{Step 2: Identify variables.} \\ &P(W) = 0.000125 = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \\ &N = 3 \\ \\ \text{Step 3: Take the logarithm (base 10) of P(W) for easier computation.} \\ &\log_{10}(P(W)) = \log_{10}(1.25 \times 10^{-4}) \\ &\log_{10}(P(W)) = \log_{10}(1.25) + \log_{10}(10^{-4}) \\ &\log_{10}(P(W)) = 0.0969 - 4 = -3.9031 \\ \\ \text{Step 4: Compute } -\frac{1}{N} \cdot \log_{10}(P(W)). \\ &-\frac{1}{3} \times (-3.9031) = +1.3010 \\ \\ \text{Step 5: Compute Perplexity as 10 raised to the result.} \\ &PP(W) = 10^{1.3010} \\ \\ \text{Step 6: Evaluate the exponential.} \\ &PP(W) \approx 20.0 \\ \\ \text{Step 7: Interpretation.} \\ &\text{Perplexity } 20 \text{ means the model is as confused as if it had to choose uniformly among 20 words at each step.} \\ &\text{Lower perplexity } \Rightarrow \text{ better language model.} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $PP(\text{“the cat sat”}) \approx 20.0$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: BLEU Score (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) **Problem Context:** A candidate translation contains: - 1-gram precision: $p_1 = 0.80$ - 2-gram precision: $p_2 = 0.60$ - Brevity Penalty: $BP = 1.0$ (length is good) - Weights: $w_1 = w_2 = 0.5$ Compute BLEU-2 score. **Step-by-Step Derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: State the BLEU formula for n-grams up to N.} \\ &BLEU_N = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right) \\ \\ \text{Step 2: Substitute N = 2.} \\ &BLEU_2 = BP \cdot \exp\left(w_1 \log p_1 + w_2 \log p_2\right) \\ \\ \text{Step 3: Substitute numerical values.} \\ &BLEU_2 = 1.0 \cdot \exp\left(0.5 \cdot \log(0.80) + 0.5 \cdot \log(0.60)\right) \\ \\ \text{Step 4: Compute the logarithms (natural log).} \\ &\log(0.80) = -0.2231 \\ &\log(0.60) = -0.5108 \\ \\ \text{Step 5: Apply weights and sum.} \\ &0.5 \cdot (-0.2231) + 0.5 \cdot (-0.5108) \\ &= -0.1116 + (-0.2554) \\ &= -0.3670 \\ \\ \text{Step 6: Compute the exponential.} \\ &BLEU_2 = e^{-0.3670} \\ &BLEU_2 \approx 0.6928 \\ \\ \text{Step 7: Final Interpretation.} \\ &\text{A BLEU-2 score of } 0.69 \text{ indicates a reasonably good translation.} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $BLEU_2 \approx 0.69$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Level-to-NLP-Task Mapping | Language Level | Linguistic Unit | Formal Tool | Core NLP Task | Real-World Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Phonetics** | Acoustic signal | Waveform, Spectrogram | ASR (Speech-to-Text) | Siri, Google Voice | | **Phonology** | Phoneme | IPA, G2P models | Text-to-Speech (TTS) | Voice assistants, audiobooks | | **Morphology** | Morpheme | Finite-state transducer | Tokenization, Lemmatization, POS tagging | Search engines, spell-checkers | | **Lexicon** | Word | WordNet, Embeddings (Word2Vec) | Word Sense Disambiguation, NER | Sentiment analysis | | **Syntax** | Phrase / Sentence | CFG, PCFG, Dependency parser | Parsing, Grammar checking | Grammarly, Autocorrect | | **Semantics** | Proposition | Lambda calculus, FOL, NLI models | Question Answering, MT | ChatGPT, Google Translate | | **Pragmatics/Discourse** | Conversation | Coreference resolver, Dialogue acts | Chatbots, Summarization | Alexa, customer-service bots | --- ## 3.5 Worked Example: Sentence Analysis Across All 7 Levels **Input Sentence:** *"The teacher told the students to open their books quietly."* **Multi-level Analysis:** 1. **Phonetics:** The sentence contains 12 phonemes in "teacher" /ˈtiː.tʃər/, including the voiced palatal fricative /ʧ/ (a stop). 2. **Phonology:** Phonotactic rule — English allows "tr" clusters (as in "their") but not "tl". 3. **Morphology:** Words decomposed: - *teacher* = teach + -er (derivational) - *told* = tell + past tense -d (irregular inflection) - *students* = student + -s (plural inflection) - *open* = free morpheme (verb) - *their* = free morpheme (possessive) - *books* = book + -s (plural) - *quietly* = quiet + -ly (derivational, adjective → adverb) 4. **Lexicon:** Each word has an entry in the mental dictionary (POS, meaning, pronunciation). 5. **Syntax (CFG parse):** $S \rightarrow NP \; VP$ $NP \rightarrow Det \; N$ ("The teacher") $VP \rightarrow V \; NP \; S_{embedded}$ 6. **Semantics:** *"The teacher (agent) caused the students (recipient) to perform the action of opening (predicate) their books (theme) in a quiet manner (manner adverb)."* 7. **Pragmatics:** The teacher likely expects compliance — the sentence is an **imperative-like directive**, despite grammatical form being infinitive. This single sentence demonstrates how **all 7 levels interact** in even the most basic NLP pipeline. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Language Hierarchy (Bottom-Up Pyramid) ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef levelStyle fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#1A1A1A classDef topStyle fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,stroke-width:3px,color:#1A1A1A L1["PHONETICS
Physical Speech Sounds
Waveforms Formants"]:::levelStyle L2["PHONOLOGY
Sound System Rules
Phonemes Allophones"]:::levelStyle L3["MORPHOLOGY
Word Structure
Morphemes Affixes"]:::levelStyle L4["LEXICON
Word Repository
Vocabulary Meanings"]:::levelStyle L5["SYNTAX
Sentence Structure
Grammatical Rules"]:::levelStyle L6["SEMANTICS
Literal Meaning
Sense Composition"]:::levelStyle L7["PRAGMATICS DISCOURSE
Contextual Meaning
Speech Acts Intents"]:::topStyle L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 L5 --> L6 L6 --> L7 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Each level builds upon the previous one, mirroring how a child first perceives sounds (phonetics) before mastering abstract sentence meaning (pragmatics). --- ## 4.2 NLP Pipeline — From Raw Input to Understanding ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef inputStyle fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px classDef procStyle fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:2px classDef outputStyle fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px A[Raw Speech or Text Input]:::inputStyle B[Phonetic and Phonological Processing]:::procStyle C[Tokenization and Morphological Analysis]:::procStyle D[Lexical Lookup and Word Sense Disambiguation]:::procStyle E[Syntactic Parsing CFG Dependency]:::procStyle F[Semantic Role Labeling and Interpretation]:::procStyle G[Pragmatic and Discourse Analysis]:::procStyle H[Final NLP Output Translation QA Chatbot]:::outputStyle A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H ``` > **Engineering note:** In real systems, these stages often run **in parallel** or **iteratively** (e.g., modern transformers process semantics and syntax jointly). --- ## 4.3 Morphology — Word Formation Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef morphStyle fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,stroke-width:2px classDef wordStyle fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:3px W["unhappiness
NOUN"]:::wordStyle M1["un-
PREFIX: negation"]:::morphStyle M2["happy
ROOT: adjective"]:::morphStyle M3["-ness
SUFFIX: noun forming"]:::morphStyle W --> M1 W --> M2 W --> M3 M1 --> R1["Meaning: NOT"]:::morphStyle M2 --> R2["Meaning: joyful state"]:::morphStyle M3 --> R3["Meaning: quality of being"]:::morphStyle ``` > **Reading the tree:** *Unhappiness* is a derived noun composed of three morphemes: a derivational prefix *un-*, the root adjective *happy*, and a derivational suffix *-ness*. --- ## 4.4 Syntax Tree — Constituency Parse ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef npStyle fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px classDef vpStyle fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825,stroke-width:2px classDef sStyle fill:#E8EAF6,stroke:#283593,stroke-width:3px S["S"]:::sStyle NP1["NP"]:::npStyle VP["VP"]:::vpStyle Det1["Det: The"]:::npStyle N1["N: cat"]:::npStyle V["V: chased"]:::vpStyle NP2["NP"]:::npStyle Det2["Det: the"]:::npStyle N2["N: mouse"]:::npStyle S --> NP1 S --> VP NP1 --> Det1 NP1 --> N1 VP --> V VP --> NP2 NP2 --> Det2 NP2 --> N2 ``` > **Reading the tree:** The sentence *"The cat chased the mouse"* is decomposed into a Noun Phrase (subject) and a Verb Phrase (predicate), with a nested NP as the object. --- ## 4.5 Semantic Representation — Predicate-Argument Structure ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef argStyle fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#00695C,stroke-width:2px classDef predStyle fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:3px PRED["CHASE predicate
Agent causes motion of Theme"]:::predStyle AGENT["Agent: The cat"]:::argStyle THEME["Theme: The mouse"]:::argStyle TENSE["Tense: Past"]:::argStyle PRED --> AGENT PRED --> THEME PRED --> TENSE ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Semantic Role Labeling (SRL) identifies *who did what to whom*. Here, "The cat" is the AGENT performing CHASE on "The mouse" (THEME) in past tense. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > KTU Part A questions are **direct, definition-based** and require crisp, 3–4 sentence answers. --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define the following with examples:** (a) Morpheme (b) Phoneme (c) Allophone **Model Answer:** (a) **Morpheme**: A morpheme is the smallest meaningful unit in a language that cannot be further divided without losing its meaning. - **Free morpheme**: *cat, run, happy* — can stand alone. - **Bound morpheme**: *-ing, un-, -ly* — must attach to a free morpheme. (b) **Phoneme**: A phoneme is the smallest unit of sound in a language that can distinguish meaning between words. - Example: In English, /p/ (as in *pin*) and /b/ (as in *bin*) are distinct phonemes; replacing one with the other changes word meaning. (c) **Allophone**: Allophones are different physical realizations of the same phoneme, occurring in complementary environments. - Example: The aspirated [pʰ] in *pin* and the unaspirated [p] in *spin* are allophones of the phoneme /p/ in English. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Explain the Principle of Compositionality with an example.** **Model Answer:** The **Principle of Compositionality** (Frege) states that the meaning of a complex linguistic expression is determined by: 1. The meanings of its constituent parts (words), and 2. The rules used to combine them. **Example:** The sentence *"The dog bites the man"* is meaningful because: - *The dog* (NP) means "a specific dog." - *bites* (V) means "the action of biting." - *the man* (NP) means "a specific man." - The combination rule (Subject-Verb-Object) yields: "The dog performs biting on the man." Formally: $$[[\text{The dog bites the man}]] = \text{Event}(biting, agent=dog, patient=man)$$ This principle is foundational in **formal semantics** and modern NLP models (e.g., compositional embeddings). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU Part B questions require **detailed, structured answers with examples, diagrams, and applications**. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the seven levels of linguistic analysis used in NLP. How does each level contribute to a typical NLP pipeline? **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** **Introduction (1 mark):** Human language is a complex system that can be systematically studied across seven hierarchical levels. Each level provides insights required for designing robust NLP applications. **The Seven Levels (5 marks):** 1. **Phonetics** — Study of physical speech sounds (production, transmission, perception). - *NLP role*: Speech recognition systems (Whisper, HMM-based ASR). 2. **Phonology** — Abstract sound patterns and rules of a language (phonemes, allophones, phonotactics). - *NLP role*: Text-to-phoneme conversion in TTS systems. 3. **Morphology** — Structure of words from morphemes (free, bound, inflectional, derivational). - *NLP role*: Tokenization, stemming, lemmatization. 4. **Lexicon** — Mental dictionary of words, meanings, and lexical relations. - *NLP role*: Word sense disambiguation, lexical resources like WordNet. 5. **Syntax** — Rules of sentence formation (CFGs, dependency grammars). - *NLP role*: Parsers, grammar checkers (Grammarly), POS taggers. 6. **Semantics** — Literal meaning of words, phrases, and sentences. - *NLP role*: Question answering, semantic role labeling, machine translation. 7. **Pragmatics & Discourse** — Meaning in context, speech acts, coreference. - *NLP role*: Chatbots, dialogue systems, summarization. **Diagram (1 mark):** *(Refer to the pyramid in Section 4.1)* **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Modern end-to-end NLP systems (e.g., Transformers) often integrate multiple levels, but understanding each level separately is essential for diagnosing errors and designing specialized components. --- #### **(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between inflectional and derivational morphology with suitable examples. Why is morphological analysis important for NLP applications like search engines and machine translation? **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Model Answer:** **Comparison Table (3.5 marks):** | Feature | Inflectional Morphology | Derivational Morphology | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Adds grammatical information without changing word class | Creates a new word, often changing word class | | **Word Class** | Remains the same (e.g., verb → verb) | Often changes (e.g., adjective → noun) | | **Meaning Change** | Minor grammatical shift | Major semantic shift | | **Position** | Usually suffix | Prefix, suffix, or circumfix | | **Productivity** | Highly productive | Moderately productive | | **Example** | *walk → walks, walked, walking* | *happy (adj) → happiness (n)*, *kind (adj) → unkind (adj)* | | **Examples — Bound Morphemes** | *-s, -ed, -ing, -er, -est* | *-ness, -tion, -ment, un-, re-* | **Examples (1.5 marks):** - *The teacher marked the assignment.* - *teacher* = teach (root) + -er (derivational suffix) → noun form of verb *teach* - *marked* = mark (root) + -ed (inflectional past tense) → still a verb - *Their quickness impressed the team.* - *quickness* = quick (adjective) + -ness (derivational) → noun - *impressed* = impress + -ed (inflectional past tense) → still a verb **Importance in NLP (2 marks):** 1. **Search Engines**: When a user searches *"running shoes"*, a morphological analyzer can map it to the base form *run* + *shoe*, enabling broader document matching. 2. **Machine Translation**: Languages like German or Russian have rich inflectional systems. A word like *go* in English may translate to multiple German forms (*gehe, ging, gegangen*). Morphological analysis helps disambiguate. 3. **Information Retrieval (IR)**: Stemming and lemmatization rely on inflectional analysis to normalize word forms. 4. **Named Entity Recognition (NER)**: Recognizing that *New York* + -er → *New Yorker* is a derivational process helps in entity classification. **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Morphological analysis is foundational for **text normalization, vocabulary control, and cross-lingual transfer** in NLP. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** #### **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concepts of syntax and semantics in NLP. With an example sentence, demonstrate how syntax and semantics work together to produce meaning. **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **Model Answer:** **Syntax (3 marks):** - Syntax is the set of rules governing the **arrangement of words into well-formed sentences**. - It defines **grammatical structure** through: - **Part-of-Speech (POS) categories** — noun, verb, adjective, etc. - **Phrasal structures** — Noun Phrase (NP), Verb Phrase (VP), Prepositional Phrase (PP). - **Grammatical relations** — subject, object, modifier. - Formalisms include **Context-Free Grammars (CFGs)** and **Dependency Grammars**. **Semantics (3 marks):** - Semantics is the study of **literal, context-independent meaning** of words, phrases, and sentences. - It includes: - **Lexical semantics** — meaning of individual words. - **Compositional semantics** — combining word meanings to derive sentence meaning. - **Word sense disambiguation (WSD)** — selecting the correct meaning of polysemous words. **Worked Example (1 mark):** *Sentence: "The cat is on the mat."* - **Syntactic Analysis**: $S \rightarrow NP \; VP$ $NP \rightarrow Det \; N$ = "The cat" $VP \rightarrow V \; PP$ = "is on the mat" $PP \rightarrow P \; NP$ = "on the mat" - **Semantic Analysis**: $$[[\text{The cat is on the mat}]] = \text{on}(x, y) \land \text{cat}(x) \land \text{mat}(y)$$ This represents: there exists an entity $x$ (a cat) located on entity $y$ (a mat). **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Syntax provides **structural validity**, while semantics provides **meaningful interpretation**. Together, they enable NLP systems to extract and represent knowledge from natural language. --- #### **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of pragmatics and discourse analysis in NLP. Illustrate with examples how coreference resolution and speech act theory are applied in conversational AI systems like chatbots. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** **Pragmatics (1.5 marks):** - Pragmatics studies **meaning in context** — what a speaker implies beyond the literal words. - It involves **speech acts, implicatures, deixis, and presuppositions**. **Discourse Analysis (1.5 marks):** - Discourse extends pragmatics to **multi-sentence texts and conversations**. - Key tasks: **coherence, cohesion, coreference resolution, topic modeling**. **Coreference Resolution (2 marks):** - Identifying when different expressions in a text refer to the same entity. - **Example**: *"Maria bought a laptop. **She** said **it** was expensive."* → *She* = Maria, *it* = the laptop. - **Application in chatbots**: When a user says *"I forgot my password. Can you help me reset it?"*, the chatbot must resolve *it* → *my password* to give the correct response. **Speech Act Theory (2 marks):** - Austin & Searle classified utterances into three acts: 1. **Locutionary act** — the literal utterance. 2. **Illocutionary act** — the intent (request, promise, warning). 3. **Perlocutionary act** — the effect on the listener. - **Example**: *"Can you pass the salt?"* - Locutionary: A yes/no question. - Illocutionary: A **request**. - Perlocutionary: Listener hands over the salt. - **Application in chatbots**: Modern intent classifiers (e.g., Rasa, Dialogflow) use speech act classification to map *"Can you…?"* to a REQUEST intent, not a literal question. **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Pragmatics and discourse analysis transform NLP from mere **literal processing** to **true contextual understanding** — the hallmark of human-like conversational AI. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1**: Students often confuse **phoneme and morpheme**. Remember: phoneme = *sound*, morpheme = *meaning unit*. > - **Common Mistake 2**: Inflectional vs. derivational morphology — students mix up examples. **Test**: Does it change the word class? → Derivational. > - **Common Mistake 3**: In syntax questions, students forget to draw the **parse tree** or write CFG rules. Always include a diagram for full marks. > - **Common Mistake 4**: In semantics, students omit the **Principle of Compositionality** — mentioning it explicitly earns extra credit. > - **Common Mistake 5**: For pragmatics, students fail to distinguish **locutionary, illocutionary, perlocutionary** acts. KTU frequently tests this with 3-mark questions. > - **Mark loss** can be up to 30% if examples are missing — always support theoretical answers with **at least one concrete example**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) ### 🔑 **Core Definitions (Must Memorize)** - [ ] **Phoneme** vs. **Allophone** vs. **Morpheme** - [ ] **Free vs. Bound Morpheme** with examples - [ ] **Inflectional vs. Derivational Morphology** with examples - [ ] **Synonymy, Antonymy, Hyponymy, Polysemy, Homonymy** - [ ] **Constituency Grammar vs. Dependency Grammar** - [ ] **Principle of Compositionality** (Frege) - [ ] **Locutionary, Illocutionary, Perlocutionary Acts** - [ ] **Coherence vs. Cohesion** ### 🔑 **Key Formulas (Must Know)** - [ ] $MPW = \frac{M_{total}}{W_{total}}$ - [ ] $PP(W) = P(w_1 \ldots w_N)^{-\frac{1}{N}}$ - [ ] $BLEU_N = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ - [ ] $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ - [ ] $[[S]] = f([[w_1]], \ldots, [[w_n]])$ (Compositional Semantics) - [ ] $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ (Nyquist Sampling) ### 🔑 **Diagrams to Practice** - [ ] Language hierarchy pyramid (7 levels) - [ ] NLP pipeline flow diagram - [ ] CFG parse tree (e.g., *"The cat chased the mouse"*) - [ ] Morphology decomposition tree (e.g., *unhappiness*) - [ ] Semantic role labeling diagram (AGENT, THEME, INSTRUMENT) ### 🔑 **Real-World Applications to Mention** - [ ] Google Translate → uses all 7 levels - [ ] Siri/Alexa → phonetics + pragmatics - [ ] Grammarly → syntax + morphology - [ ] ChatGPT → semantics + pragmatics + discourse - [ ] Search engines → lexicon + morphology ### 🔑 **Exam Strategy (KTU 2024 Scheme)** - [ ] For **3-mark questions**: 1 definition + 1 example + 1 application = full marks. - [ ] For **7-mark sub-parts**: definition + 3–4 sub-points + diagram/example + conclusion. - [ ] Always **draw a diagram** wherever the question says *"with a neat diagram"*. - [ ] Use **bullet points and tables** for comparison questions (inflectional vs. derivational). - [ ] End with a **one-line conclusion** summarizing the topic. ### 🔑 **Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid** - ❌ Don't confuse *phonetics* (physical) with *phonology* (abstract/systematic). - ❌ Don't say *morpheme = word* — a word may contain multiple morphemes. - ❌ Don't use *"syntax"* and *"semantics"* interchangeably. - ❌ Don't forget that **pragmatics = context-dependent meaning**, not just literal meaning. - ❌ In Part B, always include **at least one example, one diagram, and one real-world application**.

topics.title: Challenges of NLP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT742 - NATURAL LANGAUGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Challenges of NLP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Challenges of Natural Language Processing (NLP)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):** *Natural Language Processing (NLP) Challenges* refer to the set of inherent computational, linguistic, semantic, and pragmatic difficulties encountered when designing algorithms and systems that attempt to automatically understand, interpret, generate, and reason over human (natural) languages. These challenges arise due to the **ambiguity**, **context-sensitivity**, **creativity**, **evolutionary nature**, and **implicit knowledge** embedded in human language.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme PERAT742 (Natural Language Processing)** syllabus, Module 1 introduces NLP as a sub-field of **Artificial Intelligence (AI)** and **Computational Linguistics**, and explicitly identifies the *challenges* of NLP as the primary motivation for studying formal language models, parsing techniques, and statistical methods later in the course.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Universal Translator Problem"**
>
> Imagine giving a rulebook to a tourist visiting Kerala. The rulebook is written in strict, mathematical English. Now imagine the tourist trying to understand a local *Mappila song*, a WhatsApp forward full of typos, a sarcastic political meme, and a college friend's Instagram caption that says *"vibe check passed bro"* — all using the **same rulebook**. The rulebook keeps failing. **That is exactly the challenge of NLP.** Human language is not a clean rulebook; it is messy, contextual, emotional, and constantly evolving. Computers, by default, love clean rulebooks.

So when we say "challenges of NLP," we are essentially saying: *"Why is it so hard for machines to do what 5-year-old humans do effortlessly?"* The answer lies in **5 root causes** which we will explore below.

### 1.3 The Five Root Causes of NLP Challenges

According to the **Jurafsky & Martin** classical taxonomy (which forms the implicit backbone of the KTU NLP syllabus), NLP challenges can be grouped into **five root causes**:

| # | Root Cause | One-Line Meaning | KTU 2024 Module Mapping |
|---|-----------|------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Ambiguity** | One expression, many meanings | Lexical / Syntactic / Semantic / Pragmatic |
| 2 | **Context Dependency** | Meaning depends on surrounding text | Discourse / Pragmatics |
| 3 | **Common-Sense / World Knowledge** | Humans use unseen background knowledge | Semantic Web, Knowledge Graphs |
| 4 | **Creativity & Figurative Language** | Idioms, metaphors, sarcasm, humour | Pragmatics |
| 5 | **Language Variation & Evolution** | New words, dialects, code-mixing | Sociolinguistics, Low-resource NLP |

### 1.4 Why This Topic is Important for KTU Exams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PERAT742, Module 1):** The first module typically opens with **2–4 mark short-answer questions** asking students to *list and explain the challenges of NLP*, or *give examples of ambiguity*. It also seeds **14-mark analytical questions** where students must *discuss ambiguity at multiple linguistic levels* with examples. Mastering this topic is therefore **non-negotiable** for scoring well in **Part A and Part B of the End Semester Examination (ESE)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Taxonomy of NLP Challenges

### 2.1.1 Ambiguity — "The King of NLP Challenges"

Ambiguity occurs when a single linguistic expression can be interpreted in **more than one way**. It is the single most cited reason why NLP is hard. The **five levels of ambiguity** are:

#### (a) Lexical Ambiguity (Word-Level)
A **single word** has multiple senses.

> **Example (KTU-favourite):**
> - *"I went to the **bank**."* → River bank *or* financial bank?
> - *"He **saw** the saw."* → Verb *or* noun?

**KTU-relevant sub-types:**
- **Homonymy** — same spelling/sound, unrelated meaning (*bat* = animal / sports equipment)
- **Polysemy** — same word, related meanings (*head* of department / *head* of a beer)
- **Homophony** — same sound, different spelling (*their / there / they're*)

#### (b) Syntactic Ambiguity (Structure-Level)
A **sentence** can be parsed in multiple grammatical ways.

> **Classic KTU Example:**
> *"I saw the man **with the telescope**."*
> - Interpretation 1: I used a telescope to see the man. (PP attaches to VP)
> - Interpretation 2: I saw the man who had a telescope. (PP attaches to NP)

This is called **PP-attachment ambiguity** and is a board-exam favourite.

#### (c) Semantic Ambiguity (Meaning-Level)
A **grammatically correct sentence** still has multiple meanings.

> **Example:**
> *"The chicken is ready to eat."*
> - The chicken (animal) is ready to eat (its food).
> - The chicken (dish) is ready to be eaten (by us).

#### (d) Pragmatic Ambiguity (Intent-Level)
Meaning depends on **speaker intent, situation, and world knowledge**.

> **Example:**
> *"Can you pass the salt?"*
> - Literal: A yes/no question about physical ability.
> - Intended: A polite request.

#### (e) Discourse / Anaphoric Ambiguity
Pronouns or references whose antecedents are unclear.

> **Example:**
> *"John told Mike that **he** was right."*
> - Who is "he"? John or Mike?

---

### 2.1.2 Context Dependency

Words and sentences change meaning based on **previous and following text**. The same word may have **opposite meanings** in different contexts.

> **Example:**
> *"This phone is **cool**."* (positive)
> *"The exam was **cool**."* (Kerala engineering students would read this as negative!)

Mathematically, the meaning of a token $w_i$ in a sequence is a function of its context window:

$$P(\text{meaning}(w_i)) = f(w_{i-k}, \dots, w_{i-1}, w_{i+1}, \dots, w_{i+k})$$

where $k$ is the **context window size**.

---

### 2.1.3 Common-Sense & World Knowledge

Humans rely on **massive implicit knowledge** that is never stated. NLP systems must either be *hand-coded* with such knowledge (expensive) or *learned* from data (noisy).

> **Example:**
> *"The trophy did not fit in the suitcase because **it** was too big."*
> - Most humans know "it" = trophy (because suitcases don't usually get "big").
> - A naive NLP model picks the noun closest to the pronoun — *suitcase* — and gets it wrong.

---

### 2.1.4 Creativity, Figurative Language & Sarcasm

- **Idioms:** *"It's raining cats and dogs"* (literal interpretation = nonsense).
- **Metaphors:** *"Time is money."*
- **Sarcasm:** *"Oh great, another exam!"* (positive words, negative intent).
- **Humor & Wordplay:** Pun detection is an open research problem.

---

### 2.1.5 Language Variation, Evolution & Code-Mixing

- **New words:** *selfie, vibe, cringe, shipar* (Malayalam slang).
- **Domain shift:** Medical English ≠ Twitter English.
- **Code-mixing (very relevant to Kerala!):** *"Adikal class **start aayo**"*.
- **Dialect variation:** British / American / Indian English.
- **Spelling errors and typos:** Common in chat data.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **mathematical / quantitative metrics** that examiners love to ask as sub-parts in 14-mark questions. Memorize these — they are worth easy marks.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Meaning of Symbols | KTU Use-Case |
|---------|----------------------|--------------------|--------------|
| **Joint Probability of a Sentence** | $P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1, \dots, w_{i-1})$ | Probability of word $w_i$ given all previous words | Foundation of language models (Module 2/3) |
| **Markov (n-gram) Assumption** | $P(w_i \mid w_1, \dots, w_{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$ | Probability depends only on the previous 1 word (bigram) | Reduces computational complexity |
| **Bigram Probability** | $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \dfrac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}$ | $C(\cdot)$ = count in corpus | Directly tested in KTU problems |
| **Bayes' Rule (for Disambiguation)** | $P(c \mid d) = \dfrac{P(d \mid c) \cdot P(c)}{P(d)}$ | $c$ = class (sense), $d$ = data (context) | Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD) |
| **Naive Bayes for Text** | $P(c \mid d) \propto P(c) \prod_{i} P(w_i \mid c)$ | Multiply likelihoods of features (words) | Sentiment / classification |
| **Perplexity** | $PP(W) = \left( P(w_1, \dots, w_n) \right)^{-1/n}$ | Lower = better language model | Evaluating LMs |
| **Entropy (in bits)** | $H(X) = -\sum_{x \in X} p(x) \log_2 p(x)$ | Average information content | Quantifies uncertainty in language |
| **Zipf's Law** | $f(r) \propto \dfrac{1}{r^{\alpha}}$ | $r$ = rank, $\alpha \approx 1$ | Explains skewed word distributions |
| **Type-Token Ratio (TTR)** | $TTR = \dfrac{\vert V \vert}{\vert N \vert}$ | $\vert V \vert$ = vocabulary size, $\vert N \vert$ = total tokens | Lexical richness measure |
| **Polysemy Index** | $\text{Poly}(w) = \dfrac{\text{number of senses of } w}{1}$ | Higher = more ambiguous word | Quantifies lexical ambiguity |

> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note for Students:** When writing absolute value in tables, always use $\vert V \vert$, not $\mid V \mid$ — and never use the bare pipe `|` character inside a markdown table cell, as it breaks KTU exam-answer PDFs!

---

## 2.3 Why Are These Challenges Important in Real Engineering?

| NLP Challenge | Real-World Application Affected |
|----------------|-------------------------------|
| Lexical Ambiguity | Search engines, Machine Translation |
| Syntactic Ambiguity | Parsers, Question Answering |
| Semantic Ambiguity | Chatbots, Virtual Assistants |
| Pragmatic Ambiguity | Sentiment Analysis, Sarcasm Detection |
| Context Dependency | Conversational AI, Summarization |
| World Knowledge | Question Answering (e.g., Watson, ChatGPT) |
| Code-Mixing | Multilingual Chatbots (Kerala use-case!) |
| Low-Resource Languages | Indian language NLP (Malayalam, Hindi) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Real-World Linkage:** KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. When answering "discuss NLP challenges" in exams, always **tie the challenge to a real engineering application**. This satisfies the **Course Outcome (CO1)** of PERAT742 and earns full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> **Note for Students:** Although "Challenges of NLP" is largely conceptual, the **derivation of quantitative metrics** like Perplexity, Entropy, and Bigram probability frequently appear as 7-mark sub-parts in KTU 14-mark questions. This section walks through them step by step.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Joint Probability to Bigram Model

**Problem:** Given a sentence $S = w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n$, derive a tractable estimate of $P(S)$ using the **first-order Markov assumption**.

### Step 1: Chain Rule of Probability (Exact)
By the chain rule of probability, the joint probability of a sequence can always be factorized as:

$$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) \cdots P(w_n \mid w_1, \dots, w_{n-1})$$

This is **mathematically exact** but **computationally intractable** for long sentences, because the conditioning context grows linearly with $n$.

### Step 2: Apply the First-Order Markov Assumption
We assume that the probability of the next word depends **only on the immediately preceding word**:

$$P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$$

This is the **bigram assumption** (also called the *first-order Markov assumption*).

### Step 3: Substitute Back
Plugging the assumption into the chain rule:

$$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) \approx P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_2) \cdots P(w_n \mid w_{n-1})$$

### Step 4: Compact Notation
The product is written concisely as:

$$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$$

with the convention that $w_0 = \langle \text{s} \rangle$ (sentence-start token).

### Step 5: Estimate Each Bigram by Maximum Likelihood (MLE)
Using corpus counts $C(\cdot)$:

$$P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}$$

### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example
> **Corpus (toy):**
> - "the cat sat"
> - "the cat slept"
> - "the dog sat"
>
> **Compute** $P(\text{"the cat sat"})$:
> - $P(\text{the} \mid \langle s \rangle) = 2/2 = 1$ (both sentences start with "the")
> - $P(\text{cat} \mid \text{the}) = 2/2 = 1$ (both "the" are followed by "cat")
> - $P(\text{sat} \mid \text{cat}) = 1/2 = 0.5$ (of two "cat", one is followed by "sat")
> - $P(\text{the cat sat}) = 1 \times 1 \times 0.5 = 0.5$

This derivation is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in any KTU NLP exam.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Perplexity as "Branching Factor"

**Problem:** Show that perplexity of a uniform distribution over $V$ words equals $V$ (the vocabulary size).

### Step 1: Definition of Perplexity
For a sentence of length $N$:

$$PP(W) = \left( P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N) \right)^{-1/N}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Bigram Assumption

$$PP(W) = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) \right)^{-1/N}$$

### Step 3: Assume Uniform Distribution
Let the vocabulary size be $V$, and let $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = 1/V$ for every $i$ (worst-case model):

$$PP(W) = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{V} \right)^{-1/N} = \left( \left(\frac{1}{V}\right)^N \right)^{-1/N}$$

### Step 4: Simplify the Exponent

$$PP(W) = \left( \frac{1}{V^N} \right)^{-1/N} = V^{N \cdot (1/N)} = V^1 = V$$

### Step 5: Interpretation
> **A uniform model's perplexity equals the vocabulary size.** A *good* model should have perplexity *much less* than $V$. For example, a good English bigram model on the Brown corpus has $PP \approx 247$ when $V \approx 50{,}000$.

**Engineering Intuition:** Perplexity is the *effective branching factor* — "how many choices is the model confused between at each step?"

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Entropy of a Language

**Problem:** Quantify the **inherent uncertainty** of English at the character level using Shannon's entropy.

### Step 1: Shannon Entropy (per symbol)

$$H = -\sum_{i=1}^{V} p_i \log_2 p_i \;\; \text{bits/symbol}$$

### Step 2: Plug in Realistic English Letter Probabilities
Using the famous **Shannon (1951)** letter frequencies for English:

| Letter | $p_i$ | $-p_i \log_2 p_i$ |
|--------|------|-------------------|
| E | 0.127 | 0.3675 |
| T | 0.091 | 0.3147 |
| A | 0.082 | 0.2958 |
| O | 0.075 | 0.2803 |
| ... | ... | ... |

Summing over all 26 letters:

$$H_{\text{English letter}} \approx 4.14 \;\; \text{bits/letter}$$

### Step 3: Compare to Uniform (Worst Case)
If all 26 letters were equally likely:

$$H_{\text{uniform}} = \log_2 26 \approx 4.70 \;\; \text{bits/letter}$$

### Step 4: Compare to Truly Random
True English has **less** entropy than uniform letters, because of redundancy. This quantifies **how predictable English is** — a foundational motivation for statistical NLP.

> **Interpretation for KTU exams:** The fact that $H_{\text{English}} < H_{\text{uniform}}$ is *direct mathematical evidence* that **language has structure** — and therefore NLP is tractable!

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Levels of Ambiguity at a Glance

| Level | Linguistic Unit | Source of Ambiguity | Canonical KTU Example | Resolution Method |
|-------|----------------|---------------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| **Lexical** | Word | Multiple word senses | *"bank"* (river / finance) | WSD, WordNet, BERT |
| **Syntactic** | Phrase / Clause | Multiple parse trees | *"saw the man with telescope"* | PCFG, Dependency parsing |
| **Semantic** | Sentence | Multiple meanings | *"chicken ready to eat"* | Selectional restrictions |
| **Pragmatic** | Utterance | Speaker intent | *"Can you open the window?"* | Speech acts, context |
| **Discourse** | Multi-sentence | Pronoun / reference | *"John told Mike **he** won"* | Coreference resolution |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked *"Discuss ambiguity in NLP"*, **always present this matrix as a table** in your answer. It is examiner-friendly, visually scannable, and guarantees full marks for structure.

---

## 3.5 Derivations Summary Table

| Derivation | Starting Point | Key Assumption | Final Form | KTU Weightage |
|------------|---------------|----------------|------------|---------------|
| Bigram Model | Chain rule | First-order Markov | $P(S) \approx \prod_i P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})$ | 7 marks |
| Perplexity | Bigram model | Uniform $P$ | $PP = V$ (worst case) | 5–7 marks |
| Entropy | Shannon | Letter frequencies | $H \approx 4.14$ bits | 3–5 marks |
| Naive Bayes | Bayes' rule | Conditional independence of features | $P(c \mid d) \propto P(c) \prod P(w_i \mid c)$ | 7 marks |

---
