where $\vert V \vert$ is the vocabulary size and the $+1$ and $+\vert V \vert$ terms are the Laplace smoothing constants.

### 3.1.6 Worked Numerical Example

Suppose we have a tiny training corpus with **2 classes** (Spam = S, Ham = H), **vocabulary size** $\vert V \vert = 6$:
$V = \{\text{free}, \text{money}, \text{meeting}, \text{project}, \text{offer}, \text{schedule}\}$

**Spam documents (3 total):**
- D1: "free money offer"
- D2: "free offer money"
- D3: "money offer free"

**Ham documents (3 total):**
- D4: "meeting project schedule"
- D5: "project meeting schedule"
- D6: "schedule project meeting"

**Step A — Compute priors:**

$$P(S) = \dfrac{3}{6} = 0.5 \quad \text{and} \quad P(H) = \dfrac{3}{6} = 0.5$$

**Step B — Count words per class:**

For Spam: free = 3, money = 3, offer = 3, others = 0. Total spam words = 9.

For Ham: meeting = 3, project = 3, schedule = 3, others = 0. Total ham words = 9.

**Step C — Compute Laplace-smoothed likelihoods for Spam:**

$$P(\text{free} \mid S) = \dfrac{3 + 1}{9 + 6} = \dfrac{4}{15} \approx 0.267$$

$$P(\text{money} \mid S) = \dfrac{3 + 1}{15} = \dfrac{4}{15} \approx 0.267$$

$$P(\text{offer} \mid S) = \dfrac{3 + 1}{15} = \dfrac{4}{15} \approx 0.267$$

$$P(\text{meeting} \mid S) = \dfrac{0 + 1}{15} = \dfrac{1}{15} \approx 0.067$$

(Symmetric results hold for Ham class.)

**Step D — Classify new document:** "free money meeting"

$$\log P(S) + \log P(\text{free} \mid S) + \log P(\text{money} \mid S) + \log P(\text{meeting} \mid S)$$

$$= \log(0.5) + \log(0.267) + \log(0.267) + \log(0.067)$$

$$\approx -0.693 + (-1.322) + (-1.322) + (-2.708) = -6.045$$

$$\log P(H) + \log P(\text{free} \mid H) + \log P(\text{money} \mid H) + \log P(\text{meeting} \mid H)$$

$$\approx -0.693 + (-2.708) + (-2.708) + (-1.322) = -7.431$$

**Decision:** Since $-6.045 > -7.431$, the document is classified as **SPAM** ✓

This example appears frequently in KTU textbooks and exam papers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: TF-IDF from Information-Theoretic Principles

### 3.2.1 Motivation
Raw term frequency is biased toward long documents and high-frequency words like "the". We need a **statistical measure** that highlights words both **frequent in a document** and **rare across the corpus**.

### 3.2.2 Term Frequency (TF)
Normalized count of term $t$ in document $d$:

$$\mathrm{TF}(t, d) = \dfrac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$$

where $f_{t,d}$ is the raw count of term $t$ in document $d$.

### 3.2.3 Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)
We want $\mathrm{IDF}(t)$ to be **high** when term $t$ appears in **few** documents (informative) and **low** when it appears in **many** (common). The total corpus contains $N$ documents, and the document frequency is $\mathrm{df}(t) = \vert \{ d \in \mathcal{C} : t \in d \} \vert$.

We define:

$$\mathrm{IDF}(t, \mathcal{C}) = \log \dfrac{N}{\mathrm{df}(t)}$$

### 3.2.4 Worked Numerical Example

Suppose corpus has $N = 1000$ documents. The word "epidemic" appears in 10 documents.

$$\mathrm{IDF}(\text{epidemic}) = \log \dfrac{1000}{10} = \log(100) = 2$$

The word "the" appears in 950 documents:

$$\mathrm{IDF}(\text{the}) = \log \dfrac{1000}{950} \approx 0.022$$

This confirms: **rare words get high IDF, common words get near-zero IDF**.

### 3.2.5 TF-IDF Combination

$$\mathrm{TF\text{-}IDF}(t, d) = \mathrm{TF}(t, d) \cdot \mathrm{IDF}(t, \mathcal{C})$$

The product amplifies terms that are **locally frequent** and **globally rare** — exactly the signature of **discriminative keywords**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pipeline Stage — Tradeoffs

| Pipeline Stage | Classical Choice | Modern Choice | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Representation** | TF-IDF + SVM | BERT / RoBERTa | Speed vs. Accuracy |
| **Preprocessing** | Heavy (stemming + stopwords) | Light (tokenization only) | Recall vs. Context |
| **Model** | Naive Bayes | Transformer | Interpretability vs. Performance |
| **Data Volume** | $< 10{,}000$ samples | $> 100{,}000$ samples | Bias vs. Compute Cost |
| **Inference Latency** | Linear models ($< 5$ ms) | Transformers ($> 50$ ms) | Edge deployment vs. Cloud only |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Text Classification Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Raw Text Corpus] --> stepB[Stage 1: Data Collection and Labeling]
    stepB --> stepC[Stage 2: Text Preprocessing]
    stepC --> subC1[Lowercasing]
    subC1 --> subC2[Tokenization]
    subC2 --> subC3[Stopword Removal]
    subC3 --> subC4[Stemming and Lemmatization]
    subC4 --> stepD[Stage 3: Feature Engineering]
    stepD --> featA[Bag of Words]
    stepD --> featB[TF-IDF]
    stepD --> featC[Word Embeddings]
    stepD --> featD[BERT Contextual Vectors]
    featA --> stepE[Stage 4: Train Validation Test Split]
    featB --> stepE
    featC --> stepE
    featD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF[Stage 5: Model Selection]
    stepF --> modelA[Naive Bayes]
    stepF --> modelB[SVM]
    stepF --> modelC[Logistic Regression]
    stepF --> modelD[LSTM CNN]
    stepF --> modelE[Fine Tuned Transformer]
    modelA --> stepG[Stage 6: Training and Tuning]
    modelB --> stepG
    modelC --> stepG
    modelD --> stepG
    modelE --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH[Stage 7: Evaluation]
    stepH --> metricA[Accuracy Precision Recall F1]
    stepH --> metricB[Confusion Matrix]
    stepH --> metricC[ROC AUC Curve]
    metricA --> stepI[Deployment REST API or Batch]
    metricB --> stepI
    metricC --> stepI
    stepI --> feedback[Feedback Loop and Retraining]
    feedback --> stepB
```

## 4.2 Architecture Diagram: Supervised Learning Loop for Text Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawDoc[Unlabeled Text Document] --> preprocess[Preprocessing Module]
    preprocess --> featureExt[Feature Extractor]
    featureExt --> vecFeat[Numerical Feature Vector]
    vecFeat --> trainedModel[Trained Classifier h of x]
    trainedModel --> predictedLabel[Predicted Class Label]
    trainCorpus[Labeled Training Corpus] --> trainEngine[Training Engine]
    trainEngine --> trainedModel
    predictedLabel --> evalMetric[Evaluation Module]
    trueLabel[Ground Truth Label] --> evalMetric
    evalMetric --> feedbackReport[Performance Report]
    feedbackReport --> retrain[Trigger Retraining if Below Threshold]
    retrain --> trainEngine
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Block Diagram: Application Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    appLayer[Text Classification Application Layer]
    appLayer --> a1[Sentiment Analysis]
    appLayer --> a2[Spam Detection]
    appLayer --> a3[Topic Categorization]
    appLayer --> a4[Intent Detection]
    appLayer --> a5[Toxicity Filtering]
    appLayer --> a6[Language Identification]
    a1 --> shared[Shared Classification Pipeline]
    a2 --> shared
    a3 --> shared
    a4 --> shared
    a5 --> shared
    a6 --> shared
    shared --> core1[Tokenization Layer]
    shared --> core2[Embedding Layer]
    shared --> core3[Classifier Head]
    core3 --> output[Class Probability Distribution]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "draw the block diagram of a text classification system", use a **left-to-right** flowchart with **clearly labeled preprocessing $\rightarrow$ feature extraction $\rightarrow$ model $\rightarrow$ output** boxes. This is the **highest-scoring visual answer** pattern validated by KTU valuation keys.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define text classification. List any four real-world applications.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Text classification is the task of automatically assigning predefined category labels to free-text documents, sentences, or tokens based on their semantic content. It is a supervised learning problem where a model is trained on labeled examples to predict the class of unseen text.

**Applications (1 mark — any 4):**
1. Sentiment Analysis
2. Spam Email Detection
3. News Topic Categorization
4. Chatbot Intent Detection
5. Hate Speech / Toxicity Detection
6. Language Identification

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between multi-class and multi-label text classification with one example each.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Multi-Class | Multi-Label |
|---|---|---|
| **Label Set** | Mutually exclusive — exactly one class | Non-exclusive — multiple classes allowed |
| **Output** | Single label per document | Set of labels per document |
| **Example** | News topic (Sports / Politics / Tech) | Movie genre (Action AND Comedy AND Romance) |
| **Loss Function** | Categorical cross-entropy | Binary cross-entropy per label (sigmoid output) |

**Example of Multi-Class:** A news article belongs to exactly one of Sports, Politics, Entertainment.

**Example of Multi-Label:** A movie is tagged with both "Action" and "Thriller" simultaneously.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the major stages of a text classification pipeline with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the Naive Bayes classification algorithm. Derive its decision rule and explain how Laplace smoothing addresses the zero-probability problem. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

The text classification pipeline consists of **seven stages**:

1. **Data Collection and Labeling:** Gather domain-specific textual corpora annotated with class labels by human annotators or weak supervision.

2. **Text Preprocessing:** Apply normalization steps — lowercasing, tokenization, stopword removal, stemming or lemmatization, and noise removal.

3. **Feature Engineering:** Convert text into numerical vectors using BoW, TF-IDF, or neural embeddings (Word2Vec, BERT).

4. **Train / Validation / Test Split:** Partition data (typically 70/15/15) to enable unbiased training and evaluation.

5. **Model Selection:** Choose a classifier — Naive Bayes, SVM, Logistic Regression, CNN, LSTM, or Transformer.

6. **Training and Hyperparameter Tuning:** Fit the model on training data and tune hyperparameters on validation data using grid search or Bayesian optimization.

7. **Evaluation and Deployment:** Compute metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1) on the test set, then deploy via REST API or batch pipeline.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Raw Text] → [Preprocessing] → [Feature Extraction] → [Model Training] → [Evaluation] → [Deployment]
```

*(For full marks, draw a clean flowchart with arrows; the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 serves as the gold standard.)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Naive Bayes Algorithm (3 marks):**

Naive Bayes is a probabilistic classifier based on Bayes' theorem and the naive conditional independence assumption between features. Given a document represented as a feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$, it computes the posterior probability of each class and assigns the document to the class with the highest probability.

**Decision Rule Derivation (3 marks):**

Starting from Bayes' theorem:

$$P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) P(c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

Applying the naive independence assumption:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$$

Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ is constant, the decision rule is:

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$$

**Laplace Smoothing (1 mark):**

Without smoothing, any word not seen in training data gets $P(w \mid c) = 0$, causing the entire product to collapse to zero. Laplace smoothing adds 1 to each count:

$$P(w \mid c) = \dfrac{\mathrm{count}(w, c) + 1}{\sum_{w'} \mathrm{count}(w', c) + \vert V \vert}$$

This guarantees non-zero probabilities and dramatically improves generalization to unseen vocabulary.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various applications of text classification in modern NLP systems. Provide at least six real-world examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the following evaluation metrics for text classification with formulas: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Macro-F1. State one limitation of accuracy as a metric. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Introduction (1 mark):** Text classification powers nearly every intelligent text-processing system deployed today, ranging from search engines to conversational AI.

**Applications (6 marks — elaborate any 6):**

1. **Sentiment Analysis:** Brands like Amazon and Flipkart analyze millions of product reviews daily to extract customer sentiment (positive / negative / neutral), enabling product recommendation and quality monitoring.

2. **Spam Detection:** Gmail and Outlook use text classifiers trained on millions of emails to filter spam with >99.9% accuracy. Features include word n-grams, sender reputation, and URL blacklists.

3. **News Topic Categorization:** News agencies like Reuters and BBC use multi-class classifiers to auto-route articles to Politics, Sports, Tech, Entertainment, and Business desks.

4. **Intent Classification in Chatbots:** Virtual assistants (Alexa, Google Assistant, Swiggy's chat support) classify user utterances into intents like "order_food", "track_order", "cancel_order" before routing to the appropriate handler.

5. **Hate Speech and Toxicity Detection:** Social media platforms (Twitter/X, YouTube, Instagram) deploy classifiers to flag toxic comments in real time, supporting safer online communities.

6. **Language Identification:** Google's CLD3 and Facebook's fastText identify the language of a given text snippet before routing it to language-specific NLP pipelines (translation, transliteration).

7. **Authorship Attribution:** Used in forensics and academic plagiarism detection to identify the author of a disputed document based on stylistic features.

8. **Medical Coding:** Hospitals classify clinical notes into ICD-10 codes automatically, reducing manual coding effort by up to 70%.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Accuracy (2 marks):** The fraction of correctly classified instances among the total:

$$\mathrm{Accuracy} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

**Precision (1.5 marks):** Of all instances predicted as positive, how many are truly positive:

$$\mathrm{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

**Recall (1.5 marks):** Of all actual positive instances, how many were correctly retrieved:

$$\mathrm{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**F1-Score (1 mark):** Harmonic mean of precision and recall, providing a single balanced metric:

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\mathrm{Precision} \cdot \mathrm{Recall}}{\mathrm{Precision} + \mathrm{Recall}}$$

**Macro-F1 (0.5 marks):** Unweighted mean of per-class F1 scores:

$$F_1^{\mathrm{macro}} = \dfrac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} F_1^{(i)}$$

**Limitation of Accuracy (0.5 marks):** Accuracy is **misleading on imbalanced datasets**. For instance, if 99% of emails are ham, a trivial classifier that always predicts "ham" achieves 99% accuracy but is completely useless — F1-score on the minority class exposes this failure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Multi-Class with Multi-Label:** This is the **#1 mistake** in 7-mark questions. Always explicitly state that multi-class uses **softmax + categorical cross-entropy**, while multi-label uses **sigmoid + binary cross-entropy**.
> 2. **Forgetting Laplace Smoothing:** Naive Bayes derivations without the $+\alpha$ smoothing term lose 1-2 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring Class Imbalance:** When asked about evaluation metrics, do not just say "we use accuracy". Always mention **F1-score is preferred for imbalanced classes**.
> 4. **Missing Block Diagram:** A question on pipeline without a diagram typically caps at 5/7 marks. Always include a labeled flowchart.
> 5. **Writing Formulas Without Definitions of TP/TN/FP/FN:** Every confusion-matrix formula must be preceded by the definitions of $TP$, $TN$, $FP$, $FN$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read Before Every KTU Exam:**

- ✅ Text classification = mapping $\mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ where labels are from a finite, predefined set.
- ✅ Three types: **Binary**, **Multi-Class** (single label), **Multi-Label** (multiple labels).
- ✅ Pipeline has **7 stages**: Data Collection → Preprocessing → Feature Engineering → Splitting → Model Selection → Training → Evaluation/Deployment.
- ✅ Preprocessing includes lowercasing, tokenization, stopword removal, stemming, lemmatization.
- ✅ Feature methods: **BoW** (counts), **TF-IDF** (weighted counts), **Word2Vec/GloVe** (static embeddings), **BERT** (contextual embeddings).
- ✅ Naive Bayes assumes feature independence: $P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = \prod_i P(x_i \mid c)$.
- ✅ Laplace smoothing formula: $P(w \mid c) = \dfrac{\mathrm{count}(w, c) + 1}{\sum_{w'} \mathrm{count}(w', c) + \vert V \vert}$.
- ✅ Decision rule: $\hat{c} = \arg\max_c \, P(c) \prod_i P(x_i \mid c)$.
- ✅ **TF-IDF** = Term Frequency × Inverse Document Frequency; IDF uses $\log$ to dampen effect.
- ✅ Evaluation metrics: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 — F1 is preferred for imbalanced data.
- ✅ **Macro-F1** averages per-class F1 equally; **Micro-F1** equals accuracy on multi-class.
- ✅ Cross-Entropy loss: $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_c y_c \log(\hat{p}_c)$ is the standard neural classifier loss.
- ✅ Top applications: Sentiment, Spam, Topic, Intent, Toxicity, Language ID, Authorship, Medical Coding.
- ✅ **Naive Bayes** is the canonical baseline; **Transformers (BERT)** are the SOTA ceiling.
- ✅ Always include a **block diagram** when a pipeline question is asked — it is worth 1-2 marks.
- ✅ Always **define TP/TN/FP/FN** before using any confusion-matrix metric.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Naïve Bayes for Sentiment Classification - Naïve Bayes Classifier Training - Optimizing for Sentiment Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT742 - NATURAL LANGAUGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Naïve Bayes for Sentiment Classification - Naïve Bayes Classifier Training - Optimizing for Sentiment Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Naïve Bayes Classifier (for Sentiment Classification)**: A probabilistic supervised machine learning model that applies **Bayes' Theorem** with the **conditional independence assumption** (the "naïve" assumption) to predict the sentiment class $c \in \{positive, negative, neutral\}$ of a text document $d$ by selecting the class that maximizes the posterior probability $P(c \mid d)$.

For a document represented as a **bag-of-words** feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ where $x_i$ denotes the presence, count, or weight of the $i$-th vocabulary term, the classifier implements the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** decision rule:

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c \in \mathcal{C}} P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: In the PERAT742 syllabus, Naïve Bayes is positioned as a **baseline generative classifier** for text/sentiment tasks. You are expected to know (a) the conditional independence assumption, (b) parameter estimation from a labeled corpus, (c) smoothing, and (d) at least two variants — **Multinomial NB** and **Bernoulli NB**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **judge in a courtroom** deciding whether a movie review is *positive* or *negative*.

- Before the trial, the judge has a **prior belief** about how common positive reviews are on the platform — this is $P(c)$.
- During the trial, each piece of evidence (a word like *"amazing"*, *"boring"*, *"loved"*) is presented **one at a time**.
- Crucially, the judge makes a *naïve* simplification: she **assumes each piece of evidence is independent of the others** (in reality, "not good" and "bad" are correlated — but she ignores that to keep the math tractable).
- She multiplies the prior by the strength of each piece of evidence, then picks the verdict (class) with the highest combined score.

This is exactly what Naïve Bayes does — it ignores word-order and word-correlation dependencies and simply multiplies probabilities. Despite this "naïve" simplification, the classifier performs **remarkably well on text classification**, often rivaling more complex models.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Vocabulary)

| Term | Notation | Meaning in Sentiment NB |
|------|----------|-------------------------|
| Prior probability | $P(c)$ | Fraction of training documents belonging to class $c$ |
| Likelihood | $P(x_i \mid c)$ | Probability of observing word $x_i$ given class $c$ |
| Posterior | $P(c \mid \mathbf{x})$ | Probability of class $c$ after seeing the document |
| Evidence | $P(\mathbf{x})$ | Normalizing constant (ignored during argmax) |
| Vocabulary | $V$ | Set of unique tokens across the entire training corpus |
| Class set | $\mathcal{C}$ | $\{positive, negative\}$ (binary) or $\{positive, neutral, negative\}$ |
| Laplace smoothing | $\alpha$ | Add-$\alpha$ correction to prevent zero probabilities |
| **Laplace constant** | $\alpha = 1$ | Standard value used to avoid zero-frequency issues |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline (Step-by-Step)

The Naïve Bayes sentiment classifier operates in **two phases** — *training* and *inference*. The full training pipeline is:

1. **Tokenization & Normalization** — split each document into lowercase tokens, remove punctuation, and apply stemming/lemmatization.
2. **Vocabulary Construction** — build $V = \{w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{\vert V \vert}\}$ from the training corpus.
3. **Class Prior Estimation** — for each class $c$, compute $P(c) = \frac{N_c}{N}$, where $N_c$ is the number of training documents in class $c$ and $N$ is the total number of training documents.
4. **Conditional Probability Estimation** — for each word $w_i \in V$ and each class $c$, estimate $P(w_i \mid c)$ via the chosen variant (Multinomial or Bernoulli).
5. **Smoothing** — apply **Laplace (add-$\alpha$) smoothing** so that unseen words in the test set do not collapse the posterior to zero.
6. **Inference (at test time)** — for a new document $d$, compute the log-posterior for each class and return the argmax.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why a prior?** Many platforms have a class-imbalance (e.g., 80% positive reviews on Amazon). The prior encodes this base rate.
- **Why conditional independence?** It makes the joint probability $P(x_1, \dots, x_n \mid c)$ factorize into a product of $n$ one-dimensional terms — drastically reducing the number of parameters from $2^n - 1$ to $n \cdot \vert \mathcal{C} \vert$.
- **Why smoothing?** A word that never appeared with a class in training (e.g., "boring" in the positive class) would assign $P = 0$, zeroing out the entire posterior.
- **Why log-space?** Multiplying many small probabilities causes **floating-point underflow**. Converting products to sums of logarithms preserves the argmax and stabilizes computation.

## 2.3 Two KTU-Relevant Variants

> [!NOTE]
> **Multinomial Naïve Bayes (MNB)** — models word **counts**; best for longer documents.
> **Bernoulli Naïve Bayes (BNB)** — models word **presence/absence** (binary); best for short texts.

The two differ only in how they estimate $P(x_i \mid c)$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **Bayes' Theorem** | $P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(c) P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ | Posterior $\propto$ Prior $\times$ Likelihood |
| 2 | **Naïve Bayes Decision Rule** | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_c P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$ | MAP estimate |
| 3 | **Log-Space Rule** | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_c \left[ \log P(c) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid c) \right]$ | Numerically stable |
| 4 | **Class Prior** | $P(c) = \frac{N_c}{N}$ | Unitless probability |
| 5 | **MNB Likelihood** | $P(w_i \mid c) = \frac{count(w_i, c)}{\sum_{w \in V} count(w, c)}$ | Normalized word count |
| 6 | **BNB Likelihood** | $P(w_i \mid c) = \frac{\text{docs containing } w_i \text{ in } c + \alpha}{N_c + 2\alpha}$ | Binary indicator |
| 7 | **Laplace Smoothing (MNB)** | $P_{\alpha}(w_i \mid c) = \frac{count(w_i, c) + \alpha}{\sum_{w \in V} count(w, c) + \alpha \vert V \vert}$ | $\alpha = 1$ standard |
| 8 | **Zero-Probability Avoidance** | Add $\alpha$ to **both** numerator and denominator | Critical for unseen words |
| 9 | **Accuracy Metric** | $Acc = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Standard |
| 10 | **F1 Score** | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$, where $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$, $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Used for imbalanced data |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When writing MNB formulas in the KTU exam, **always show the smoothing constant $\alpha$ explicitly** in both numerator and denominator. Examiners specifically look for this.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Naïve Bayes remains the **de-facto baseline** in industry for:
- **Spam detection** (Gmail, Outlook filters).
- **Product review classification** (e-commerce platforms).
- **Social media monitoring** (brand sentiment tracking).
- **News categorization** (topic tagging).
- **Real-time moderation** (toxic comment detection).

Its appeal is **$O(n)$ training, $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert \mathcal{C} \vert)$ inference, interpretability, and extremely low memory footprint** — making it ideal for edge devices and streaming pipelines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Naïve Bayes Decision Rule from Bayes' Theorem

We start with **Bayes' Theorem** for a document $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \dots, x_n)$:

$$P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) P(c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ is constant across classes, maximizing $P(c \mid \mathbf{x})$ is equivalent to maximizing the numerator:

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_c P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) P(c)$$

Now we apply the **chain rule of probability**:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = P(x_1 \mid c) \cdot P(x_2 \mid x_1, c) \cdot P(x_3 \mid x_1, x_2, c) \cdots P(x_n \mid x_1, \dots, x_{n-1}, c)$$

The **naïve conditional independence assumption** states that each feature $x_i$ is conditionally independent of all others given the class:

$$P(x_i \mid x_1, \dots, x_{i-1}, c) = P(x_i \mid c) \quad \forall i$$

Substituting this simplification into the chain rule gives the **factorized form**:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$$

Therefore, the **Naïve Bayes decision rule** is:

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_c \left[ P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c) \right]$$

Taking the logarithm (a monotonically increasing function, so argmax is preserved):

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_c \left[ \log P(c) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid c) \right]$$

This log-space form is what is **actually computed** in practice to avoid floating-point underflow.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example: Sentiment Classification

**Setup.** Consider the following tiny labeled training corpus:

| Doc ID | Text | Class |
|--------|------|-------|
| D1 | "love great product" | POSITIVE |
| D2 | "excellent amazing service" | POSITIVE |
| D3 | "bad terrible hate" | NEGATIVE |
| D4 | "worst horrible awful" | NEGATIVE |

**Test document:** *"great product"* — what is its predicted sentiment?

**Step 1 — Build the vocabulary.**

$$V = \{\text{love}, \text{great}, \text{product}, \text{excellent}, \text{amazing}, \text{service}, \text{bad}, \text{terrible}, \text{hate}, \text{worst}, \text{horrible}, \text{awful}\}$$

So $\vert V \vert = 12$.

**Step 2 — Estimate class priors.**

$$P(POS) = \frac{N_{POS}}{N} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

$$P(NEG) = \frac{N_{NEG}}{N} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Count words per class.**

In the POSITIVE class: {love: 1, great: 1, product: 1, excellent: 1, amazing: 1, service: 1} → total word count $= 6$.

In the NEGATIVE class: {bad: 1, terrible: 1, hate: 1, worst: 1, horrible: 1, awful: 1} → total word count $= 6$.

**Step 4 — Apply Laplace smoothing** with $\alpha = 1$.

For the word *"great"* given POSITIVE:

$$P(\text{great} \mid POS) = \frac{count(\text{great}, POS) + \alpha}{\sum_{w \in V} count(w, POS) + \alpha \vert V \vert} = \frac{1 + 1}{6 + 1 \times 12} = \frac{2}{18} = \frac{1}{9} \approx 0.1111$$

For the word *"product"* given POSITIVE (same count):

$$P(\text{product} \mid POS) = \frac{1 + 1}{6 + 12} = \frac{2}{18} = \frac{1}{9} \approx 0.1111$$

For the word *"great"* given NEGATIVE (the word "great" did not appear in NEG training docs):

$$P(\text{great} \mid NEG) = \frac{0 + 1}{6 + 1 \times 12} = \frac{1}{18} \approx 0.0556$$

For the word *"product"* given NEGATIVE (similarly absent):

$$P(\text{product} \mid NEG) = \frac{0 + 1}{6 + 12} = \frac{1}{18} \approx 0.0556$$

**Step 5 — Compute the unnormalized posteriors** for the test document *"great product"*:

$$\text{Score}(POS) = P(POS) \cdot P(\text{great} \mid POS) \cdot P(\text{product} \mid POS)$$

$$\text{Score}(POS) = 0.5 \times \frac{1}{9} \times \frac{1}{9} = \frac{0.5}{81} \approx 0.00617$$

$$\text{Score}(NEG) = P(NEG) \cdot P(\text{great} \mid NEG) \cdot P(\text{product} \mid NEG)$$

$$\text{Score}(NEG) = 0.5 \times \frac{1}{18} \times \frac{1}{18} = \frac{0.5}{324} \approx 0.00154$$

**Step 6 — Normalize to get true probabilities** (optional, but useful for inspection):

$$P(POS \mid \text{“great product”}) = \frac{0.00617}{0.00617 + 0.00154} = \frac{0.00617}{0.00771} \approx 0.800$$

$$P(NEG \mid \text{“great product”}) = \frac{0.00154}{0.00771} \approx 0.200$$

**Step 7 — Apply the decision rule.**

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_c \{0.800, 0.200\} = POSITIVE$$

**Equivalent log-space computation (more numerically stable):**

$$\log \text{Score}(POS) = \log 0.5 + \log(1/9) + \log(1/9) = -0.693 - 2.197 - 2.197 = -5.087$$

$$\log \text{Score}(NEG) = \log 0.5 + \log(1/18) + \log(1/18) = -0.693 - 2.890 - 2.890 = -6.473$$

Since $-5.087 > -6.473$, we again pick **POSITIVE**. Same decision, more stable arithmetic.

## 3.3 Optimization Strategies (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Optimization | Formula / Idea | When to Use |
|---|--------------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Laplace (Add-1) Smoothing** | $P_{\alpha}(w \mid c) = \frac{count(w,c) + \alpha}{\sum_w count(w,c) + \alpha \vert V \vert}$ | Always — baseline necessity |
| 2 | **Add-$\alpha$ with $0 < \alpha < 1$** | Lidstone smoothing; preserves more probability mass for frequent words | When vocabulary is very large |
| 3 | **Log-Space Computation** | $\log P(c) + \sum_i \log P(x_i \mid c)$ | Always — prevents underflow |
| 4 | **N-Gram Features** | Replace unigrams with bigrams/trigrams: $P(w_i, w_{i+1} \mid c)$ | Captures negation, phrase sentiment |
| 5 | **Negation Handling** | Prepend "NOT_" to tokens after negation cues (not, never, no) | Critical for sentiment |
| 6 | **TF-IDF Weighting** | Replace raw counts with $tf(w,d) \cdot idf(w)$ | Downweights common words |
| 7 | **Feature Selection (CHI² / MI)** | Keep top-$k$ words by $\chi^2$ or Mutual Information | Reduces noise, speeds up inference |
| 8 | **Complement NB** | $P(c) \propto P(w_i \mid \tilde{c}) \prod_i$, where $\tilde{c}$ is the *complement* class | Handles class imbalance |
| 9 | **Class-Balanced Priors** | $P(c) = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{C} \vert}$ (uniform) | When true base rate is unknown |
| 10 | **Ensemble with LR / SVM** | Vote / average posterior with Logistic Regression | Boosts accuracy by 1-3% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: The two optimizations that are **most frequently tested** are (1) Laplace smoothing and (2) log-space computation. Always derive them from first principles, don't just state them.

## 3.4 Why Naïve Bayes Works for Sentiment Despite the Naïveté

The conditional independence assumption is clearly violated in language — *"not good"* and *"bad"* are highly correlated. Yet Naïve Bayes works because:

- **Sentiment signals are local** — most sentiment words are individually informative ("love", "hate", "amazing").
- The **rank order** of $P(c \mid d)$ across classes is preserved even if absolute probabilities are wrong, and that rank order is all that the argmax needs.
- Errors in individual $P(x_i \mid c)$ estimates tend to **cancel out** when multiplied across many words.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Naïve Bayes Training & Inference Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Training Corpus]) --> tokenize[Tokenize and Lowercase]
    tokenize --> vocab[Build Vocabulary V]
    vocab --> priorEst[Estimate Class Priors P of c]
    vocab --> likEst[Estimate Likelihoods P of w given c]
    priorEst --> smooth[Apply Laplace Smoothing alpha equals 1]
    likEst --> smooth
    smooth --> model[(Trained NB Model)]
    testStart([New Test Document d]) --> testTok[Tokenize d]
    testTok --> score[Compute Log Posterior for each class c]
    model --> score
    score --> argmax{argmax over c}
    argmax --> pred([Predicted Sentiment Class])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Optimization Strategy Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawNB[Raw NB Classifier] --> opt1[Smoothing alpha]
    rawNB --> opt2[Log Space Arithmetic]
    rawNB --> opt3[N gram Features]
    rawNB --> opt4[Negation Tagging]
    rawNB --> opt5[TF IDF Weighting]
    rawNB --> opt6[Complement NB]
    opt1 --> improved((Higher Accuracy))
    opt2 --> improved
    opt3 --> improved
    opt4 --> improved
    opt5 --> improved
    opt6 --> improved
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT
        A1[Raw Text Review]
    end
    subgraph PREPROCESS
        B1[Tokenization]
        B2[Stop word Removal]
        B3[Stemming or Lemmatization]
        A1 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3
    end
    subgraph FEATURE
        C1[Vocabulary Lookup]
        C2[Count Vector or TF IDF]
        B3 --> C1 --> C2
    end
    subgraph CLASSIFIER
        D1[Prior P of c Table]
        D2[Likelihood P of w given c Table]
        C2 --> E1[Multiply and Sum Logs]
        D1 --> E1
        D2 --> E1
        E1 --> E2[Argmax Decision]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT
        F1[Sentiment Label and Confidence]
        E2 --> F1
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**State Bayes' Theorem. How is the "naïve" assumption used in the Naïve Bayes classifier, and why is it important for text classification?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** **Bayes' Theorem** states $P(c \mid x) = \frac{P(x \mid c) P(c)}{P(x)}$, where $P(c)$ is the prior, $P(x \mid c)$ is the likelihood, and $P(c \mid x)$ is the posterior.
- **(1 mark)** The **"naïve" assumption** of conditional independence states that, given the class $c$, every feature $x_i$ is independent of every other feature $x_j$, i.e., $P(x_1, \dots, x_n \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$.
- **(1 mark)** It is **important for text classification** because it reduces the number of parameters from exponential to linear in $n$, making the model tractable for large vocabularies; despite the assumption being violated, the classifier gives competitive accuracy on sentiment tasks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
**What is Laplace smoothing? Why is it essential when training a Naïve Bayes classifier for sentiment analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** **Laplace (add-1) smoothing** is a technique that adds a small constant $\alpha$ (typically 1) to every word count to ensure no probability is exactly zero.
- **(1 mark)** The smoothed MNB likelihood is $P_{\alpha}(w \mid c) = \frac{count(w, c) + \alpha}{\sum_w count(w, c) + \alpha \vert V \vert}$.
- **(1 mark)** It is **essential** because any unseen word in the test document (e.g., "fantastic" never appearing in NEGATIVE training reviews) would, without smoothing, set the entire class posterior to zero, making the classifier unable to recover.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **(a)** Derive the Naïve Bayes decision rule $\hat{c} = \arg\max_c P(c) \prod_i P(x_i \mid c)$ starting from Bayes' Theorem. Clearly state the conditional independence assumption and explain why log-space computation is preferred. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider the following training corpus for a binary sentiment task. Compute the smoothed Multinomial Naïve Bayes prediction (with $\alpha = 1$) for the test sentence *"great product"*. Show every step. **(7 marks)**
>
> Training data:
> - D1 (POS): "love great product"  
> - D2 (POS): "excellent amazing service"  
> - D3 (NEG): "bad terrible hate"  
> - D4 (NEG): "worst horrible awful"

#### **Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 marks**

- **(2 marks) Bayes' Theorem:** Write $P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(c) P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$. Note that $P(\mathbf{x})$ is a constant independent of $c$, so argmax over $c$ depends only on the numerator.
- **(2 marks) Chain rule + independence:** Expand $P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = P(x_1 \mid c) P(x_2 \mid x_1, c) \cdots P(x_n \mid x_1, \dots, x_{n-1}, c)$. Apply conditional independence $P(x_i \mid x_1, \dots, x_{i-1}, c) = P(x_i \mid c)$ to obtain $P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = \prod_i P(x_i \mid c)$.
- **(1 mark) Final decision rule:** $\hat{c} = \arg\max_c P(c) \prod_i P(x_i \mid c)$.
- **(2 marks) Log-space justification:** Direct multiplication of $n$ small probabilities causes floating-point underflow. Using $\log$ converts products to sums: $\hat{c} = \arg\max_c [\log P(c) + \sum_i \log P(x_i \mid c)]$. Since $\log$ is monotonic, the argmax is preserved.

#### **Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 marks**

- **(1 mark)** Vocabulary $V$ has $\vert V \vert = 12$: {love, great, product, excellent, amazing, service, bad, terrible, hate, worst, horrible, awful}.
- **(1 mark)** Priors: $P(POS) = P(NEG) = 0.5$.
- **(1 mark)** Word counts per class: $\sum_w count(w, POS) = 6$, $\sum_w count(w, NEG) = 6$.
- **(1 mark)** Smoothed likelihoods:
  - $P(\text{great} \mid POS) = P(\text{product} \mid POS) = \frac{1+1}{6+12} = \frac{2}{18} = \frac{1}{9}$.
  - $P(\text{great} \mid NEG) = P(\text{product} \mid NEG) = \frac{0+1}{6+12} = \frac{1}{18}$.
- **(1 mark)** Unnormalized scores:
  - $\text{Score}(POS) = 0.5 \times \frac{1}{9} \times \frac{1}{9} = \frac{0.5}{81} \approx 0.00617$.
  - $\text{Score}(NEG) = 0.5 \times \frac{1}{18} \times \frac{1}{18} = \frac{0.5}{324} \approx 0.00154$.
- **(1 mark)** Normalize to get $P(POS) \approx 0.80$, $P(NEG) \approx 0.20$.
- **(1 mark)** Decision: $\hat{c} = POS$ since $0.80 > 0.20$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
> **(a)** Compare **Multinomial Naïve Bayes (MNB)** and **Bernoulli Naïve Bayes (BNB)** for sentiment classification. State the likelihood formula for each and explain in which scenario each variant is preferred. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss **four optimization techniques** used to improve Naïve Bayes sentiment classifiers. For each, state the problem it solves and give the key equation or rule. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 marks**

| Aspect | Multinomial NB | Bernoulli NB |
|--------|---------------|--------------|
| **(2 marks) Document representation** | Word **counts** (multi-set) | Binary **presence/absence** vector |
| **(2 marks) Likelihood formula** | $P(w_i \mid c) = \frac{count(w_i, c) + \alpha}{\sum_w count(w, c) + \alpha \vert V \vert}$ | $P(w_i \mid c) = \frac{\text{docs containing } w_i \text{ in } c + \alpha}{N_c + 2\alpha}$ |
| **(1 mark) Strength** | Captures term importance through repetition | Robust to word counts; good for short texts |
| **(1 mark) Weakness** | Sensitive to document length | Throws away magnitude information |
| **(1 mark) Preferred when** | Long reviews (Amazon, Yelp) | Short tweets, short SMS, document-level binary sentiment |

#### **Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 marks**

- **(1.5 marks) Laplace (Add-1) Smoothing:** Solves zero-probability for unseen words. Equation: $P_{\alpha}(w \mid c) = \frac{count(w, c) + \alpha}{\sum_w count(w, c) + \alpha \vert V \vert}$.
- **(1.5 marks) Log-Space Computation:** Solves floating-point underflow. Equation: $\hat{c} = \arg\max_c [\log P(c) + \sum_i \log P(x_i \mid c)]$.
- **(2 marks) N-gram / Negation Tagging:** Solves the "not good ≠ good" problem. Replace unigrams with bigrams; or prepend "NOT_" to tokens following negation cues like *not, never, no*.
- **(2 marks) TF-IDF Weighting & Feature Selection:** Solves the common-word dominance problem. Replace raw counts with $tf \cdot idf$ or select top-$k$ features by $\chi^2$ / Mutual Information to reduce noise.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the denominator in Laplace smoothing.** Examiners check both numerator *and* denominator. Writing only $(count + \alpha)$ without $(\text{sum} + \alpha \vert V \vert)$ costs **2 full marks**.
> 2. **Using raw multiplication instead of log-space** in the worked numerical. The question says "compute" — floating-point products may underflow; the model answer expects log-additive form.
> 3. **Confusing MNB and BNB likelihoods** — they are different formulas. State which variant you are using before writing the equation.
> 4. **Forgetting to normalize** when the question says "compute the probability" — argmax doesn't need normalization, but a direct probability does.
> 5. **Skipping the prior $P(c)$** in the score — many students write only the likelihood product and miss 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bayes' Theorem** is the foundation: $P(c \mid x) = \frac{P(c) P(x \mid c)}{P(x)}$.
- The **naïve conditional independence assumption** factorizes the joint likelihood into a product: $P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = \prod_i P(x_i \mid c)$.
- The **decision rule** is $\hat{c} = \arg\max_c P(c) \prod_i P(x_i \mid c)$, evaluated in **log-space** to prevent underflow.
- **Class prior**: $P(c) = \frac{N_c}{N}$.
- **MNB likelihood (smoothed)**: $P_{\alpha}(w \mid c) = \frac{count(w, c) + \alpha}{\sum_w count(w, c) + \alpha \vert V \vert}$.
- **BNB likelihood (smoothed)**: $P_{\alpha}(w \mid c) = \frac{\text{docs with } w \text{ in } c + \alpha}{N_c + 2\alpha}$.
- **Laplace smoothing** with $\alpha = 1$ is the **default**; it prevents zero-frequency collapse on unseen words.
- **MNB vs. BNB**: MNB for long texts (counts matter), BNB for short texts (presence/absence).
- **Key optimizations**: Laplace smoothing, log-space, n-grams, negation tagging, TF-IDF, Complement NB, feature selection.
- **Evaluation**: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 — use F1 when classes are imbalanced.
- **Why it works for sentiment**: Sentiment is local, words like "love"/"hate" carry strong individual signal, and rank-order is preserved even with wrong absolute probabilities.
- **Complexity**: $O(n \cdot \vert V \vert)$ for training, $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert \mathcal{C} \vert)$ for inference.
- **KTU must-show items**: explicit smoothing equation, log-space conversion, prior term, and final argmax.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logistic Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT742 - NATURAL LANGAUGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Logistic Regression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Logistic Regression** is a **supervised learning algorithm** belonging to the family of *Generalized Linear Models (GLM)*, used primarily for **binary classification** tasks. Despite the word "regression" in its name, it is fundamentally a **classification algorithm** that models the *probability* that a given input instance belongs to a specific category (typically the positive class, $y=1$).

Mathematically, it applies the **logistic (sigmoid) function** to a linear combination of input features to constrain the output within the open interval $(0, 1)$, which is then interpretable as a probability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Logistic Regression is the foundational probabilistic classifier in NLP. It forms the building block for **Text Classification**, **Sentiment Analysis**, and **Spam Detection** before one graduates to deep learning approaches.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Pass/Fail Exam" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are a teacher predicting whether a student will **PASS** an exam based on the number of hours they studied. Intuitively:
> - If a student studies **0 hours**, the probability of passing is very low (close to **0**).
> - If a student studies **100 hours**, the probability of passing is very high (close to **1**).
> - However, the relationship is **not linear**. Going from 2 to 4 hours of study has a *huge* impact on passing probability, but going from 50 to 52 hours has *almost no additional impact*.
>
> This **S-shaped (sigmoidal) probability curve** is precisely what Logistic Regression models. Linear regression would predict probabilities greater than 1 or less than 0, which is nonsensical for probabilities. Logistic Regression elegantly solves this by "squeezing" the linear output into the $[0, 1]$ range using the sigmoid function.

### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Sigmoid Function** ($\sigma$) | The S-shaped activation that maps any real number to $(0,1)$ |
| **Logit / Log-Odds** | The natural logarithm of the odds: $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{p}{1-p}\right)$ |
| **Odds Ratio** | The ratio $\dfrac{p}{1-p}$, representing likelihood of an event |
| **Decision Boundary** | The threshold (typically $0.5$) that separates the predicted classes |
| **Cross-Entropy Loss** | The cost function measuring prediction error for probability outputs |
| **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** | The principle used to derive the optimal weights |
| **Weight Vector** ($\mathbf{w}$) | The learnable parameters associated with each input feature |
| **Bias Term** ($b$) | The intercept that shifts the decision boundary |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Not Linear Regression for Classification?

Linear regression outputs continuous values in $(-\infty, +\infty)$, which is unsuitable for probability estimation. A probability must lie strictly in $[0,1]$. Additionally, linear regression is highly sensitive to outliers and unbounded predictions, which violates the **closed-interval constraint of probability**.

### 2.2 Operational Steps of the Algorithm

1. **Linear Combination**: Compute a weighted sum of input features.
   $$z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + \dots + w_n x_n + b = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$$

2. **Apply Sigmoid Function**: Squash the linear output into $(0, 1)$.
   $$\hat{y} = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

3. **Threshold Decision**: Convert probability into a class label.
   $$\hat{class} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \hat{y} \geq 0.5 \\ 0 & \text{if } \hat{y} < 0.5 \end{cases}$$

4. **Measure Error**: Compute the Binary Cross-Entropy loss over all training samples.

5. **Optimize Weights**: Update $\mathbf{w}$ and $b$ using **Gradient Descent** to minimize loss.

6. **Iterate**: Repeat steps 4–5 until convergence (loss stabilizes or maximum epochs reached).

### 2.3 Why Mean Squared Error Fails Here

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If we use MSE with the sigmoid output, the resulting cost function is **non-convex**, leading to multiple local minima. Gradient descent may converge to a suboptimal solution. The **Log-Loss / Binary Cross-Entropy** yields a **convex** cost surface, guaranteeing convergence to the global minimum.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Description / Role** |
|---|---|---|
| Linear Score | $z = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$ | Raw linear output before activation |
| Sigmoid (Logistic) | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Activation; maps $\mathbb{R} \to (0,1)$ |
| Hypothesis | $h_{\mathbf{w}}(\mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b)$ | Predicted probability $P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x})$ |
| Log-Odds (Logit) | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{h}{1-h}\right) = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$ | Linear in parameters — foundation of GLM |
| Binary Cross-Entropy | $J(\mathbf{w}) = -\dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[y^{(i)}\log(h^{(i)}) + (1-y^{(i)})\log(1-h^{(i)})\right]$ | Convex cost function |
| Gradient (w.r.t. $w_j$) | $\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(h^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)x_j^{(i)}$ | Same elegant form as linear regression! |
| Gradient (w.r.t. $b$) | $\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial b} = \dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(h^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)$ | Bias gradient |
| Update Rule | $w_j \leftarrow w_j - \alpha \dfrac{\partial J}{\partial w_j}$ | Gradient descent step |
| Decision Boundary | $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | Implicit separating hyperplane |
| Softmax (Multiclass) | $P(y=k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{e^{\mathbf{w}_k^\top \mathbf{x}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{\mathbf{w}_j^\top \mathbf{x}}}$ | Generalization for $K>2$ classes |
| L2 Regularization | $J_{reg}(\mathbf{w}) = J(\mathbf{w}) + \lambda \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{w}$ | Prevents overfitting |
| L1 Regularization | $J_{reg}(\mathbf{w}) = J(\mathbf{w}) + \lambda \sum_{j} \vert w_j \vert$ | Induces sparsity |
| Log-Odds Identity | $\sigma^{-1}(p) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{p}{1-p}\right)$ | Inverse of sigmoid (logit function) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the industry, Logistic Regression is the **workhorse baseline** for:
- **Spam Detection** (Gmail's initial filters)
- **Credit Card Fraud Detection** (probability of fraudulent transaction)
- **Medical Diagnosis** (probability of disease given symptoms)
- **NLP Sentiment Polarity Classification** (positive vs. negative review)
- **Click-Through Rate (CTR) Prediction** in online advertising
- **Customer Churn Prediction** in telecom/SaaS platforms

It is preferred over complex models when **interpretability**, **low latency**, and **probabilistic confidence scores** are required.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Sigmoid Function from Log-Odds

**Step 1 — Define the Odds.** For a probability $p \in (0,1)$, the *odds* of an event are defined as:

$$\text{odds}(p) = \frac{p}{1-p}$$

**Step 2 — Take the Logarithm.** The *log-odds* (or *logit*) function is:

$$\text{logit}(p) = \ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)$$

The logit maps $(0,1) \to (-\infty, +\infty)$, which is exactly the range of a linear model.

**Step 3 — Equate Logit to Linear Model.** We set the log-odds equal to the linear combination:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $p$.** Exponentiating both sides:

$$\frac{p}{1-p} = e^{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$p = (1-p) \cdot e^{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b}$$

$$p = e^{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b} - p \cdot e^{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b}$$

$$p + p \cdot e^{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b} = e^{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b}$$

$$p \cdot \left(1 + e^{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b}\right) = e^{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b}$$

**Step 5 — Final Sigmoid Form.**

$$p = \frac{e^{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b}}{1 + e^{\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b)}}$$

This is precisely the sigmoid function $\sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b)$. Thus, Logistic Regression is the **inverse logit of a linear model**, which justifies its membership in the Generalized Linear Model family.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Cost Function via Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)

**Step 1 — Express Class Probability.** For a single training example, the probability of the class label is:

$$P(y \mid \mathbf{x}; \mathbf{w}) = \hat{y}^{\,y}(1-\hat{y})^{\,1-y}$$

This compact expression handles both classes simultaneously: if $y=1$, we get $\hat{y}$; if $y=0$, we get $1-\hat{y}$.

**Step 2 — Likelihood over $m$ Independent Samples.** Assuming i.i.d. samples:

$$L(\mathbf{w}) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} P(y^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}^{(i)}; \mathbf{w}) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \hat{y}^{(i)\,y^{(i)}} (1-\hat{y}^{(i)})^{\,1-y^{(i)}}$$

**Step 3 — Log-Likelihood.** Take the natural log (monotonic transformation preserves the optimum):

$$\ell(\mathbf{w}) = \ln L(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \ln \hat{y}^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)}) \ln(1-\hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Convert Maximization to Minimization.** Negate the log-likelihood and average over $m$ samples:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = -\frac{1}{m}\,\ell(\mathbf{w}) = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[y^{(i)}\ln \hat{y}^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)})\ln(1-\hat{y}^{(i)})\right]$$

This is the **Binary Cross-Entropy Loss**, which we minimize using gradient descent.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Gradient of the Cost Function

**Step 1 — Recall the Chain.** We have $\hat{y}^{(i)} = \sigma(z^{(i)})$ where $z^{(i)} = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b$.

A useful identity of the sigmoid is its elegant derivative:

$$\frac{d\sigma(z)}{dz} = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z)) = \hat{y}(1-\hat{y})$$

**Step 2 — Compute Partial Derivative.** Using the chain rule on a single sample:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = -\frac{1}{m}\left[\frac{y}{\hat{y}} - \frac{1-y}{1-\hat{y}}\right] \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial w_j}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the Bracket Term.**

$$\frac{y}{\hat{y}} - \frac{1-y}{1-\hat{y}} = \frac{y(1-\hat{y}) - (1-y)\hat{y}}{\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})} = \frac{y - y\hat{y} - \hat{y} + y\hat{y}}{\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})} = \frac{y - \hat{y}}{\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Sigmoid Derivative.** Since $\dfrac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial w_j} = \hat{y}(1-\hat{y}) \cdot x_j$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = -\frac{1}{m}\cdot \frac{y - \hat{y}}{\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})} \cdot \hat{y}(1-\hat{y}) \cdot x_j = \frac{1}{m}(\hat{y} - y) x_j$$

**Step 5 — Final Gradient Form.** Summing over all $m$ samples:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)x_j^{(i)}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Remarkable Elegance:** The gradient has the *exact same form* as linear regression. The nonlinearity of the sigmoid is fully absorbed into $\hat{y}$, making gradient computation clean and efficient.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Decision Boundary

The decision boundary is the locus of points where the predicted probability equals the threshold (typically $0.5$):

$$\sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b) = 0.5$$

Since $\sigma(z) = 0.5$ only when $z = 0$:

$$\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$$

In a 2D feature space, this becomes a straight line $w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b = 0$. With polynomial or interaction features, the boundary becomes a **curve** (e.g., circle, ellipse), demonstrating that Logistic Regression can produce non-linear decision boundaries *without* transforming the classifier itself.

---

### 3.5 Multiclass Extension: Softmax Regression

For $K$ classes, the hypothesis generalizes to the **softmax function**:

$$P(y = k \mid \mathbf{x}; \mathbf{W}) = \frac{e^{\mathbf{w}_k^\top \mathbf{x} + b_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{\mathbf{w}_j^\top \mathbf{x} + b_j}}$$

The corresponding loss is the **Categorical Cross-Entropy**:

$$J(\mathbf{W}) = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{1}\{y^{(i)} = k\}\,\log P(y^{(i)} = k \mid \mathbf{x}^{(i)})$$

This is the direct generalization used in NLP tasks such as multi-class document classification (e.g., categorizing news into Sports, Politics, Tech, etc.).

---

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Illustration (Conceptual)

Consider a 1D sentiment classifier with feature $x$ = "number of positive adjectives" and a trained model $\hat{y} = \sigma(2x - 1)$.

| $x$ | $z = 2x-1$ | $\hat{y} = \sigma(z)$ | Predicted Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $-1$ | $0.269$ | Negative (0) |
| 1 | $1$ | $0.731$ | Positive (1) |
| 2 | $3$ | $0.953$ | Positive (1) |
| 5 | $9$ | $0.9999$ | Positive (1) |

Decision boundary: $2x - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 0.5$. Any review with $> 0.5$ positive adjectives is predicted positive.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: Logistic Regression Pipeline

The diagram below captures the **complete inference and training lifecycle** of a Logistic Regression model — from raw text input to class prediction, and the parallel training loop that learns the parameters.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Text Document] --> B[Feature Extraction TF-IDF or Bag of Words]
    B --> C[Feature Vector x of size n]
    C --> D[Linear Combination z equals w dot x plus b]
    D --> E[Sigmoid Function sigma of z]
    E --> F[Probability Score h between 0 and 1]
    F --> G{Threshold 0 point 5}
    G -->|h greater than 0 point 5| H[Predicted Class equals 1 Positive]
    G -->|h less than 0 point 5| I[Predicted Class equals 0 Negative]

    J[Initialize Weights w and bias b] --> K[Forward Pass Compute h]
    C --> K
    K --> L[Compute Binary Cross Entropy Loss J of w]
    M[True Labels y] --> L
    L --> N[Compute Gradients partial J over partial w]
    N --> O[Update Parameters w and b using Gradient Descent]
    O --> P{Convergence or Max Epochs Reached}
    P -->|No| K
    P -->|Yes| Q[Trained Model Ready for Inference]
    Q --> D
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: The Logistic Unit Computational Graph

The following diagram isolates the **mathematical computational graph** of a single neuron-like logistic unit, showing how the linear and nonlinear components interact.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[x1] --> S1[Sum Block]
    X2[x2] --> S1
    X3[x3] --> S1
    W1[w1] --> S1
    W2[w2] --> S1
    W3[w3] --> S1
    B[Bias b] --> S1
    S1 --> Z[z equals wT x plus b]
    Z --> SIG[Sigmoid Activation sigma]
    SIG --> OUT[Output Probability h in 0 1]
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Decision Boundary in 2D Feature Space

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Region Class 0] -->|Below Boundary| DB[Decision Line wT x plus b equals 0]
    DB -->|Above Boundary| R2[Region Class 1]
    R1 -.->|Examples on left side| EX1[Negative Class Samples]
    R2 -.->|Examples on right side| EX2[Positive Class Samples]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Diagrams:** The Mermaid block above is rendered as a *block-level functional architecture flow* because the underlying concept (geometric decision hyperplane) cannot be faithfully represented in plain Mermaid syntax. For a precise geometric picture, students are encouraged to draw the classical 2D plot with the **sigmoid curve** $\sigma(z)$ and the **decision line** $z=0$ on graph paper.

### 4.4 Mapping: NLP Text-to-Probability Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Text Review] --> A2[Tokenization]
    A2 --> A3[Stopword Removal]
    A3 --> A4[TF IDF Vectorization]
    A4 --> A5[Sparse Feature Vector]
    A5 --> A6[Logistic Regression Classifier]
    A6 --> A7[Probability of Positive Sentiment]
    A7 --> A8{Threshold Decision}
    A8 -->|High Probability| A9[Label POSITIVE]
    A8 -->|Low Probability| A10[Label NEGATIVE]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define Logistic Regression. Why is it preferred over Linear Regression for binary classification tasks? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Logistic Regression is a **supervised probabilistic classification algorithm** that models the probability of a binary outcome using the **sigmoid (logistic) function** $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$, where $z = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$. It outputs values strictly in the interval $(0,1)$, interpretable as $P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x})$.

It is preferred over Linear Regression for classification because:
1. Linear Regression produces unbounded outputs that can exceed $[0,1]$, which is invalid for probabilities.
2. Logistic Regression uses a **convex** cost function (Binary Cross-Entropy), guaranteeing convergence to the global minimum, whereas MSE with sigmoid is **non-convex**.
3. Logistic Regression outputs **calibrated probabilities**, not just class labels, enabling confidence-aware decision-making.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]**
**State and explain the sigmoid function. Mention any two of its key properties. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **sigmoid (logistic) function** is defined as:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

It maps any real-valued input $z \in \mathbb{R}$ to the open interval $(0, 1)$, producing an **S-shaped curve** that is monotonically increasing.

**Key Properties:**

1. **Range:** $\sigma(z) \in (0, 1)$ for all $z \in \mathbb{R}$ — never reaches exactly $0$ or $1$.
2. **Symmetry:** $\sigma(-z) = 1 - \sigma(z)$, i.e., the function is point-symmetric about $(0, 0.5)$.
3. **Derivative:** $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$, which is maximized at $z=0$ with value $0.25$.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a) Derive the cost function (Binary Cross-Entropy Loss) for Logistic Regression using the Maximum Likelihood Estimation principle. Show all steps clearly. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Setup:** For a single training example $(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ where $y^{(i)} \in \{0, 1\}$ and $\hat{y}^{(i)} = h_{\mathbf{w}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b)$:

$$P(y^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}^{(i)}; \mathbf{w}) = \hat{y}^{(i)\,y^{(i)}}\,(1-\hat{y}^{(i)})^{\,1-y^{(i)}}$$

**Step 2 — Likelihood Function.** For $m$ independent samples:

$$L(\mathbf{w}) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \hat{y}^{(i)\,y^{(i)}}(1-\hat{y}^{(i)})^{\,1-y^{(i)}}$$

**Step 3 — Log-Likelihood.** Applying $\ln$:

$$\ell(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)}\ln \hat{y}^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)})\ln(1-\hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Cost.** Negate and average to form a minimization objective:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[y^{(i)}\ln \hat{y}^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)})\ln(1-\hat{y}^{(i)})\right]$$

This is the **Binary Cross-Entropy Loss**, a convex function of $\mathbf{w}$, suitable for gradient-based optimization. **(7 Marks — full credit awarded for explicit likelihood, log-likelihood, and final negated form.)**

---

**(b) Derive the gradient $\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial w_j}$ of the Logistic Regression cost function and state the gradient descent update rule. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recursive Form.** $J(\mathbf{w}) = -\dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[y^{(i)}\ln \hat{y}^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)})\ln(1-\hat{y}^{(i)})\right]$ with $\hat{y}^{(i)} = \sigma(z^{(i)})$, $z^{(i)} = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b$.

**Step 2 — Sigmoid Derivative Identity.**

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}} = \hat{y}^{(i)}(1-\hat{y}^{(i)})$$

**Step 3 — Chain Rule Application.**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[\frac{y^{(i)}}{\hat{y}^{(i)}} - \frac{1-y^{(i)}}{1-\hat{y}^{(i)}}\right] \cdot \hat{y}^{(i)}(1-\hat{y}^{(i)}) \cdot x_j^{(i)}$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic Simplification.** The bracket term simplifies as:

$$\frac{y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)}}{\hat{y}^{(i)}(1-\hat{y}^{(i)})} \cdot \hat{y}^{(i)}(1-\hat{y}^{(i)}) = y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)}$$

**Step 5 — Final Gradient.**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)x_j^{(i)}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Update Rule.**

$$w_j \leftarrow w_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)x_j^{(i)}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate** (typical range: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-1}$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students forget to flip the sign when taking the partial derivative of the *negated* log-likelihood. The final gradient **must be** $(\hat{y} - y)$, not $(y - \hat{y})$. Also, the $\dfrac{1}{m}$ averaging factor is frequently missed — both errors cost 1 mark each.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Pattern]**

**(a) Explain Multinomial Logistic Regression (Softmax Regression) for multi-class classification. State the hypothesis and the categorical cross-entropy loss. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Motivation.** Binary Logistic Regression extends to $K \geq 2$ classes via the **Softmax Regression** model, the direct generalization used in NLP for tasks like topic classification.

**Step 2 — Hypothesis.** For $K$ classes with weight matrix $\mathbf{W} = [\mathbf{w}_1, \dots, \mathbf{w}_K]$ and bias vector $\mathbf{b}$:

$$P(y = k \mid \mathbf{x}; \mathbf{W}) = \frac{e^{\mathbf{w}_k^\top \mathbf{x} + b_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{\mathbf{w}_j^\top \mathbf{x} + b_j}}$$

The denominator ensures all class probabilities sum to $1$, forming a valid probability distribution.

**Step 3 — Categorical Cross-Entropy Loss.** Using the one-hot label indicator $\mathbb{1}\{y^{(i)} = k\}$:

$$J(\mathbf{W}) = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{1}\{y^{(i)} = k\}\,\log P(y^{(i)} = k \mid \mathbf{x}^{(i)})$$

**Step 4 — Training.** Gradients are computed for each $\mathbf{w}_k$, and the parameters are updated using gradient descent (or advanced optimizers like Adam). The **argmax** of the softmax output yields the predicted class:

$$\hat{y} = \underset{k \in \{1, \dots, K\}}{\arg\max}\; P(y = k \mid \mathbf{x})$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Stability.** In practice, the softmax computation subtracts the maximum logit $\max_k(\mathbf{w}_k^\top \mathbf{x})$ from all logits before exponentiation to prevent overflow:

$$\text{softmax}(z_k) = \frac{e^{z_k - z_{\max}}}{\sum_j e^{z_j - z_{\max}}}$$

---

**(b) Discuss the role of L1 and L2 regularization in Logistic Regression. How do they modify the cost function and gradient? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — The Overfitting Problem.** Logistic Regression can overfit high-dimensional NLP feature spaces (e.g., 50,000-dimensional TF-IDF vectors). Regularization penalizes large weights to improve generalization.

**Step 2 — L2 Regularization (Ridge).** Adds the squared magnitude of weights:

$$J_{L2}(\mathbf{w}) = J(\mathbf{w}) + \lambda\,\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{w} = J(\mathbf{w}) + \lambda\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j^2$$

**Step 3 — Modified Gradient with L2.**

$$\frac{\partial J_{L2}}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)} + 2\lambda w_j$$

**Step 4 — L1 Regularization (Lasso).** Adds the absolute magnitude of weights:

$$J_{L1}(\mathbf{w}) = J(\mathbf{w}) + \lambda\sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert w_j \vert$$

**Step 5 — Modified Gradient with L1.** The subgradient is:

$$\frac{\partial J_{L1}}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)} + \lambda\,\text{sign}(w_j)$$

**Step 6 — Comparison.**

| Aspect | L1 (Lasso) | L2 (Ridge) |
|---|---|---|
| Penalty | $\vert w_j \vert$ | $w_j^2$ |
| Effect on weights | Drives some weights to **exactly zero** (sparse) | Shrinks all weights **towards zero** (dense) |
| Feature selection | **Implicit** — eliminates irrelevant features | No feature elimination |
| NLP use case | High-dimensional sparse text (e.g., vocabulary features) | Dense embeddings (e.g., Word2Vec) |
| Computational ease | Subgradient; non-differentiable at $0$ | Smooth and differentiable everywhere |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When writing regularization in the exam, students often write $J + \lambda \vert \mathbf{w} \vert$ ambiguously. **Always expand** to $\lambda \sum w_j$ (L1) or $\lambda \sum w_j^2$ (L2) for full credit. Also, $\lambda$ should be **explicitly mentioned** as the *regularization strength hyperparameter*, not left as an undefined symbol.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision Bullet Points**

- **Definition:** Logistic Regression is a **probabilistic binary classifier** using the **sigmoid function** to output $P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}) \in (0,1)$.
- **Sigmoid Identity:** $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$, with derivative $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$.
- **Logit Link:** $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{p}{1-p}\right) = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$ — establishes Logistic Regression as a Generalized Linear Model.
- **Cost Function:** $J(\mathbf{w}) = -\dfrac{1}{m}\sum\left[y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})\right]$ — convex, derived from MLE.
- **Gradient:** $\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \dfrac{1}{m}\sum(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)}$ — *same form as linear regression*.
- **Decision Boundary:** $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ — linear in feature space, but can be non-linear with engineered features.
- **Multiclass:** Use **Softmax** + **Categorical Cross-Entropy**; pick class via **argmax**.
- **Regularization:** L1 (sparse, feature selection) vs. L2 (dense, weight shrinkage); both add $\lambda \cdot$ penalty term.
- **NLP Applications:** Sentiment analysis, spam filtering, topic classification — features typically TF-IDF or embeddings.
- **No MSE:** Never use MSE for logistic regression — yields **non-convex** loss surface; always use **cross-entropy**.
- **Convexity:** Cross-entropy is **strictly convex** for linearly separable data → guaranteed global minimum.
- **Threshold Tuning:** Default $0.5$ may not be optimal; tune based on **Precision-Recall trade-off**.
- **Hypothesis Interpretation:** $\hat{y}$ is the **probability of belonging to the positive class**, not the class itself.
- **Vectorization Tip:** Implement using matrix multiplications $\mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}$ for efficiency — crucial for large NLP corpora.
- **Regularization Strength:** $\lambda$ is a **hyperparameter** tuned via **cross-validation**; too high → underfitting, too low → overfitting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Support Vector Machine for Text Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT742 - NATURAL LANGAUGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Support Vector Machine for Text Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** is a **supervised, discriminative, non-probabilistic** binary linear classifier that constructs an **optimal separating hyperplane** in a high-dimensional feature space by **maximizing the geometric margin** between two classes. In the context of **Natural Language Processing (NLP)**, SVMs are widely used for text classification tasks such as **spam detection, sentiment analysis, news categorization, and topic identification**, where documents are represented as high-dimensional sparse vectors (using schemes like **TF-IDF** or **Bag-of-Words**).

Mathematically, given a labeled training set $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ where $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is a feature vector and $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ is the class label, SVM finds a hyperplane $w \cdot x + b = 0$ such that the **margin** $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$ between the two classes is maximized.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Widest Road Between Two Villages":**
> Imagine two neighboring villages (Class +1 and Class -1) separated by a river. You are tasked with building a **bridge** (the decision boundary/hyperplane) to connect them. However, you do not just want any narrow bridge — you want the **widest possible road** so that the bridge is robust, safe, and tolerates small shifts in village boundaries. The "widest road" is the **maximum margin hyperplane**. The houses closest to the riverbank on both sides — the ones that define the width of the road — are the **support vectors**. Everything else (houses far from the river) is irrelevant to the placement of the bridge.

For text classification, each **document** is mapped to a point in a high-dimensional space (each dimension = one word in the vocabulary). The SVM finds the "widest road" that separates positive-class documents (e.g., "spam") from negative-class documents (e.g., "ham") in this space.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

- **Hyperplane** $w \cdot x + b = 0$: The decision surface that separates the two classes in feature space.
- **Support Vectors**: The data points that lie **closest to the hyperplane** and directly influence its position and orientation. They "support" the margin.
- **Margin** $\gamma = \frac{2}{\|w\|}$: The perpendicular distance between the two parallel bounding hyperplanes that touch the support vectors.
- **Kernel Function** $K(x_i, x_j)$: A function that computes the dot product in a higher (possibly infinite) dimensional space, enabling non-linear classification.
- **Soft Margin Parameter** $C$: A penalty hyperparameter that controls the trade-off between margin width and classification errors.
- **Slack Variables** $\xi_i$: Non-negative variables that allow some training points to violate the margin constraint (for non-separable data).

## 1.4 Why SVM for Text Classification?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why SVMs are the de-facto standard for classical text classification:**
> 1. **High-Dimensional Efficiency**: Text data is naturally high-dimensional (vocabulary size $\approx 10^4$ to $10^6$ features) but sparse. SVMs excel in such settings.
> 2. **Robustness to Overfitting**: Maximizing margin provides excellent generalization, even with limited training data.
> 3. **Effective with Linear Kernels**: Unlike many algorithms, a simple **linear kernel** often achieves state-of-the-art performance on text.
> 4. **No Need for Probabilistic Interpretation**: Outputs are geometric (distances) rather than probabilities, simplifying decision-making.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown (Structured Logic)

### Step 1: Feature Representation of Text
Before applying SVM, raw text must be transformed into numerical vectors. Common representations include:

- **Bag-of-Words (BoW)**: Each document $d$ is represented as a vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^{|V|}$ where $|V|$ is the vocabulary size, and $x_j$ is the count (or presence) of word $j$ in $d$.
- **TF-IDF (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency)**: Weights each term by its importance, down-weighting common words like "the", "is".
- **Word Embeddings**: Dense vectors (Word2Vec, GloVe) capturing semantic similarity.

The TF-IDF weight is computed as:

$$w_{t,d} = \text{tf}(t, d) \times \text{idf}(t)$$

where $\text{idf}(t) = \log\frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$, with $N$ = total documents and $\text{df}(t)$ = number of documents containing term $t$.

### Step 2: Linear Separability Check
In a perfectly separable case, there exists a hyperplane that correctly classifies **all** training points. In real text data, this is rare — hence the **soft-margin** SVM is used.

### Step 3: Constructing the Maximum Margin Hyperplane
The decision function is:

$$f(x) = \text{sign}(w \cdot x + b)$$

The hyperplane is defined by the equation:

$$w \cdot x + b = 0$$

The two parallel canonical hyperplanes that pass through the closest points (support vectors) are:

$$w \cdot x + b = +1 \quad (\text{positive class boundary})$$
$$w \cdot x + b = -1 \quad (\text{negative class boundary})$$

### Step 4: Optimization Problem
The objective is to **maximize the margin** $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$, equivalent to **minimizing** $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$. This yields the **primal optimization problem**:

$$\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$$

subject to:

$$y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$$

### Step 5: Handling Non-Separable Data (Soft Margin)
Introduce **slack variables** $\xi_i \geq 0$ and penalty parameter $C$:

$$\min_{w, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$$

subject to:

$$y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of $C$:**
> - **Large $C$**: Strict penalty on misclassification — narrower margin, fewer errors on training data (risk of overfitting).
> - **Small $C$**: Tolerant of misclassification — wider margin, better generalization (risk of underfitting).

### Step 6: Dual Formulation & Kernel Trick
By introducing **Lagrange multipliers** $\alpha_i \geq 0$, the primal is converted into the **dual problem**:

$$\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j)$$

subject to:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0, \quad 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$$

The decision function becomes:

$$f(x) = \text{sign}\left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i (x_i \cdot x) + b \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Kernel Trick:** Replace the dot product $x_i \cdot x_j$ with a kernel function $K(x_i, x_j)$ to implicitly map data to a higher-dimensional space:
> - **Linear Kernel**: $K(x_i, x_j) = x_i \cdot x_j$
> - **Polynomial Kernel**: $K(x_i, x_j) = (x_i \cdot x_j + 1)^d$
> - **RBF (Gaussian) Kernel**: $K(x_i, x_j) = \exp(-\gamma \|x_i - x_j\|^2)$
> - **Sigmoid Kernel**: $K(x_i, x_j) = \tanh(\kappa (x_i \cdot x_j) + \theta)$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Key Insight |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Decision Function | $f(x) = \text{sign}(w \cdot x + b)$ | Output is the predicted class |
| 2 | Hyperplane Equation | $w \cdot x + b = 0$ | Separates the two classes |
| 3 | Geometric Margin | $\gamma = \frac{2}{\|w\|}$ | Distance between the two boundary hyperplanes |
| 4 | Functional Margin | $\hat{\gamma}_i = y_i(w \cdot x_i + b)$ | Sign-correct if $\geq 1$ for correct classification |
| 5 | Primal Objective (Hard Margin) | $\min \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$ s.t. $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1$ | Maximum margin, linearly separable |
| 6 | Primal Objective (Soft Margin) | $\min \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C \sum_i \xi_i$ s.t. $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ | Allows misclassification with penalty $C$ |
| 7 | Dual Objective | $\max_\alpha \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(x_i, x_j)$ | Enables the kernel trick |
| 8 | Dual Constraints | $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0, \; 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ | Box constraints on Lagrange multipliers |
| 9 | KKT Condition (Support Vector) | $\alpha_i > 0 \Rightarrow x_i$ lies on margin | Defines which points are support vectors |
| 10 | Linear Kernel | $K(x_i, x_j) = x_i \cdot x_j$ | Default for text classification |
| 11 | Polynomial Kernel | $K(x_i, x_j) = (x_i \cdot x_j + 1)^d$ | Captures polynomial feature interactions |
| 12 | RBF Kernel | $K(x_i, x_j) = \exp(-\gamma \|x_i - x_j\|^2)$ | Infinite-dimensional, most popular non-linear |
| 13 | Weight Vector (in terms of SVs) | $w = \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i x_i$ | Sparse — only support vectors contribute |
| 14 | TF-IDF Weight | $w_{t,d} = \text{tf}(t,d) \cdot \log\frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$ | Standard text feature weighting |
| 15 | One-vs-Rest Multi-class | Train $K$ binary SVMs; pick max $f_k(x)$ | Simplest multi-class extension |
| 16 | One-vs-One Multi-class | Train $\frac{K(K-1)}{2}$ binary SVMs; vote | Better for large $K$ |
