## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Spam Filtering (Email Systems)**: Linear SVMs achieve >98% accuracy on spam detection by treating emails as TF-IDF vectors.
- **Sentiment Analysis (Product Reviews)**: Classifies reviews as positive/negative with high precision.
- **News Categorization (Google News, Reuters)**: Multi-class SVM (one-vs-all) sorts articles into predefined topics.
- **Bioinformatics & Medical NLP**: Categorizing clinical notes, detecting adverse drug events.
- **Legal Document Classification**: Routing case files to appropriate departments.
- **Information Retrieval (IR)**: Rank-based document classification in search engines.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Geometric Margin to the Primal Optimization Problem

### Step 1: Define the Decision Hyperplane
Let the separating hyperplane be parameterized by a weight vector $w \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and bias $b \in \mathbb{R}$. The hyperplane is the set of points satisfying:

$$w \cdot x + b = 0$$

The two parallel boundary hyperplanes that pass through the support vectors of each class are:

$$w \cdot x + b = +1 \quad \text{and} \quad w \cdot x + b = -1$$

### Step 2: Compute the Distance Between the Two Boundary Hyperplanes
The perpendicular distance from any point $x_0$ to the hyperplane $w \cdot x + b = 0$ is given by:

$$d = \frac{\vert w \cdot x_0 + b \vert}{\|w\|}$$

For the support vector $x_+$ on the positive boundary ($w \cdot x_+ + b = +1$) and the support vector $x_-$ on the negative boundary ($w \cdot x_- + b = -1$), the total margin (sum of distances from both sides) is:

$$\gamma = \frac{\vert +1 \vert}{\|w\|} + \frac{\vert -1 \vert}{\|w\|} = \frac{2}{\|w\|}$$

### Step 3: Convert Maximization to Minimization
Maximizing the margin $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$ is equivalent to minimizing $\|w\|$, and to make the problem quadratic and differentiable, we minimize $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$. This yields the **hard-margin SVM primal**:

$$\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i = 1, \ldots, N$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$ and not just $\|w\|$?**
> The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is purely for mathematical convenience. When we take the gradient $\nabla_w \left( \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 \right) = w$, the constant is absorbed, simplifying subsequent derivations.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Soft-Margin Primal Formulation

### Step 1: Introduce Slack Variables
For non-separable data, allow some points to lie inside the margin or even be misclassified. Introduce slack variables $\xi_i \geq 0$ for each training point:

$$y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0$$

### Step 2: Add Penalty Term
Penalize the sum of slacks to the objective:

$$\min_{w, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$$

subject to:

$$y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0, \quad C > 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The role of $C$:** $C$ balances the trade-off between a **wide margin** (small $\|w\|$) and **training errors** (small $\sum \xi_i$). As $C \to \infty$, the soft-margin SVM reduces to the hard-margin SVM.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Lagrangian Dual Formulation

### Step 1: Construct the Lagrangian
For the **hard-margin** primal, introduce Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ for each constraint:

$$\mathcal{L}(w, b, \alpha) = \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) - 1 \right]$$

### Step 2: Take Partial Derivatives and Set to Zero
Compute $\nabla_w \mathcal{L} = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = w - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad w = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i$$

Compute $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

### Step 3: Substitute Back into the Lagrangian
Substitute $w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i$ into $\mathcal{L}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}(w, b, \alpha) &= \frac{1}{2}\left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i \right) \cdot \left( \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_j y_j x_j \right) - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i \left( \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_j y_j x_j \cdot x_i + b \right) - 1 \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j) - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j) - b \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \\
&= -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j) + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$, the $b$ term vanishes.

### Step 4: The Dual Problem
Maximize the Lagrangian (since it is concave in $\alpha$):

$$\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j)$$

subject to:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0, \quad \alpha_i \geq 0$$

### Step 5: Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) Conditions
At optimality, the following must hold:

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{(a) Stationarity:} & w &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i \\
&\text{(b) Primal Feasibility:} & y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) &\geq 1 \\
&\text{(c) Dual Feasibility:} & \alpha_i &\geq 0 \\
&\text{(d) Complementary Slackness:} & \alpha_i \left[ y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) - 1 \right] &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Insight from Complementary Slackness:** $\alpha_i > 0$ **only if** $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) = 1$, i.e., the point $x_i$ lies **exactly on the margin**. These are the **support vectors**. For all other points, $\alpha_i = 0$. This makes the solution **sparse** — only a few training points define the hyperplane.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: The Kernel Trick

### Step 1: Observation
The dual formulation only requires the **dot product** $x_i \cdot x_j$, never the individual vectors explicitly.

### Step 2: Implicit Mapping to Higher Dimensions
Suppose we have a mapping $\phi : \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}^D$ (where $D \gg d$). The dot product in the new space is:

$$\phi(x_i) \cdot \phi(x_j)$$

If $D$ is very large (or infinite), this is computationally infeasible.

### Step 3: Kernel Substitution
Define the **kernel function**:

$$K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i) \cdot \phi(x_j)$$

By **Mercer's Theorem**, any symmetric, positive semi-definite function $K$ corresponds to an inner product in some Hilbert space. So we can **replace** $x_i \cdot x_j$ with $K(x_i, x_j)$ without ever computing $\phi(x)$.

### Step 4: Common Kernel Examples
For polynomial features $\phi(x) = $ all monomials up to degree $d$:

$$K(x_i, x_j) = (x_i \cdot x_j + 1)^d$$

For the **RBF (Gaussian) kernel**, $\phi$ maps to an **infinite-dimensional** space:

$$K(x_i, x_j) = \exp\left( -\gamma \|x_i - x_j\|^2 \right)$$

### Step 5: Final Decision Function with Kernel
$$\begin{aligned}
f(x) &= \text{sign}\left( \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i K(x_i, x) + b \right)
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Linear Kernel Often Suffices for Text:** In text classification, the number of features (words) is enormous, so the data is typically **already high-dimensional and approximately linearly separable**. Non-linear kernels may not improve performance significantly and increase computational cost.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Primal vs. Dual Formulations

| Aspect | Primal | Dual |
|--------|--------|------|
| Variables optimized | $w, b$ (and $\xi_i$ for soft margin) | $\alpha_i$ |
| Number of variables | $d + 1$ (feature dimension) | $N$ (number of samples) |
| Useful when | $d \ll N$ (few features, many samples) | $N \ll d$ (many features, few samples) — **text case** |
| Kernel trick | Hard to incorporate | Natural — only inner products appear |
| Sparsity | Not directly sparse | Sparse — only support vectors have $\alpha_i > 0$ |
| Solution form | $w$ directly | $w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i$ |

## 3.6 Multi-Class SVM Strategies

### Strategy 1: One-vs-Rest (OvR) / One-vs-All (OvA)
- Train $K$ binary SVMs, each separating one class from the rest.
- For class $k$, train with labels: $y_i = +1$ if $x_i \in \text{class } k$, else $y_i = -1$.
- Total classifiers: **$K$**.
- Prediction: $\hat{y} = \arg\max_k f_k(x)$.

### Strategy 2: One-vs-One (OvO)
- Train one binary SVM for **every pair** of classes.
- Total classifiers: **$\frac{K(K-1)}{2}$**.
- Prediction: **majority voting** among all classifiers.

### Strategy 3: Single Multi-Class Formulation (Crammer-Singer)
- A single optimization problem with $K$ weight vectors $\{w_k\}$.
- Loss: maximizes the score of the correct class while keeping the margin from incorrect classes.
- More complex; rarely used in practice compared to OvR/OvO.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the term **support vector** in the context of a Support Vector Machine. Why are these vectors considered crucial to the model?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A support vector is a data point from the training set that lies **closest to the decision hyperplane**, i.e., it satisfies $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) = 1$. These points define the **margin** of the classifier and directly determine the orientation and position of the optimal hyperplane. They are crucial because:
1. They are the only training points with non-zero Lagrange multipliers ($\alpha_i > 0$).
2. Removing any non-support vector does not change the model, but removing a support vector would shift the hyperplane.
3. They make the solution **sparse** and computationally efficient.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** What is the **kernel trick** in SVMs? Name any two kernel functions commonly used in text classification.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **kernel trick** is a mathematical technique that allows SVMs to operate in a high-dimensional (possibly infinite-dimensional) feature space **without explicitly computing the mapping** $\phi(x)$. It replaces the dot product $x_i \cdot x_j$ in the dual formulation with a kernel function $K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i) \cdot \phi(x_j)$, which is computationally cheap.
Two commonly used kernels are:
1. **Linear Kernel**: $K(x_i, x_j) = x_i \cdot x_j$
2. **Radial Basis Function (RBF) Kernel**: $K(x_i, x_j) = \exp(-\gamma \|x_i - x_j\|^2)$

For text classification, the **linear kernel** is most commonly preferred due to the high-dimensional, sparse nature of text data.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students may choose any ONE of the two alternatives below. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 3 (14 Marks)]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** for binary text classification. Illustrate with a clear diagram showing the **maximum margin hyperplane**, **support vectors**, and **boundary hyperplanes**. Discuss the role of the geometric margin $\gamma = \frac{2}{\|w\|}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Formulate the **primal optimization problem** for soft-margin linear SVM. Explain the role of **slack variables** $\xi_i$ and the **penalty parameter** $C$. How does the choice of $C$ affect the classifier's performance? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Working of SVM for Text Classification (7 marks)**

SVM is a discriminative classifier that finds an **optimal hyperplane** separating two classes of documents (e.g., spam vs. ham) in a high-dimensional feature space.

**Step 1 — Feature Vectorization:** Each document $d$ is converted into a TF-IDF vector $x_d \in \mathbb{R}^{|V|}$, where each dimension corresponds to a vocabulary term.

**Step 2 — Decision Hyperplane:** The classifier uses the decision function $f(x) = \text{sign}(w \cdot x + b)$, where $(w, b)$ define the separating hyperplane:

$$w \cdot x + b = 0$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Margin Criterion:** Among all hyperplanes that separate the classes, SVM picks the one that **maximizes the geometric margin** $\gamma = \frac{2}{\|w\|}$.

**Step 4 — Support Vectors:** The training samples lying on the boundary hyperplanes $w \cdot x + b = \pm 1$ are called support vectors. They uniquely determine $(w, b)$.

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.2):** The diagram shows the positive class (blue) and negative class (orange) with two boundary hyperplanes and the central maximum-margin hyperplane. Support vectors (red) lie on the boundaries, and the margin is the perpendicular distance between them.

**Geometric Margin Significance:** A larger margin means greater **robustness** — small perturbations in feature values are less likely to cause misclassification. This translates to better **generalization** on unseen test documents.

---

**(b) Soft-Margin SVM Formulation (7 marks)**

**Primal Problem:**

$$\min_{w, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$$

subject to:

$$y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0, \quad i = 1, \ldots, N$$

**Role of Slack Variables $\xi_i$:**
- $\xi_i = 0$: Point $x_i$ is correctly classified and outside (or on) the margin.
- $0 < \xi_i \leq 1$: Point $x_i$ is correctly classified but lies inside the margin.
- $\xi_i > 1$: Point $x_i$ is **misclassified** (lies on the wrong side of the hyperplane).

**Role of Penalty Parameter $C$:**
- $C$ controls the trade-off between **margin width** ($\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$) and **training errors** ($\sum \xi_i$).
- **Large $C$**: Strict penalty on misclassification → narrower margin, fits training data tightly (risk of overfitting).
- **Small $C$**: Lenient penalty → wider margin, tolerates some errors (better generalization, risk of underfitting).

In practice, $C$ is chosen via **cross-validation** (e.g., grid search over $C \in \{0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100\}$).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 3 (14 Marks)]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the **kernel trick** in SVMs. Describe any **three kernel functions** (linear, polynomial, RBF) with their mathematical formulations and discuss when each is most suitable for text classification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the application of **SVM to text classification**. Discuss the **feature representation** (TF-IDF, BoW) and explain **two multi-class strategies** (one-vs-rest and one-vs-one) for extending binary SVM to $K$-class problems. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Kernel Trick in SVMs (7 marks)**

The **kernel trick** is a method of computing the dot product $\phi(x_i) \cdot \phi(x_j)$ in a transformed (higher or infinite-dimensional) space **without explicitly performing the transformation** $\phi$. The kernel function $K(x_i, x_j)$ directly returns the result, exploiting the fact that the dual formulation of SVM only requires dot products — never the individual vectors.

**Mathematical Basis:** By **Mercer's Theorem**, any symmetric, positive semi-definite function $K$ is a valid kernel.

**Three Kernels:**

**(i) Linear Kernel:** $K(x_i, x_j) = x_i \cdot x_j = \sum_{k=1}^{d} x_i^{(k)} x_j^{(k)}$
- **Most suitable for text classification** because text features are already high-dimensional and approximately linearly separable.
- Computationally efficient: $O(d)$ time per kernel evaluation.
- Equivalent to a linear SVM — no implicit feature transformation.

**(ii) Polynomial Kernel:** $K(x_i, x_j) = (x_i \cdot x_j + 1)^d$ for some degree $d \in \mathbb{N}$
- Captures **interactions between features** (e.g., $x_i^{(1)} x_j^{(1)} x_i^{(2)} x_j^{(2)}$ for $d = 2$).
- Useful when document classes are distinguished by feature combinations (e.g., "not good" indicates negative sentiment).
- Computational cost grows with $d$; can overfit for large $d$.

**(iii) RBF (Gaussian) Kernel:** $K(x_i, x_j) = \exp\left( -\gamma \|x_i - x_j\|^2 \right)$
- Maps data to an **infinite-dimensional** feature space.
- Highly flexible; can model complex non-linear decision boundaries.
- Requires careful tuning of $\gamma$: small $\gamma$ = smoother boundary; large $\gamma$ = more complex boundary (overfitting risk).
- Less commonly used for text (linear is usually sufficient), but powerful when non-linearity exists.

**When to Use Each in Text Classification:**
- **Linear**: Default choice for high-dimensional sparse text data. Fast, robust, excellent baselines.
- **Polynomial**: When you suspect feature interactions (e.g., bigrams, n-grams).
- **RBF**: When classes are clearly non-linear and feature space is small; rare for text.

---

**(b) SVM for Text Classification (7 marks)**

**Feature Representation for Text:**

**Bag-of-Words (BoW):** Each document $d$ is represented as a vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^{|V|}$ where the $j$-th component is the count (or binary presence) of vocabulary term $v_j$ in $d$. Simple but ignores word importance.

**TF-IDF:** Each term is weighted by:

$$w_{t,d} = \text{tf}(t, d) \times \log\frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$$

This down-weights frequent words (e.g., "the") and emphasizes discriminative terms. TF-IDF typically outperforms raw counts in SVM-based text classification.

**Multi-Class Strategies:**

**(i) One-vs-Rest (OvR):**
- Train $K$ binary SVMs, one per class.
- For class $k$: positive examples = documents of class $k$; negative examples = all other documents.
- Total classifiers: $K$.
- Prediction: $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} f_k(x)$ (the class with the largest decision score wins).
- **Drawback**: Class imbalance in each binary classifier (negatives $\gg$ positives).

**(ii) One-vs-One (OvO):**
- Train one binary SVM for **each pair** of classes $(k, l)$.
- Total classifiers: $\frac{K(K-1)}{2}$.
- Each classifier uses only documents from the two involved classes → balanced training sets.
- Prediction: **majority voting** — the class receiving the most "wins" across all pairwise classifiers is the output.
- **Advantage**: Each binary problem is smaller and more balanced.

**Example:** For $K = 4$ classes:
- OvR: 4 classifiers.
- OvO: $\frac{4 \times 3}{2} = 6$ classifiers.

**Practical Note:** LibSVM and scikit-learn's `SVC` use OvO by default; scikit-learn's `LinearSVC` uses OvR.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in SVM Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the Margin:** Students often incorrectly state that the margin is $\frac{1}{\|w\|}$ instead of $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$ — the factor of 2 arises from the two boundary hyperplanes.
> 2. **Misinterpreting the Dual:** Failing to mention that the dual formulation enables the **kernel trick** because it depends only on dot products — this is the **single most important reason** the dual is used.
> 3. **Forgetting KKT Conditions:** The KKT complementary slackness condition is what makes the solution **sparse** and identifies support vectors — examiners explicitly test this.
> 4. **Confusing Primal vs. Dual Variables:** $w, b$ are primal; $\alpha_i$ are dual. Don't mix them up in derivations.
> 5. **Wrong C Interpretation:** Saying "$C$ is the number of support vectors" or "$C$ is the kernel width" — these are completely incorrect. $C$ is the **trade-off parameter** between margin and misclassification.
> 6. **Skipping the Diagrammatic Explanation:** KTU examiners expect a clear, labeled diagram showing the hyperplane, support vectors, and margin. Skipping it costs 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Not Specifying Kernel for Text:** Always explicitly justify why a **linear kernel** is preferred for text (high-dimensional, sparse, near-linear separability).
> 8. **Forgetting to Substituting $w = \sum \alpha_i y_i x_i$** back into the Lagrangian during dual derivation — examiners check this step carefully.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **SVM** finds the **maximum-margin hyperplane** separating two classes in feature space.
- [x] **Decision function**: $f(x) = \text{sign}(w \cdot x + b)$.
- [x] **Geometric margin**: $\gamma = \frac{2}{\|w\|}$.
- [x] **Hard-margin primal**: $\min \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$ s.t. $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1$.
- [x] **Soft-margin primal**: $\min \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C \sum \xi_i$ s.t. $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$.
- [x] **Dual problem**: $\max_\alpha \sum \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(x_i, x_j)$ subject to $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$ and $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$.
- [x] **Weight vector in terms of support vectors**: $w = \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i x_i$ — **sparse solution**.
- [x] **KKT complementary slackness** is the theoretical reason why only support vectors contribute to $w$.
- [x] **Kernel trick** replaces $x_i \cdot x_j$ with $K(x_i, x_j)$, enabling non-linear classification at low cost.
- [x] **Common kernels**: Linear, Polynomial, RBF (Gaussian), Sigmoid.
- [x] **Linear kernel is preferred for text classification** due to high-dimensional sparse nature.
- [x] **TF-IDF** is the standard feature representation for text with SVMs.
- [x] **Multi-class extension**: One-vs-Rest ($K$ classifiers) or One-vs-One ($\frac{K(K-1)}{2}$ classifiers).
- [x] **Hyperparameter $C$**: Large $C$ = strict (low bias, high variance); Small $C$ = tolerant (high bias, low variance).
- [x] **Hyperparameter $\gamma$ (RBF only)**: Small $\gamma$ = smooth boundary; Large $\gamma$ = complex boundary.
- [x] **Computational advantage of dual**: Useful when $N < d$ (text classification scenario).
- [x] **SVMs are non-probabilistic**: Output is a geometric distance, not a probability (use Platt scaling to get probabilities).
- [x] **Key applications in NLP**: Spam filtering, sentiment analysis, news categorization, document routing, intent classification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Information Extraction (IE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT742 - NATURAL LANGAUGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Information Extraction (IE)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — INFORMATION EXTRACTION (IE)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Information Extraction (IE)** is the task of automatically extracting **structured information** (entities, relations, events, attributes, and their temporal/spatial references) from **unstructured or semi-structured natural language text** and transforming it into a machine-readable, queryable format such as a database, knowledge graph, or filled template.

In the precise vocabulary of the KTU 2024 NLP syllabus, IE is positioned as a *post-parse*, *semantic-level* operation that sits on top of Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging, chunking, and parsing, converting linguistic structure into **factual knowledge units** suitable for downstream applications such as Question Answering, Knowledge Base Population, and Decision Support Systems.

The classical definition (as per the foundational Message Understanding Conference — MUC evaluations) frames IE around five canonical subtasks:

1. **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** — detection and classification of atomic elements (PERSON, ORGANIZATION, LOCATION, DATE, MONEY, etc.).
2. **Coreference Resolution (CR)** — linking multiple mentions of the same real-world entity.
3. **Template Element Construction** — merging coreferring entity mentions into single canonical entries.
4. **Template Relation Extraction (TR)** — identifying semantic relations between template elements.
5. **Template/Scenario Filling (ST)** — populating event templates with extracted arguments.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Newspaper Highlighter

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are handed a 50-page newspaper and asked to build a spreadsheet of *every company acquisition mentioned, who bought whom, for how much, and when*. You would not rewrite the news — you would **scan, highlight** the names of companies, **underline** the buyer-seller pair, **circle** the amount, and **bracket** the date, then write each fact into a row. **Information Extraction is exactly that — but performed by algorithms on millions of documents at machine speed.**

The *spreadsheet* is the **structured output** (a knowledge base or template). The *newspaper* is the **unstructured input** (free text). The *highlighting rules* are the **IE models** (rules, statistical learners, or neural networks).

### 1.3 Why IE Matters — The Information Avalanche

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** The World Wide Web contains an estimated **>50 billion indexed pages** of unstructured text. Over **80% of enterprise data is unstructured** (text, email, social media, reports). IE is the only scalable mechanism to convert this textual *dark data* into structured form for analytics, search, BI dashboards, and AI training corpora.

The three pillars that make IE indispensable in modern NLP pipelines are:

- **Scalability** — rules + ML replace thousands of human readers.
- **Structured Compatibility** — output aligns with SQL/RDF/JSON, making it directly consumable by databases and knowledge graphs (e.g., Google Knowledge Graph, DBpedia, Wikidata).
- **Downstream Enablement** — IE output powers QA, recommendation, summarization, and reasoning.

### 1.4 Position of IE in the Standard NLP Pipeline

IE is generally placed *downstream* of morphological, syntactic, and shallow semantic processing:

$$
\text{Raw Text} \rightarrow \text{Tokenisation} \rightarrow \text{POS Tagging} \rightarrow \text{Parsing} \rightarrow \text{NER / RE / EE} \rightarrow \text{Knowledge Base}
$$

The structured artefacts produced by IE are also called **"Knowledge-Grade Facts"** or **"Slots and Fillers"** — where the *slot* is the relation or attribute name, and the *filler* is the entity or value bound to it.

### 1.5 Key Terminology Checklist (Must Memorize)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Slot** | A named attribute in a template (e.g., `BUYER`, `SELLER`, `AMOUNT`). |
| **Filler** | The extracted value filling the slot. |
| **Mention** | A textual span referring to an entity. |
| **Entity** | The real-world object (abstract, identified via coreference). |
| **Trigger Word** | Lexical cue that signals an event (e.g., *acquired, merged, died*). |
| **BIO / IOB Tags** | Sequence labelling scheme: **B**egin, **I**nside, **O**utside. |
| **Knowledge Base Population (KBP)** | Continuous enrichment of a KB from text via IE. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The IE Architecture — A Layered View

Information Extraction in the KTU 2024 syllabus is taught as a **hierarchical pipeline of subtasks**, each operating on the output of the previous one. The conceptual flow is:

$$
\text{Text} \rightarrow \text{Tokenise} \rightarrow \text{Segment} \rightarrow \text{NER} \rightarrow \text{Coreference} \rightarrow \text{Relation Extraction} \rightarrow \text{Event Extraction} \rightarrow \text{Template}
$$

### 2.2 Named Entity Recognition (NER) — The Cornerstone

NER is treated as a **sequence labelling problem**. The system scans a sentence left-to-right and assigns a tag to each token.

#### 2.2.1 BIO Tagging Scheme (Most Frequently Tested)

> [!NOTE]
> In the **BIO scheme** (also called IOB-1):
> - **B-X** = token is the *Beginning* of an entity of type X.
> - **I-X** = token is *Inside* (a continuation of) an entity of type X.
> - **O** = token is *Outside* any entity.

**Example Sentence:**
*"Apple Inc. was founded by Steve Jobs in California."*

| Token | BIO Tag |
|---|---|
| Apple | B-ORG |
| Inc. | I-ORG |
| was | O |
| founded | O |
| by | O |
| Steve | B-PER |
| Jobs | I-PER |
| in | O |
| California | B-LOC |
| . | O |

There are also extended variants — **BIOES** (Begin, Inside, Outside, End, Singleton) — used in modern neural NER to mark single-token entities and end-points explicitly.

#### 2.2.2 Approaches to NER

The KTU syllabus categorises NER approaches into four families:

**A. Rule-Based / Handcrafted**
- Use **gazetteers** (lists of known names), **regular expressions**, **morphological patterns**, and **contextual rules**.
- Example tool: **LTG (Edinburgh), LaSIE-II, SAR (SRA)**.
- *Advantage:* Interpretable, no training data. *Disadvantage:* Brittle, low recall on out-of-vocabulary entities.

**B. Dictionary-Based / Lookup**
- Direct match against a curated dictionary. Used as a **baseline** and for known entities.
- Failure mode: ambiguity (*"Paris"* — city vs. person vs. Hilton).

**C. Machine-Learning Based (Supervised)**

These are the most heavily tested approaches in KTU papers:

- **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** — generative, joint probability $P(X,Y)$.
- **Maximum Entropy Markov Model (MEMM)** — discriminative, conditional $P(Y \vert X)$.
- **Conditional Random Field (CRF)** — global discriminative model; the de-facto standard for feature-rich NER.
- **Support Vector Machines (SVM)** — one-vs-rest classifiers.
- **Neural sequence models** — BiLSTM, BiLSTM-CRF, Transformers (BERT, RoBERTa).

**D. Unsupervised / Semi-Supervised**
- **Clustering** of similar contextual mentions (e.g., *Field-independent clustering by Collins and Singer*).
- **Bootstrapping / Distant supervision** — start with a few seeds, learn patterns, iterate.

#### 2.2.3 The HMM Formulation for NER

A token is treated as a **hidden state** (the entity tag) and the **observed word** as evidence. The two key independence assumptions are:

$$
P(X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n, Y_1, Y_2, \dots, Y_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(Y_i \vert Y_{i-1}) \cdot P(X_i \vert Y_i)
$$

- $Y_i$ = tag at position $i$ (hidden).
- $X_i$ = word at position $i$ (observed).
- $P(Y_i \vert Y_{i-1})$ = **transition probability** (how often tag $Y_i$ follows $Y_{i-1}$).
- $P(X_i \vert Y_i)$ = **emission probability** (how likely the word is given the tag).

Inference is performed via the **Viterbi algorithm** to find the globally optimal tag sequence.

#### 2.2.4 CRF Formulation for NER

CRF overcomes the *label-bias problem* of MEMMs by modelling a **single global conditional distribution** over the entire sequence:

$$
P(Y \vert X) = \frac{1}{Z(X)} \exp\!\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{k} \lambda_k \, f_k(y_{i-1}, y_i, X, i) \right)
$$

Where:
- $f_k$ = feature functions (e.g., word shape, prefix, suffix, POS, surrounding context).
- $\lambda_k$ = learned weights.
- $Z(X)$ = partition function for normalisation.
- The denominator sums over **all** possible tag sequences, which is why CRF avoids MEMM's local normalisation bias.

### 2.3 Relation Extraction (RE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Relation Extraction** is the task of detecting and classifying **semantic relationships** between pairs of detected entities. Formally: given entities $e_1$ and $e_2$ in a sentence $S$, predict a relation $r \in \mathcal{R}$ (e.g., *born-in, employed-by, located-in, spouse-of, CEO-of*).

#### 2.3.1 RE Approaches

| Approach | Description | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Handcrafted Rules** | Regex over surface patterns: `<PER> was born in <LOC>`. | High precision | Low recall |
| **Supervised (Feature-based)** | SVM/MaxEnt with lexical, syntactic, dependency features. | High accuracy | Needs annotated data |
| **Supervised (Kernel-based)** | Use convolution/dependency/tree kernels; similarity between parse trees. | Captures structural info | Slow, complex |
| **Semi-Supervised — Bootstrapping** | DIPRE, Snowball — start with seed tuples, learn patterns, iterate. | Reduces annotation | Semantic drift |
| **Distant Supervision** | Auto-label training data by aligning KB facts with text (e.g., "If (Obama, born-in, Hawaii) is in KB, every sentence with Obama & Hawaii is a positive example"). | Huge data | Noisy labels |
| **Unsupervised / Open IE** | Extract relation-agnostic tuples from text — ReVerb, OLLIE, Stanford OpenIE. | Domain-independent | Relations not predefined |
| **Neural / Transformer-based** | Fine-tune BERT/RoBERTa for relation classification. | State-of-the-art | Resource-heavy |

#### 2.3.2 Distant Supervision — Formal Definition

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition:** Distant Supervision assumes that *if two entities participate in a known relation in a knowledge base, then every sentence containing those two entities expresses that relation*.

Formally, for a relation $r$ and an entity pair $(e_1, e_2) \in \mathcal{KB}_r$, the *at-least-one* hypothesis asserts:

$$
\exists \, s \in \mathcal{C}(e_1, e_2) \;\text{ such that}\; s \;\text{expresses}\; r
$$

where $\mathcal{C}(e_1, e_2)$ is the corpus context set. The MultiR / Multi-Instance models relax this to noisy label aggregation.

### 2.4 Event Extraction (EE)

> [!NOTE]
> **Event Extraction** identifies *event triggers* (verbs/nouns anchoring an event) and classifies the event type, then extracts *event arguments* (participants) and assigns argument roles. The standard framework is the **ACE (Automatic Content Extraction)** event ontology.

Components:
- **Trigger Detection** — classify the trigger word into one of 33 ACE event types.
- **Argument Extraction** — for each detected trigger, find the participant entities and assign roles (e.g., *attacker, target, instrument, victim*).
- **Event Coreference** — link event mentions across a document that refer to the same real-world event.

### 2.5 Coreference Resolution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coreference Resolution** clusters **mentions** in a document that refer to the same real-world entity. There are two flavours:
> - **Anaphora** — a pronoun/abbreviation referring back to a prior antecedent (*"Steve Jobs founded Apple. **He** later returned."*).
> - **Cataphora** — forward reference (*"When **he** arrived, John ran the meeting."*).

A coreference system typically performs: (1) mention detection, (2) mention-pair scoring, (3) clustering — using rule-based *Hobbs' algorithm*, mention-pair models, or neural end-to-end models.

### 2.6 Evaluation Metrics — The KTU Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table is the **single most important reference for solving KTU numerical/analytical questions on IE**. Memorise the formulas and the typical values reported in the literature.

| Metric | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Precision (P)** | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of all extractions made, how many are correct? |
| **Recall (R)** | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of all gold extractions, how many did we recover? |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean; the standard headline metric. |
| **Accuracy (Token-level NER)** | $A = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Used for flat sequence tagging. |
| **MUC F1** | $F_{MUC}$ | Counts minimal link edits to match gold clusters. |
| **B-CUBED F1** | $F_{B^3}$ | Per-mention precision & recall — used for coreference. |
| **CEAF F1** | $F_{CEAF}$ | Entity-alignment based — strictest coreference metric. |
| **CoNLL F1** | Average of MUC, B-CUBED, CEAF | Headline score in CoNLL shared tasks. |

Where:
- **TP (True Positive)** — correctly extracted entity/relation/event.
- **FP (False Positive)** — spuriously extracted (hallucination).
- **FN (False Negative)** — missed gold extraction.
- **TN (True Negative)** — correctly identified as non-entity (mainly in token-level NER).

### 2.7 Engineering Applications

| Domain | IE Application |
|---|---|
| **Finance** | Extract deal terms, M&A participants, financial figures from filings. |
| **Biomedical** | Extract drug–gene–disease–mutation relations from PubMed. |
| **Legal** | Extract parties, dates, monetary penalties, jurisdictions from case law. |
| **News/Media** | Real-time event detection, fact-checking, knowledge graph population. |
| **Cybersecurity** | Extract CVEs, IOCs, threat actors from threat intelligence feeds. |
| **Enterprise Search** | Power semantic search over internal documents. |
| **Voice Assistants** | Convert spoken queries into structured intent-slot frames. |

### 2.8 The KTU Cheat-Sheet — Pattern-Based vs. ML-Based IE

| Dimension | Rule/Pattern | Statistical ML | Neural/DL |
|---|---|---|---|
| Training Data | None | Annotated corpus | Large corpus (+ optional fine-tune) |
| Adaptation | Manual rewriting | Re-train | Fine-tune / prompt |
| Interpretability | **High** | Medium | **Low** |
| Recall on new entities | Poor | Good | **Best** |
| KTU Frequency | Medium | **High** | High (recent scheme) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Evaluating an NER System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Question:** "An NER system is tested on 200 sentences containing 350 gold entity mentions. The system produces 300 entity predictions, of which 250 are correct. Compute Precision, Recall, and F1-score."

### Step 1 — Identify the given values

The problem provides:

$$
TP + FP = 300 \quad \text{(total predictions made)}
$$

$$
TP = 250 \quad \text{(correct predictions)}
$$

$$
\text{Gold entities} = TP + FN = 350
$$

### Step 2 — Compute FP

$$
FP = (TP + FP) - TP = 300 - 250 = 50
$$

Therefore, the system made 50 *spurious* extractions.

### Step 3 — Compute FN

$$
FN = (TP + FN) - TP = 350 - 250 = 100
$$

Therefore, the system *missed* 100 gold entities.

### Step 4 — Compute Precision

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{250}{300} = 0.8333
$$

Hence, $P = \mathbf{83.33\%}$.

### Step 5 — Compute Recall

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{250}{350} = 0.7143
$$

Hence, $R = \mathbf{71.43\%}$.

### Step 6 — Compute F1-Score

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \times 0.8333 \times 0.7143}{0.8333 + 0.7143}
$$

$$
F_1 = \frac{1.1904}{1.5476} = 0.7692
$$

Hence, $F_1 = \mathbf{76.92\%}$.

### Step 7 — Interpretation

> The system is **moderately precise** (over-predicts cautiously) but suffers in **recall** (misses ~29% of entities). The F1 of 0.77 indicates room for improvement — typical industry-grade NER systems target $F_1 \geq 0.90$.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common IE Task Formulations

| Subtask | Input | Output | Common Model | KTU Test Probability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NER** | Tokenised sentence | Tag sequence (BIO) | CRF / BiLSTM-CRF / BERT | **Very High** |
| **Relation Extraction** | Sentence + entity pair | Relation class | SVM / Neural RE | **Very High** |
| **Event Detection** | Sentence | Trigger spans + event type | Joint NN / DyGIE++ | High |
| **Coreference** | Document | Mention clusters | Mention-ranking NN | Medium |
| **Open IE** | Sentence | Tuple list (arg1, rel, arg2) | ReVerb / OLLIE | Medium |
| **Temporal IE** | Sentence / document | Timexes + relations | HeidelTime / UWTime | Low–Medium |

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation — Viterbi for HMM-based NER

The **Viterbi algorithm** computes the most probable tag sequence $\hat{Y} = \arg\max_Y P(Y \vert X)$ in $O(n \cdot T^2)$ time, where $n$ is the sentence length and $T$ the tag-set size.

Define the *trellis* value:

$$
V_t(j) = \max_{y_1, \dots, y_{t-1}} \; P(y_1, \dots, y_{t-1}, y_t = j, x_1, \dots, x_t)
$$

Recurrence:

$$
V_t(j) = \max_{i} \Big[ V_{t-1}(i) \cdot P(y_t = j \vert y_{t-1} = i) \Big] \cdot P(x_t \vert y_t = j)
$$

Back-pointer:

$$
BP_t(j) = \arg\max_{i} \Big[ V_{t-1}(i) \cdot P(y_t = j \vert y_{t-1} = i) \Big]
$$

The optimal final state is:

$$
\hat{y}_n = \arg\max_{j} V_n(j)
$$

And tags are recovered by backtracking through the back-pointers from $\hat{y}_n$ to position $1$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Feature Templates for NER (CRF)

The CRF model relies on rich **feature functions** $f_k$. Standard templates:

| Feature | Example |
|---|---|
| Word identity | $w_i = $ "Jobs" |
| Lower-cased word | $w_i = $ "jobs" |
| Word shape | $w_i = $ "Xxxx" (capitalised) |
| Prefix / Suffix | prefix-3 = "Job", suffix-3 = "obs" |
| POS tag | $p_i = $ NNP |
| Previous tag | $y_{i-1} = $ B-PER |
| Previous previous tag | $y_{i-2} = $ O |
| Word in gazetteer | $w_i \in \text{PERSON\_LIST}$ |
| Capitalisation | $w_i$ is ALL-CAPS |
| Numeric pattern | $w_i$ matches `\d+` |
| Bigram $w_i + w_{i+1}$ | "Steve Jobs" |

### 3.5 Worked Example — BIO Tagging

**Sentence:** *"Microsoft acquired LinkedIn for 26.2 billion dollars in 2016."*

| Token | BIO Tag |
|---|---|
| Microsoft | B-ORG |
| acquired | O |
| LinkedIn | B-ORG |
| for | O |
| 26.2 | B-MONEY |
| billion | I-MONEY |
| dollars | I-MONEY |
| in | O |
| 2016 | B-DATE |
| . | O |

> A *BiLSTM-CRF* would consume the embeddings of these tokens and emit this tag sequence, with the CRF layer ensuring globally consistent labels (e.g., a `B-` must be followed by either `I-` of the same type or `O`).

### 3.6 Distant Supervision — Noise Reduction Derivation

Distant supervision labels sentences with KB relations, but introduces **noisy labels** (a sentence mentioning *Obama–Hawaii* may be about a *vacation*, not *birthplace*). The **Multi-Instance Learning** formulation treats the entity pair $(e_1, e_2)$ as a **bag of sentences** $\mathcal{B} = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_k\}$ and trains the classifier to label the bag, not individual sentences.

Objective (at-least-one):

$$
\max_\theta \sum_{(e_1,e_2)} \log P(r \vert \mathcal{B}, \theta) = \sum_{(e_1,e_2)} \log \left[ 1 - \prod_{s_i \in \mathcal{B}} \big(1 - P(r \vert s_i, \theta)\big) \right]
$$

This probabilistic OR aggregation ensures *at least one* sentence in the bag is correctly labelled by the model, reducing noise sensitivity.

### 3.7 Analytical Decision — When to Use What

| Scenario | Recommended IE Approach |
|---|---|
| Highly specialised domain, small corpus, no labels | Rule-based + Gazetteer |
| Moderate corpus, labels available | CRF with rich features |
| Large corpus, transformer-friendly | Fine-tune BERT/RoBERTa for NER / RE |
| Massive unlabelled corpus, KB available | Distant Supervision |
| Domain-independent, open relations | Open IE (ReVerb, OLLIE) |
| Streaming / real-time, low latency | Lightweight BiLSTM or rule cascade |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — End-to-End IE Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawText["A. RAW TEXT INPUT"] --> tokenise["B. TOKENISATION AND SENTENCE BOUNDARY"]
    tokenise --> posTag["C. POS TAGGING AND SHALLOW PARSE"]
    posTag --> nerStage["D. NAMED ENTITY RECOGNITION NER"]
    nerStage --> corefStage["E. COREFERENCE RESOLUTION"]
    corefStage --> reStage["F. RELATION EXTRACTION RE"]
    reStage --> eeStage["G. EVENT EXTRACTION EE"]
    eeStage --> template["H. TEMPLATE FILLING AND KNOWLEDGE BASE POPULATION"]
    template --> output["I. STRUCTURED OUTPUT JSON OR RDF OR SQL"]

    classDef stage fill:E8F0FE,stroke:1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B3D91;
    classDef input fill:FFF3CD,stroke:B58900,stroke-width:2px,color:#5C4400;
    classDef output fill:D5E8D4,stroke:2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B4D1B;
    class rawText,tokenise,posTag,nerStage,corefStage,reStage,eeStage,template input;
    class output output;
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — NER Tagging Workflow with BiLSTM-CRF

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sent["INPUT SENTENCE TOKENS"] --> embed["WORD EMBEDDING LAYER W2V OR BERT"]
    embed --> charEmb["CHARACTER LEVEL CNN OR LSTM"]
    charEmb --> concat["CONCATENATE WORD PLUS CHAR FEATURES"]
    concat --> biLSTM["BIDIRECTIONAL LSTM ENCODE FORWARD PLUS BACKWARD"]
    biLSTM --> emission["EMISSION SCORES PER TOKEN PER TAG"]
    emission --> crfLayer["CRF DECODING LAYER VITERBI OVER TAG TRANSITIONS"]
    crfLayer --> tagOut["OPTIMAL BIO TAG SEQUENCE"]

    classDef nn fill:E1F5FE,stroke:0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#01579B;
    classDef io fill:FFF8E1,stroke:FF8F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#5C4400;
    class embed,charEmb,concat,biLSTM,emission,crfLayer nn;
    class sent,tagOut io;
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Distant Supervision Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    kb["A. KNOWLEDGE BASE TRIPLES HEAD RELATION TAIL"] --> align["B. CORPUS ALIGNMENT ENTITY LINKING"]
    corpus["C. UNLABELLED TEXT CORPUS"] --> align
    align --> autoLabel["D. AUTOMATIC NOISY LABELLING"]
    autoLabel --> bag["E. BAG CONSTRUCTION SENTENCES PER ENTITY PAIR"]
    bag --> mil["F. MULTI INSTANCE NEURAL CLASSIFIER PCNN PLUS ATTENTION"]
    mil --> pred["G. RELATION PREDICTION PER BAG"]
    pred --> kbPop["H. KB POPULATION AND NOISE FILTERING"]
    kbPop --> output["I. ENRICHED KNOWLEDGE BASE"]

    classDef kb fill:EDE7F6,stroke:5E35B1,stroke-width:2px,color:#311B92;
    classDef model fill:E3F2FD,stroke:1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1;
    classDef data fill:FFF3E0,stroke:EF6C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#4E342E;
    class kb,corpus,bag,output,kbPop data;
    class align,autoLabel,mil,pred model;
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Coreference Resolution Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    doc["A. INPUT DOCUMENT"] --> md["B. MENTION DETECTION NP AND PRONOUNS"]
    md --> pairs["C. PAIRWISE MENTION CANDIDATES"]
    pairs --> score["D. SCORING FUNCTION ANTECEDENT RANKING NN OR RULE"]
    score --> anaphoric{"E. IS ANAPHORIC YES OR NO"}
    anaphoric -->|YES| link["F. LINK TO ANTECEDENT CLUSTER"]
    anaphoric -->|NO| newClus["G. CREATE NEW CLUSTER"]
    link --> cluster["H. UPDATE CLUSTERS"]
    newClus --> cluster
    cluster --> final["I. FINAL CLUSTERS EQUAL REAL WORLD ENTITIES"]
    final --> metric["J. EVALUATE MUC B CUBED CEAF CO NLL F1"]

    classDef dec fill:FFFDE7,stroke:F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#5C4400;
    classDef proc fill:E8EAF6,stroke:3949AB,stroke-width:2px,color:#1A237E;
    class anaphoric dec;
    class doc,md,pairs,score,link,newClus,cluster,final,metric proc;
```

## 4.5 Block Architecture — NER Module as a Knowledge-Grade Component

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    layer1["LAYER 1 INPUT NORMALISER RAW TO CLEAN TEXT"] --> layer2["LAYER 2 LINGUISTIC PREPROCESSOR TOKENISER POS TAGGER CHUNKER"]
    layer2 --> layer3["LAYER 3 FEATURE EXTRACTOR WORD SHAPE CAPITALISATION PREFIX SUFFIX GAZETTEER LOOKUP"]
    layer3 --> layer4["LAYER 4 STATISTICAL OR NEURAL ENCODER CRF OR BILSTM CRF OR BERT"]
    layer4 --> layer5["LAYER 5 TAG DECODER VITERBI OR CONSTRAINED DECODING"]
    layer5 --> layer6["LAYER 6 POST PROCESSOR SPAN MERGING ENTITY NORMALISATION"]
    layer6 --> layer7["LAYER 7 OUTPUT FORMATTER JSON OR CO NLL OR BRAT STANDO FF"]

    classDef L1 fill:FFEBEE,stroke:C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#7F0000;
    classDef L2 fill:E3F2FD,stroke:1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1;
    classDef L3 fill:E8F5E9,stroke:2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20;

```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Layer 4 encoder** is the focus of most KTU 2024 questions — students must be able to articulate the *mathematical* difference between an HMM encoder (generative, single-direction), a CRF encoder (discriminative, global), and a BiLSTM-CRF encoder (neural contextual + global decoding).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define Information Extraction. List and briefly explain the major subtasks of IE as per the ACE/MUC framework.**

**Model Answer:**

Information Extraction (IE) is the automatic process of converting **unstructured or semi-structured natural language text** into **structured, machine-readable knowledge** — typically entity lists, relation tuples, event frames, or knowledge-graph triples.

The major subtasks are:

1. **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** — identifying and classifying atomic entities (PERSON, ORG, LOC, DATE, MONEY).
2. **Coreference Resolution (CR)** — clustering all text spans that refer to the same real-world entity.
3. **Template Element Construction** — canonicalising coreferred entity mentions.
4. **Relation Extraction (TR)** — detecting and classifying semantic relations between entity pairs.
5. **Event Extraction (ST)** — detecting events, their triggers, and participant arguments; filling scenario templates.

> These subtasks form a *cascaded pipeline* where each stage consumes the output of the previous.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a BIO tagging scheme? Illustrate with one example sentence of at least 8 tokens containing at least one PERSON and one LOCATION entity.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **BIO (Begin, Inside, Outside)** scheme is a *token-level label encoding* used in NER. Each token is assigned one of:
> - **B-X** = the token *begins* an entity of type X.
> - **I-X** = the token is *inside* (continues) an entity of type X.
> - **O** = the token is *outside* (not part of) any entity.

**Example Sentence:** *"Prime Minister Narendra Modi visited Paris on Monday."*

| Token | Tag |
|---|---|
| Prime | O |
| Minister | O |
| Narendra | B-PER |
| Modi | I-PER |
| visited | O |
| Paris | B-LOC |
| on | O |
| Monday | B-DATE |
| . | O |

This encoding enables a sequence labeller (CRF, BiLSTM-CRF) to predict entity boundaries token-by-token.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing NER with POS tagging** — POS tags words; NER identifies *spans* of named entities.
> 2. **Forgetting the O tag** — without the O tag, a BIO encoding is incomplete and your example will be marked wrong.
> 3. **Mixing generative and discriminative models** — HMM models $P(X, Y)$ jointly; CRF models $P(Y \vert X)$ conditionally. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you conflate them.
> 4. **Skipping the Viterbi derivation** in HMM questions — you *must* show the trellis recurrence, not just state the result.
> 5. **Failing to distinguish evaluation metrics** — MUC, B-CUBED, and CEAF are *not* interchangeable for coreference. State which one you are using and why.
> 6. **Ignoring semantic drift** in bootstrapping questions — this is a *guaranteed* 1-mark loss if you do not mention it.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 4, Set A]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a **rule-based Information Extraction system**. Discuss the role of **gazetteers, regular expressions, and trigger-word patterns**, with one example for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a suitable example, derive the **HMM formulation for NER**, including transition, emission, and Viterbi decoding. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A **rule-based IE system** uses handcrafted *pattern-action rules* over linguistic and surface features to extract structured information. The architecture is modular:

1. **Pre-processing module** — tokenisation, sentence boundary detection, POS tagging, NP chunking.
2. **Gazetteer lookup module** — matches tokens against domain-specific lists (e.g., country names, company names, drug names).
3. **Pattern-matching engine** — applies regex / cascade of finite-state rules.
4. **Contextual rule application** — uses surrounding tokens to disambiguate (e.g., *Capitalised + follows "Mr." → PERSON*).
5. **Template-merging module** — combines matches into structured records.

**Examples of each component:**

- **Gazetteer** — A list `[Kerala, Bengaluru, Mumbai, Delhi, Chennai]` identifies a token like *"Kerala"* as a **LOCATION**.
- **Regular Expression** — `\d{1,2}\s(January|February|...|December)\s\d{4}` extracts a **DATE** like *"26 January 2024"*.
- **Trigger-word pattern** — `(<ORG>)\s+(acquired|merged with|bought)\s+(<ORG>)` extracts a **M&A relation** from *"Microsoft acquired LinkedIn"*.

> **Advantage:** Zero training data, fully interpretable. **Disadvantage:** Low recall on novel patterns, expensive to maintain.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**HMM Formulation for NER:**

- **Hidden states** $Y = (y_1, y_2, \dots, y_n)$ — the entity tag of each token (e.g., B-PER, I-PER, B-LOC, O).
- **Observations** $X = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ — the actual word at each position.

The joint probability factorises as:

$$
P(X, Y) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(y_i \vert y_{i-1}) \cdot P(x_i \vert y_i)
$$

- $P(y_i \vert y_{i-1})$ = **transition probability** — how often tag $y_i$ follows $y_{i-1}$. Estimated as $\dfrac{\text{count}(y_{i-1}, y_i)}{\text{count}(y_{i-1})}$ from training data.
- $P(x_i \vert y_i)$ = **emission probability** — how likely word $x_i$ is given tag $y_i$. Estimated as $\dfrac{\text{count}(y_i, x_i)}{\text{count}(y_i)}$.

**Inference via Viterbi:** Define the trellis value $V_t(j)$ as the maximum probability of being in state $j$ at time $t$ given the observations up to $t$:

$$
V_t(j) = \max_{i} \Big[ V_{t-1}(i) \cdot P(y_t = j \vert y_{i-1} = i) \Big] \cdot P(x_t \vert y_t = j)
$$

$$
V_1(j) = P(y_1 = j) \cdot P(x_1 \vert y_1 = j)
$$

A back-pointer records the argmax for reconstruction:

$$
BP_t(j) = \arg\max_{i} \Big[ V_{t-1}(i) \cdot P(y_t = j \vert y_{t-1} = i) \Big]
$$

The optimal final state is $\hat{y}_n = \arg\max_j V_n(j)$, and we backtrack using $BP$ to retrieve the full tag sequence. **Time complexity:** $O(n T^2)$, where $T = $ number of tags.

**Worked Mini-Example** — Sentence *"John works at Google"*, tags $\{B\text{-}PER, O, B\text{-}ORG\}$:

Assuming transition $P(O \to B\text{-}ORG) = 0.05$, emission $P(\text{"Google"} \vert B\text{-}ORG) = 0.4$, the contribution of position 3 to the trellis is:

$$
V_3(B\text{-}ORG) = \max_i \big[ V_2(i) \cdot P(B\text{-}ORG \vert i) \big] \cdot 0.4
$$

The Viterbi path will recover the tag sequence $\langle B\text{-}PER, O, B\text{-}ORG \rangle$ with maximum joint probability.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 4, Set B]**

**(a)** Discuss the **Conditional Random Field (CRF)** model for NER. Compare it with **HMM** and explain why CRF generally gives better performance. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An NER system was tested on a benchmark. The system made **400 predictions**, of which **280 were correct**, and there were **350 gold entity mentions** in the test set. Compute **Precision, Recall, and F1-score**, and interpret the result. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Conditional Random Field (CRF) for NER:**

A CRF is a **discriminative, globally-normalised** sequence model that directly models $P(Y \vert X)$:

$$
P(Y \vert X) = \frac{1}{Z(X)} \exp\!\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{k} \lambda_k \, f_k(y_{i-1}, y_i, X, i) \right)
$$

Where:
- $f_k$ = feature function depending on the *current and previous* tag and the *entire observation sequence*.
- $\lambda_k$ = learned weight for feature $k$.
- $Z(X)$ = partition function that sums over **all** possible tag sequences:

$$
Z(X) = \sum_{Y'} \exp\!\left( \sum_{i,k} \lambda_k f_k(y'_{i-1}, y'_i, X, i) \right)
$$

**CRF vs HMM — Comparative Analysis:**

| Dimension | HMM | CRF |
|---|---|---|
| **Model type** | Generative (joint) | Discriminative (conditional) |
| **Probability** | $P(X, Y)$ | $P(Y \vert X)$ |
| **Independence assumption** | Strong (emission depends only on current tag) | No emission-independence assumption |
| **Feature support** | Limited to identity of current word | Arbitrary, overlapping, non-independent features (shape, prefix, gazetteer) |
| **Global normalisation** | No | Yes — avoids label-bias of MEMMs |
| **Training** | Counts + smoothing | Numerical optimisation (L-BFGS, SGD) |
| **Inference** | Viterbi | Viterbi on CRF potentials |
| **Performance** | Lower F1 on rich-feature tasks | **Higher F1**, especially on noisy text |

**Why CRF wins:** Because it does not need to model the observation distribution $P(X)$, it can use **rich, overlapping features** (word shape, affixes, POS, context window) without paying the cost of modelling their joint distribution. Global normalisation avoids the *label-bias problem* suffered by MEMMs.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Total predictions: $TP + FP = 400$
- Correct predictions: $TP = 280$
- Gold entities: $TP + FN = 350$

**Step 1 — Compute FP:**

$$
FP = (TP + FP) - TP = 400 - 280 = 120
$$

**Step 2 — Compute FN:**

$$
FN = (TP + FN) - TP = 350 - 280 = 70
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Precision:**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{280}{400} = 0.70 = \mathbf{70\%}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Recall:**

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{280}{350} = 0.80 = \mathbf{80\%}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute F1-Score:**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \times 0.70 \times 0.80}{0.70 + 0.80} = \frac{1.12}{1.50} = 0.7467
$$

$$
F_1 \approx \mathbf{74.67\%}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

- The system is **recall-biased** ($R > P$) — it over-predicts entities (120 false positives) but captures more of the gold set.
- An F1 of **0.7467** is *acceptable but below* the 0.90+ SOTA benchmarks (e.g., CoNLL-2003 NER English test set SOTA $\approx 0.94$).
- **Recommendation:** Apply post-processing (gazetteer filtering) to suppress the 120 false positives and re-evaluate.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for IE — KTU 2024:**

- **IE = unstructured text → structured knowledge.** Always state this canonical definition first in long-answer questions.
- **Subtask hierarchy:** NER → Coreference → Relation Extraction → Event Extraction → Template Filling. Know the order.
- **BIO tagging is mandatory** — practice encoding at least 3 multi-entity sentences (PER + ORG + LOC + DATE).
- **HMM vs CRF vs BiLSTM-CRF** — the *trinity* of NER modelling. Memorise the joint, conditional, and neural formulations.
- **Viterbi algorithm** is the inference engine for both HMM and linear-chain CRF. Know the trellis recurrence and back-pointer.
- **Precision / Recall / F1** are the only metrics used for IE evaluation in KTU papers. Practice 3 numerical problems.
- **Distant supervision** = KB-aligned, auto-labelled, noisy training data. Always mention the *multi-instance bag* formulation in long answers.
- **Bootstrapping** suffers from **semantic drift** — explicit mention gets full marks.
- **Open IE (ReVerb, OLLIE)** extracts relation-agnostic tuples, unlike supervised RE which is relation-class-bounded.
- **ACE event ontology** has **33 event types** and **35 argument roles** — be ready to explain trigger detection and argument role labelling.
- **Coreference metrics:** MUC, B-CUBED, CEAF — their *CoNLL-F1 average* is the official headline score.
- **Engineering applications** (biomedical, legal, finance, news) — frame at least one answer with a real-world example for higher marks.
- **Transition vs emission probabilities** in HMM are computed as *normalised counts* — examiners will deduct marks if you write the formula but skip the *normalisation* denominator.
- **Feature templates** in CRF are exhaustive — word shape, prefix/suffix, capitalisation, gazetteer, POS, bigrams. The more the better.
- **CRF avoids the label-bias problem of MEMMs** by **global normalisation** — the *Z(X) partition function* is the technical reason.
- **Distant supervision noise reduction** is best handled by **multi-instance learning** — the *at-least-one* hypothesis is the key.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "I.E. = N-C-R-E-T":**
> **N**amed entities, **C**oreference, **R**elations, **E**vents, **T**emplates.
> This is the canonical 5-step IE pipeline tested in KTU 2024 Scheme papers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
