```mermaid
    A[Encountered Token or Phrase] --> B{Is it in Gazetteer?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Multiple Entries?}
    B -- No --> D[Apply ML Model]
    C -- No --> E[Assign Single Type]
    C -- Yes --> F[Analyze Context Window]
    F --> G[Check Surrounding Words]
    G --> H[Apply Disambiguation Heuristic]
    H --> I[Final Entity Type]
    D --> J[CRF plus Embedding Score]
    J --> K[Highest Probability Class]
    K --> I
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style I fill:#e1ffe1
```

**Interpretation:** When ambiguity arises (multiple possible types for a token), the system consults the **context window** and combines signals from gazetteers, embeddings, and sequence models to resolve the correct entity type.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Named Entity Recognition. List the standard entity categories used in NER.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Named Entity Recognition (NER) is a sub-task of Information Extraction in NLP that involves identifying and classifying named entities in text into predefined categories.

**Standard entity categories:**
1. **PER** — Person names (e.g., "Narendra Modi")
2. **ORG** — Organizations (e.g., "Infosys")
3. **LOC** — Locations (e.g., "Kerala")
4. **DATE/TIME** — Temporal expressions (e.g., "15 August 2024")
5. **MONEY** — Monetary values (e.g., "₹1000")
6. **PERCENT** — Percentages (e.g., "85%")
7. **MISC** — Miscellaneous named entities

NER is typically formulated as a **sequence labeling** problem using the **BIO tagging scheme** (Beginning, Inside, Outside).

---

### **Question 2: What is the BIO tagging scheme in NER? Illustrate with an example.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **BIO tagging scheme** is a token-level annotation format used in NER:

- **B-X** — Beginning token of an entity of type X
- **I-X** — Inside (continuation) token of an entity of type X
- **O** — Outside any entity (a common word)

**Example:** *"Rahul works at TCS in Mumbai."*

| Token | Tag |
|-------|-----|
| Rahul | B-PER |
| works | O |
| at | O |
| TCS | B-ORG |
| in | O |
| Mumbai | B-LOC |

The BIO scheme enables a model to learn **boundary detection** (where an entity starts and ends) and **type classification** simultaneously.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A: Explain the Conditional Random Field (CRF) model for Named Entity Recognition. Discuss its advantages over Hidden Markov Models (HMM).**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **[14 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

### (a) CRF Architecture and Mathematical Formulation [7 Marks]

A **Conditional Random Field (CRF)** is a discriminative probabilistic model used for structured prediction tasks like NER. Unlike generative models (HMMs), CRFs model the **conditional probability** $P(Y \mid X)$ directly, where $X$ is the input token sequence and $Y$ is the corresponding label sequence.

For a sentence $X = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ and a label sequence $Y = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_n)$, the CRF defines an unnormalized score:

$$
s(X, Y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E(x_i, y_i) + \sum_{i=2}^{n} T(y_{i-1}, y_i)
$$

where $E(x_i, y_i)$ is the **emission score** (how well token $x_i$ fits label $y_i$) and $T(y_{i-1}, y_i)$ is the **transition score** (how likely one label follows another).

The conditional probability is obtained by normalizing over all possible label sequences using the **partition function**:

$$
P(Y \mid X) = \frac{\exp(s(X, Y))}{\sum_{Y' \in \mathcal{Y}_X} \exp(s(X, Y'))}
$$

**Training** is performed by maximizing the log-likelihood of correct label sequences in the training data. The gradient has a clean interpretation: the difference between empirical and expected feature counts.

**Inference** is done using the **Viterbi algorithm**, a dynamic programming approach that finds the highest-scoring label sequence in $O(n \cdot \vert \mathcal{L} \vert^2)$ time.

### (b) Advantages of CRF over HMM [7 Marks]

| Aspect | HMM | CRF |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Model Type** | Generative: models $P(X, Y)$ | Discriminative: models $P(Y \mid X)$ |
| **Feature Richness** | Limited to emission probabilities | Can use arbitrary, overlapping features |
| **Observation Dependence** | Observations conditionally independent given state | No independence assumption on observations |
| **Label Bias** | No explicit label bias | Avoids label bias problem of MEMMs |
| **Global Normalization** | Local (per-state) | Global (over entire sequence) |
| **Performance on NER** | Lower F1 | State-of-the-art among traditional ML |

**Key reasons CRF wins on NER:**
1. **Arbitrary features:** CRF can use word shape, capitalization, prefix/suffix, surrounding word context — HMM cannot easily use such rich features.
2. **Avoids independence assumption:** HMM assumes observations are conditionally independent given the state, which is unrealistic for language.
3. **Enforces valid label transitions:** The transition matrix naturally prevents invalid sequences like `B-PER → I-LOC`.
4. **Better F1 scores:** On CoNLL-03, CRFs achieve ~88 F1 vs ~77 F1 for HMMs.

**Conclusion:** CRFs were the gold standard for NER before the deep learning era, and BiLSTM-CRF hybrid models remain competitive for resource-constrained settings.

---

### **Question B: Discuss the various types of ambiguities encountered in Named Entity Recognition with suitable examples. Explain how modern systems handle them.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **[14 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

### (a) Types of Ambiguities in NER [7 Marks]

Ambiguity is the **central challenge** in NER. The same surface form can belong to different entity types, span different boundaries, or even not be an entity at all. The major ambiguity types are:

**1. Type Ambiguity (Semantic Ambiguity):**
The same word can refer to different entity types depending on context.
- *"Washington"* can be a **Person** (George Washington), a **Location** (Washington D.C.), or an **Organization** (Washington Post).
- *"Apple"* can be a **Person** (Apple the singer? rare) or an **Organization** (Apple Inc.) or a **Product** (Apple iPhone).

**2. Boundary Ambiguity:**
Disagreement on where an entity starts or ends.
- *"The University of Kerala"* — Does the entity include "The"? "University of Kerala"?
- *"New York City Hall"* — Is this LOC + LOC or a single ORG?

**3. Named Entity vs. Common Noun Ambiguity:**
Some words are named entities in one context and common nouns in another.
- *"I had an apple"* (common noun, **O** tag)
- *"Apple released a new iPhone"* (**B-ORG**)

**4. Entity Type Confusion Across Languages:**
- *"Sachin"* in English typically refers to a person; in some Indian languages, "Sachin" could be a name, but the script and context differ.

**5. Coreference / Pronoun Ambiguity:**
- *"He said Microsoft will…"* — "He" refers back to some person entity.

**6. Nested Entity Ambiguity:**
One entity is embedded inside another.
- *"University of Mumbai"* — "Mumbai" (LOC) is nested inside an organization name.

**7. Metonymy:**
- *"Paris agreed to the treaty"* — "Paris" refers to the French government (ORG), not the city (LOC).

**8. Abbreviation / Alias Ambiguity:**
- *"UN"* can refer to the United Nations (ORG) or a university in some contexts.

### (b) How Modern Systems Handle Ambiguity [7 Marks]

Modern NER systems address ambiguity through several mechanisms:

**1. Contextual Word Embeddings:**
Models like **BERT, ELMo, and RoBERTa** generate different vector representations for the same word depending on surrounding context. So "Washington" in a financial context (Washington Post) gets a different embedding than in a political geography context (Washington D.C.). This solves type ambiguity elegantly.

**2. Sequence Context Modeling:**
**BiLSTM** architectures process the sentence in both directions, so when classifying token $x_i$, the model has seen both the left context $(x_1, \ldots, x_{i-1})$ and the right context $(x_{i+1}, \ldots, x_n)$. This resolves boundary ambiguity.

**3. CRF Transition Constraints:**
The CRF layer's transition matrix encodes rules like "B-PER can be followed by I-PER or O, but not B-LOC". This prevents invalid entity sequences.

**4. Gazetteer Disambiguation with Tie-Breaking:**
When a gazetteer lists "Washington" as both a person and a location, the system uses contextual cues (e.g., preceding verb "live in" → LOC).

**5. Multi-Task Learning:**
Joint training of NER with **POS tagging, dependency parsing, and coreference resolution** provides auxiliary signals that disambiguate entity types.

**6. External Knowledge Integration:**
Linking predicted entities to **knowledge bases** (Wikidata, DBpedia) helps resolve type ambiguity by grounding the entity to a canonical type.

**7. Active Learning for Ambiguous Cases:**
Human annotators review low-confidence predictions, and the system is retrained on these hard cases iteratively.

**Conclusion:** Ambiguity is not fully solved — it remains an active research area — but contextual embeddings combined with sequence labeling constraints have reduced ambiguity-related errors by 40–50% in modern benchmarks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in NER questions (leading to 2–4 mark deductions):**
>
> 1. **Confusing token-level and entity-level evaluation.** Always report Precision/Recall/F1 at the **entity level** (spans), not the token level, unless the question specifically asks for token-level.
> 2. **Forgetting the CRF partition function.** Many students write the numerator $e^{s(X,Y)}$ but forget to divide by $Z(X)$. The full conditional probability is **mandatory** for full marks.
> 3. **Not distinguishing BIO from BIOES.** Examiners will deduct marks if BIOES is presented as "the" standard when the question asks specifically about BIO. State both and clarify.
> 4. **Confusing HMM and CRF.** HMM is **generative**; CRF is **discriminative**. Mixing them up is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss.
> 5. **Writing definitions of NER without examples.** Always pair definitions with at least one concrete example.
> 6. **Forgetting to list ambiguity types explicitly.** A 14-mark question on ambiguity expects at least **5–6 distinct types** with examples. Just mentioning "contextual ambiguity" is insufficient.
> 7. **Not stating assumptions.** When writing the CRF objective, mention that $T$ is the transition score matrix and $E$ is the emission score.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — NER & Ambiguity**

### Core Concepts to Memorize
- ✅ NER is a **sequence labeling task** under **Information Extraction**.
- ✅ Standard entity types: **PER, ORG, LOC, DATE, TIME, MONEY, PERCENT, MISC**.
- ✅ **BIO scheme**: B-X (begin), I-X (inside), O (outside). Extended: **BIOES** (adds E-end, S-single).
- ✅ NER is a token-to-tag mapping: $f: X \rightarrow Y$, where $Y^* = \arg\max_{Y} P(Y \mid X)$.

### Model Families
- ✅ **Rule-based:** Gazetteers, regex, handcrafted patterns.
- ✅ **ML-based:** HMM, MEMM, CRF (CRF is the gold-standard traditional ML model).
- ✅ **DL-based:** BiLSTM-CRF, CNN-CRF, Transformer (BERT), LLM prompting.

### CRF Essentials
- ✅ **Discriminative** model — models $P(Y \mid X)$ directly.
- ✅ Score function: $s(X, Y) = \sum E(x_i, y_i) + \sum T(y_{i-1}, y_i)$.
- ✅ Normalization: $P(Y \mid X) = \frac{e^{s(X,Y)}}{Z(X)}$.
- ✅ Inference: **Viterbi decoding** in $O(n \cdot \vert \mathcal{L} \vert^2)$.
- ✅ Advantages over HMM: arbitrary features, no independence assumption, global normalization.

### Evaluation Metrics
- ✅ **Precision** $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ — exact entity span + type match.
- ✅ **Recall** $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$.
- ✅ **F1-Score** $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$.
- ✅ Evaluation is **strict** at the **entity level**, not the token level.

### Ambiguity in NER
- ✅ **Type ambiguity:** Same word, different entity types (Washington = PER/LOC/ORG).
- ✅ **Boundary ambiguity:** Disagreement on entity span.
- ✅ **NE vs Common Noun:** "Apple" the fruit vs. "Apple" the company.
- ✅ **Nested entities:** One entity inside another.
- ✅ **Metonymy:** "Paris" = the city OR the government.
- ✅ **Resolution methods:** Contextual embeddings (BERT), BiLSTM context, CRF constraints, gazetteer disambiguation, knowledge base linking, multi-task learning.

### Must-Remember Equations
- ✅ CRF conditional probability with partition function.
- ✅ Viterbi recursion: $\delta_t(j) = \max_i \left[ \delta_{t-1}(i) + T_{i,j} + E_{x_t, j} \right]$.
- ✅ F1 formula in both forms (harmonic mean and TP/FP/FN form).

> [!NOTE]
> **One-liner to remember:** *"NER is a sequence labeling task that identifies entity boundaries and types; CRF globally models label dependencies; BERT-style contextual embeddings largely resolve type ambiguity; F1 is the standard evaluation metric."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NER as Sequence Labeling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT742 - NATURAL LANGAUGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** NER as Sequence Labeling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Named Entity Recognition (NER) as Sequence Labeling** is the formulation of the entity-extraction task as a *structured prediction problem* in which every token $w_i$ in an input sentence $\mathbf{w} = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)$ is mapped to a discrete tag $y_i$ drawn from a predefined tag set $\mathcal{Y}$. The joint output $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2, \dots, y_n)$ is the *tag sequence*, and the model learns the conditional distribution $P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{w})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sequence Labeling Paradigm (KTU Definition):** NER is *not* solved token-by-token independently. The model leverages **inter-token dependencies** (e.g., an `I-PER` tag is only valid after a `B-PER` tag) to produce globally consistent predictions. This is the defining characteristic that distinguishes sequence labeling from simple classification.

### 1.2 Common Tagging Schemes

| Scheme | Tag Set Notation | Granularity | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IO** | `I-PER`, `O` | Coarse | Simplest baseline |
| **BIO** | `B-PER`, `I-PER`, `O` | Standard | Industry standard for CoNLL-2003 |
| **BIOES** | `B-PER`, `I-PER`, `E-PER`, `S-PER`, `O` | Fine-grained | Best F1, used in state-of-the-art systems |

Where:
- `B-X` = **Begin** of entity of type X
- `I-X` = **Inside** (continuation) of entity of type X
- `E-X` = **End** of entity of type X
- `S-X` = **Single**-token entity of type X
- `O` = **Outside** any named entity

> [!NOTE]
> For **standard KTU examinations**, the **BIO scheme** is the most commonly tested tagging format.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bookmark Insertion" Intuition

Imagine you are reading a 500-page novel and your professor hands you **80 colored sticky notes**:
- **Red notes** mark the beginning of every character's name.
- **Blue notes** mark the continuation of a name that spans multiple words.
- **Yellow notes** mark ordinary words that are not names.

You flip through the novel and place exactly one sticky note on *every single page*. The challenge is: you cannot decide what note to put on page 247 without looking at what note you placed on page 246. This dependency between consecutive page notes is exactly what makes NER a **sequence labeling** problem rather than a simple per-token classification problem.

**The Sequence Labeling Problem** is therefore solved using models that explicitly encode inter-tag dependencies:
1. **Hidden Markov Models (HMM)** — generative, uses independence assumptions.
2. **Maximum Entropy Markov Models (MEMM)** — discriminative, fixes label bias.
3. **Conditional Random Fields (CRF)** — discriminative, globally normalized, the KTU-preferred approach.
4. **BiLSTM-CRF / BERT-CRF** — neural variants, current state-of-the-art.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** NER as sequence labeling is the foundational building block for downstream applications like **Information Extraction**, **Question Answering**, **Machine Translation**, **Biomedical Text Mining** (extracting drug/protein names), and **Resume Parsing** in HR-tech platforms.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Problem Formulation

Given a token sequence $\mathbf{w} = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)$ and a tag-set $\mathcal{Y}$, sequence labeling seeks:

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \arg\max_{\mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{Y}^n} \; P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{w})$$

### 2.2 Operational Logic Steps — NER Pipeline

1. **Tokenization:** Split raw text into tokens using whitespace + punctuation rules.
2. **Feature Extraction:** For each token $w_i$, construct a feature vector $\mathbf{f}_i$ that includes:
   - **Word-level features:** $w_i$, $w_{i-1}$, $w_{i+1}$, lowercased form, lemma.
   - **Orthographic features:** capitalization pattern (e.g., `AllCaps`, `InitCap`, `lowercase`), digit patterns.
   - **Affix features:** prefixes/suffixes of length 2–4.
   - **Gazetteer features:** membership in known entity lists (countries, organizations).
   - **Context features:** bigrams, trigrams, surrounding Part-of-Speech tags.
3. **Sequence Scoring:** Compute a score for every possible tag sequence using the model.
4. **Viterbi Decoding:** Use dynamic programming to find the globally optimal tag sequence in $O(n \cdot \vert \mathcal{Y} \vert^2)$ time.
5. **Entity Extraction:** Merge consecutive `B-X` / `I-X` tags into entity spans.

### 2.3 Hidden Markov Model (HMM) Formulation

Under the Markov assumption, HMM models:

$$P(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{w}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(y_i \mid y_{i-1}) \cdot P(w_i \mid y_i)$$

- **Transition probability** $P(y_i \mid y_{i-1})$: likelihood of moving from tag $y_{i-1}$ to $y_i$.
- **Emission probability** $P(w_i \mid y_i)$: likelihood that token $w_i$ is generated by tag $y_i$.

Using Bayes' rule:

$$P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{w}) \propto \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(y_i \mid y_{i-1}) \cdot P(w_i \mid y_i)$$

### 2.4 Conditional Random Field (CRF) — Linear-Chain CRF

The CRF computes a *globally normalized* conditional probability:

$$P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{Z(\mathbf{w})} \exp\!\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{k} \lambda_k \, f_k(y_{i-1}, y_i, \mathbf{w}, i) \right)$$

Where the partition function ensures valid probability:

$$Z(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_{\mathbf{y}^{\prime} \in \mathcal{Y}^n} \exp\!\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{k} \lambda_k \, f_k(y^{\prime}_{i-1}, y^{\prime}_i, \mathbf{w}, i) \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CRF is superior to HMM/MEMM:** CRF does **not** suffer from the *label bias problem* because the global partition function $Z(\mathbf{w})$ considers *all* possible tag sequences. MEMM normalizes locally per token, biasing predictions toward states with few successors.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Definition | Purpose / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sequence labeling objective | $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \arg\max_{\mathbf{y}} P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{w})$ | Predict optimal tag sequence |
| 2 | HMM joint probability | $P(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{w}) = \prod_i P(y_i \mid y_{i-1}) P(w_i \mid y_i)$ | Generative modeling of tag-token pairs |
| 3 | Linear-chain CRF | $P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{w}) = \dfrac{1}{Z(\mathbf{w})} \exp\!\left(\sum_i \sum_k \lambda_k f_k\right)$ | Discriminative sequence labeling (KTU-favorite) |
| 4 | Partition function | $Z(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_{\mathbf{y}^{\prime}} \exp\!\left(\sum_i \sum_k \lambda_k f_k\right)$ | Global normalization constant |
| 5 | Precision | $\text{P} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Fraction of predicted entities that are correct |
| 6 | Recall | $\text{R} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Fraction of true entities that are recovered |
| 7 | F1-score | $\text{F}_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall |
| 8 | Viterbi complexity | $O(n \cdot \vert \mathcal{Y} \vert^2)$ | Decoding time for length-$n$ sequence |
| 9 | BIO tag count | $\vert \mathcal{Y} \vert = 2K + 1$ | For $K$ entity types (B-X, I-X, O) |
| 10 | BIOES tag count | $\vert \mathcal{Y} \vert = 4K + 1$ | For $K$ entity types (B, I, E, S, O) |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search Engines (Google/Bing):** Highlight entities in knowledge panels.
- **Clinical NLP (BioBERT):** Extract drug–disease–gene triplets from electronic health records.
- **Financial NLP:** Detect company names and ticker symbols in earnings call transcripts.
- **Voice Assistants (Siri/Alexa):** Parse commands like *"Book a flight to Paris next Monday"* — "Paris" is tagged `B-LOC`, "next Monday" is tagged `B-DATE`.
- **Resume Parsing:** Identify candidate skills, education institutions, and prior employers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes' Theorem to the HMM Decoding Rule

We start from the formal sequence-labeling objective and derive the HMM-specific decoding rule used in NER.

**Step 1 — Posterior via Bayes' Theorem:**

$$P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{w}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{w} \mid \mathbf{y}) \, P(\mathbf{y})}{P(\mathbf{w})}$$

The denominator $P(\mathbf{w})$ is constant with respect to $\mathbf{y}$, so it can be dropped during $\arg\max$:

$$P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{w}) \propto P(\mathbf{w} \mid \mathbf{y}) \, P(\mathbf{y})$$

**Step 2 — Apply Markov Assumption to Tag Sequence:**

The first-order Markov assumption states that $P(y_i \mid y_1, \dots, y_{i-1}) = P(y_i \mid y_{i-1})$. Therefore:

$$P(\mathbf{y}) = P(y_1) \prod_{i=2}^{n} P(y_i \mid y_{i-1})$$

**Step 3 — Apply Output-Independence Assumption to Emissions:**

HMMs assume each token is conditionally independent of other tokens given its tag:

$$P(\mathbf{w} \mid \mathbf{y}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid y_i)$$

**Step 4 — Combine Into Joint Probability:**

$$P(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{w}) = P(y_1) \prod_{i=2}^{n} P(y_i \mid y_{i-1}) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid y_i)$$

**Step 5 — Take Logarithm for Numerical Stability:**

$$\log P(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{w}) = \log P(y_1) + \sum_{i=2}^{n} \log P(y_i \mid y_{i-1}) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \mid y_i)$$

**Step 6 — Apply Viterbi Decoding:**

Define the Viterbi variable $\delta_i(y)$ as the max log-probability of any path ending in tag $y$ at position $i$:

$$\delta_i(y) = \max_{y^{\prime}} \left[ \delta_{i-1}(y^{\prime}) + \log P(y \mid y^{\prime}) + \log P(w_i \mid y) \right]$$

This yields an $O(n \cdot \vert \mathcal{Y} \vert^2)$ decoding algorithm, which is what makes HMM tractable for NER.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — BIO Conversion

**Sentence:** *"Apple Inc. was founded by Steve Jobs in California."*

**Tokens & BIO Tags:**

| Token | BIO Tag | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| Apple | `B-ORG` | Start of organization name |
| Inc. | `I-ORG` | Continuation of same organization |
| was | `O` | Outside any entity |
| founded | `O` | Outside any entity |
| by | `O` | Outside any entity |
| Steve | `B-PER` | Start of person name |
| Jobs | `I-PER` | Continuation of person name |
| in | `O` | Outside any entity |
| California | `B-LOC` | Start of location |

**Step A — Identify Entity Spans:**
- `B-ORG` → `I-ORG` ⇒ *Apple Inc.* (Organization)
- `B-PER` → `I-PER` ⇒ *Steve Jobs* (Person)
- `B-LOC` ⇒ *California* (Location)

**Step B — Convert to BIOES Equivalent:**

| Token | BIO Tag | BIOES Tag |
|---|---|---|
| Apple | `B-ORG` | `B-ORG` |
| Inc. | `I-ORG` | `E-ORG` (end of multi-token org) |
| Steve | `B-PER` | `B-PER` |
| Jobs | `I-PER` | `E-PER` (end of multi-token person) |
| California | `B-LOC` | `S-LOC` (single-token location) |

This conversion demonstrates the transition logic of BIOES: a `B-X` followed by `I-X` becomes `E-X` at the last token; a lone `B-X` becomes `S-X`.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Feature Engineering for NER Sequence Labeling

| Feature Type | Concrete Example | Tag Influence | KTU Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Word Identity** | $w_i = \text{"Apple"}$ | Strong indicator of `B-ORG` | High |
| **Capitalization** | `InitCap` → `B-PER`, `B-LOC` | First-letter capitalization signals entities | High |
| **All Caps** | `"IBM"`, `"UN"` | Strong `B-ORG` indicator | High |
| **Digits Pattern** | `"2024"`, `"19%"` | Triggers `B-DATE`, `B-PERCENT` | Medium |
| **Prefix** | `pre-2("un")` → "United" | Lowercase function-word test | Medium |
| **Suffix** | `suf-3("ton")` → "Washington" | Geographic name detector | Medium |
| **Gazetteer Match** | $w_i \in$ ListOfCountries | Boosts `B-LOC` probability | Very High |
| **POS Tag** | `NNP` (proper noun singular) | Almost certain entity trigger | High |
| **Word Shape** | `Xx+` → `Apple`, `XXX` → `IBM` | Captures capitalization patterns | Medium |
| **Bigram Context** | $w_{i-1} = \text{"Mr."}, w_i = \text{"Smith"}$ | Strong `B-PER` boost | High |

## 3.4 Validation Rule for BIO Tag Sequences (Strict)

A BIO tag sequence $\mathbf{y}$ is **valid** iff for every position $i$:

$$\left[ y_i = \text{I-X} \right] \implies \left[ y_{i-1} = \text{B-X} \;\lor\; y_{i-1} = \text{I-X} \right]$$

In words: an `I-X` tag is only legal if it is preceded by either a `B-X` or another `I-X` of the **same type X**. This constraint prevents illegal sequences such as `O, I-PER` (an `I-PER` cannot follow `O`).

For BIOES, additional rules apply:
- `B-X` must be followed by `I-X` (and ultimately `E-X`).
- `S-X` must be a singleton (no following `I-X` of same type).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NER as Sequence Labeling — End-to-End Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputDoc["Raw Text Document"] --> tokMod["Tokenizer Module"]
    tokMod --> t1["Token w1"]
    tokMod --> t2["Token w2"]
    tokMod --> t3["Token wi"]
    tokMod --> tN["Token wn"]
    t1 --> featEng["Feature Engineering Layer"]
    t2 --> featEng
    t3 --> featEng
    tN --> featEng
    featEng --> fVec["Feature Vectors f1 to fn"]
    fVec --> seqModel["Sequence Labeling Model HMM or CRF or BiLSTM CRF"]
    seqModel --> viterbi["Viterbi Decoder argmax over tag sequences"]
    viterbi --> tagSeq["Predicted Tag Sequence y1 to yn"]
    tagSeq --> spanMerge["Span Merging Layer"]
    spanMerge --> entities["Extracted Named Entities"]
    entities --> output1["ORG Apple Inc"]
    entities --> output2["PER Steve Jobs"]
    entities --> output3["LOC California"]
```

## 4.2 State Diagram — BIO Transition Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> O
    O --> O: Outside word
    O --> B_PER: Start Person
    O --> B_LOC: Start Location
    O --> B_ORG: Start Organization
    B_PER --> I_PER: Continue Person
    I_PER --> I_PER: Continue Person
    I_PER --> E_PER: End Person
    B_PER --> E_PER: Single boundary
    B_LOC --> I_LOC: Continue Location
    I_LOC --> E_LOC: End Location
    B_ORG --> I_ORG: Continue Organization
    I_ORG --> E_ORG: End Organization
    E_PER --> O: Outside
    E_LOC --> O: Outside
    E_ORG --> O: Outside
    I_PER --> B_LOC: New entity start
    note right of I_PER: Illegal transition I_PER then O is blocked
```

## 4.3 CRF Feature Function Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    obs["Observation Window w(i-1), wi, w(i+1)"] --> unigram["Unigram Feature f1 yi, wi"]
    obs --> bigram["Bigram Feature f2 y(i-1), yi"]
    bigram --> pairPot["Pairwise Potential Lambda f2"]
    unigram --> nodePot["Node Potential Lambda f1"]
    nodePot --> score["Total Score per Sequence"]
    pairPot --> score
    score --> normalize["Normalize by Z(w)"]
    normalize --> prob["Conditional Probability P(y pipe w)"]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    blockA["Input Layer Token Embeddings"] --> blockB["Contextual Encoder BiLSTM or BERT"]
    blockB --> blockC["Emission Score Matrix n by Y"]
    blockC --> blockD["Transition Score Matrix Y by Y"]
    blockD --> blockE["CRF Layer Global Normalization"]
    blockE --> blockF["Viterbi Decoder Optimal Path"]
    blockF --> blockG["Output Layer BIO Tag Sequence"]
    blockG --> blockH["Post-Processing Entity Span Extraction"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip:** The CRF Layer in **blockE** is what makes this architecture strictly superior to a vanilla BiLSTM. The transition matrix $T \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{Y} \vert \times \vert \mathcal{Y} \vert}$ encodes *legal* tag transitions learned from training data, ensuring the model never produces illegal sequences like `O → I-PER`.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Named Entity Recognition as a sequence labeling task. List any three common entity types used in standard NER systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Named Entity Recognition (NER) is a sequence labeling task in which every token $w_i$ in an input sentence is assigned a tag $y_i$ from a predefined tag set $\mathcal{Y}$, producing a tag sequence $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, \dots, y_n)$ that marks entity boundaries and types.

Three common entity types:
1. **PER** — Person (e.g., *Steve Jobs*)
2. **LOC** — Location (e.g., *California*)
3. **ORG** — Organization (e.g., *Apple Inc.*)

Additional types frequently used: `MISC` (miscellaneous), `DATE`, `TIME`, `MONEY`, `PERCENT`.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between BIO and BIOES tagging schemes with one example sentence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | BIO | BIOES |
|---|---|---|
| Begin tag | `B-X` | `B-X` |
| Inside tag | `I-X` | `I-X` |
| End tag | Same as `I-X` | `E-X` |
| Single token | `B-X` | `S-X` |
| Granularity | Coarser | Finer |

**Example:** *"Sundar Pichai leads Google."*

| Token | BIO | BIOES |
|---|---|---|
| Sundar | `B-PER` | `B-PER` |
| Pichai | `I-PER` | `E-PER` |
| Google | `B-ORG` | `S-ORG` |

BIOES is more expressive but increases the tag-set size from $2K+1$ to $4K+1$ for $K$ entity types.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the Hidden Markov Model formulation for NER as sequence labeling. Derive the Viterbi decoding rule and compute its time complexity. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Given the sentence *"Barack Obama visited New Delhi in 2015"* with the BIO-tagged sequence: `B-PER, I-PER, O, B-LOC, I-LOC, O, B-DATE`. Convert this to BIOES tagging and extract the entity spans. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) HMM Formulation for NER (7 marks):**

An HMM defines a joint distribution over tag sequence $\mathbf{y}$ and word sequence $\mathbf{w}$ using two independence assumptions:

$$P(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{w}) = P(y_1) \prod_{i=2}^{n} P(y_i \mid y_{i-1}) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid y_i)$$

- **Transition probability** $P(y_i \mid y_{i-1})$: Encodes tag-tag dependencies (e.g., `B-PER → I-PER` is likely, `O → I-PER` is illegal).
- **Emission probability** $P(w_i \mid y_i)$: Encodes how likely a token is generated by a given tag.
- **Initial probability** $P(y_1)$: Probability distribution over starting tags.

**Viterbi Decoding Derivation:**

Define $\delta_i(y)$ as the maximum log-probability of any tag sequence ending in tag $y$ at position $i$:

$$\delta_i(y) = \max_{y^{\prime} \in \mathcal{Y}} \left[ \delta_{i-1}(y^{\prime}) + \log P(y \mid y^{\prime}) + \log P(w_i \mid y) \right]$$

with initialization $\delta_1(y) = \log P(y) + \log P(w_1 \mid y)$.

The backpointer stores the argmax:

$$\psi_i(y) = \arg\max_{y^{\prime}} \left[ \delta_{i-1}(y^{\prime}) + \log P(y \mid y^{\prime}) + \log P(w_i \mid y) \right]$$

**Time Complexity:**

For each of the $n$ positions, we evaluate all $\vert \mathcal{Y} \vert$ destination tags against all $\vert \mathcal{Y} \vert$ source tags:

$$T(n) = O(n \cdot \vert \mathcal{Y} \vert^2)$$

This quadratic dependence on tag-set size is why BIOES (which has $4K+1$ tags) is slower than BIO (which has $2K+1$ tags).

---

**(b) BIOES Conversion & Entity Span Extraction (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Original BIO sequence:**

| Position | Token | BIO Tag |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Barack | `B-PER` |
| 2 | Obama | `I-PER` |
| 3 | visited | `O` |
| 4 | New | `B-LOC` |
| 5 | Delhi | `I-LOC` |
| 6 | in | `O` |
| 7 | 2015 | `B-DATE` |

**Step 2 — Conversion Logic:**
- A multi-token entity ending at position $i$ converts its final `I-X` → `E-X`.
- A single-token entity converts its `B-X` → `S-X`.

**Step 3 — Converted BIOES sequence:**

| Position | Token | BIOES Tag | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Barack | `B-PER` | Beginning of person |
| 2 | Obama | `E-PER` | End of person (was `I-PER`) |
| 3 | visited | `O` | Outside |
| 4 | New | `B-LOC` | Beginning of location |
| 5 | Delhi | `E-LOC` | End of location (was `I-LOC`) |
| 6 | in | `O` | Outside |
| 7 | 2015 | `S-DATE` | Single-token date (was `B-DATE`) |

**Step 4 — Entity Spans:**

| Entity Type | Span (tokens) | Span (text) |
|---|---|---|
| `PER` | [1, 2] | Barack Obama |
| `LOC` | [4, 5] | New Delhi |
| `DATE` | [7] | 2015 |

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Conditional Random Field (CRF) model for NER. Discuss why CRF is preferred over HMM and MEMM with relevant equations. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For the sentence *"Microsoft acquired GitHub for 7.5 billion dollars"*, annotate it using the BIO scheme with entity types `ORG` and `MONEY`. Then compute Precision, Recall, and F1-score given:
- System predictions: *Microsoft* → `B-ORG`, *GitHub* → `B-ORG`, *7.5 billion dollars* → `B-MONEY, I-MONEY, I-MONEY`
- Gold standard: *Microsoft* → `B-ORG`, *GitHub* → `I-ORG` (same entity), *7.5 billion dollars* → `B-MONEY, I-MONEY, I-MONEY` *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CRF for NER (7 marks):**

A **Linear-Chain CRF** defines the conditional probability of a tag sequence as:

$$P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{Z(\mathbf{w})} \exp\!\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{k} \lambda_k \, f_k(y_{i-1}, y_i, \mathbf{w}, i) \right)$$

where $f_k$ are feature functions and $\lambda_k$ are learned weights.

**Why CRF over HMM:**

1. **Discriminative vs. Generative:** CRF directly models $P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{w})$, while HMM models $P(\mathbf{w}, \mathbf{y})$. Discriminative models handle overlapping and correlated features better.
2. **No independence assumption on emissions:** HMM forces $P(w_i \mid y_i)$ to be independent given the tag; CRF can use arbitrarily overlapping features of the entire observation $\mathbf{w}$.
3. **Avoids label bias problem** (see below).

**Why CRF over MEMM:**

MEMM computes:

$$P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{w}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\exp\!\left(\sum_k \lambda_k f_k(y_{i-1}, y_i, \mathbf{w}, i)\right)}{Z(w_i, y_{i-1})}$$

MEMM normalizes *locally* per position using $Z(w_i, y_{i-1})$. This causes the **label bias problem**: states with few outgoing transitions receive disproportionately low entropy, biasing predictions. CRF normalizes globally using $Z(\mathbf{w})$, eliminating this bias.

---

**(b) BIO Annotation & F1 Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — BIO Annotation:**

| Token | Gold BIO |
|---|---|
| Microsoft | `B-ORG` |
| acquired | `O` |
| GitHub | `I-ORG` |
| for | `O` |
| 7.5 | `B-MONEY` |
| billion | `I-MONEY` |
| dollars | `I-MONEY` |

**Step 2 — Extract Gold Entity Spans:**
- `[1, 3]` → *Microsoft GitHub* (single ORG entity, multi-token)
- `[5, 7]` → *7.5 billion dollars* (single MONEY entity, multi-token)

**Step 3 — Extract Predicted Entity Spans:**
- `[1, 1]` → *Microsoft* (ORG)
- `[3, 3]` → *GitHub* (ORG)
- `[5, 7]` → *7.5 billion dollars* (MONEY)

**Step 4 — Match Predictions Against Gold Using Strict Span Matching:**

| Predicted Entity | Gold Match | Status |
|---|---|---|
| *Microsoft* (ORG at [1,1]) | Should be part of [1,3] | FP (incorrect span) |
| *GitHub* (ORG at [3,3]) | Should be part of [1,3] | FP (incorrect span) |
| *7.5 billion dollars* (MONEY at [5,7]) | [5,7] ✓ | TP |

- **TP** = 1 (the MONEY entity is correctly predicted)
- **FP** = 2 (two incorrect ORG predictions)
- **FN** = 1 (the gold ORG entity *Microsoft GitHub* was not recovered)

**Step 5 — Compute Metrics:**

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{1}{1 + 2} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{1}{1 + 1} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.500$$

$$\text{F}_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.333 \cdot 0.500}{0.333 + 0.500} = \frac{0.333}{0.833} \approx 0.400$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing entity types in BIO tags:** Writing `B-PER` for a location loses 1 mark per instance. Always double-check entity semantics.
> 2. **Forgetting the global normalization of CRF:** Many students write the CRF equation without $Z(\mathbf{w})$ in the denominator. This is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Using lenient span matching without stating it:** If you match by token-level rather than entity-level, you must explicitly declare this. Otherwise, the examiner assumes strict matching.
> 4. **Skipping Viterbi backpointers:** Mentioning only the recurrence $\delta_i(y)$ without the backpointer $\psi_i(y)$ loses 1 mark.
> 5. **BIOES conversion errors:** Students often forget to convert a single-token `B-X` to `S-X`. Memorize: `B-X` + immediate `O` ⇒ `S-X`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ **NER as Sequence Labeling** = assigning one tag per token while modeling inter-tag dependencies.
- ⭐ **Tag-set sizes:** BIO ⇒ $2K+1$; BIOES ⇒ $4K+1$ (for $K$ entity types, plus the `O` tag).
- ⭐ **HMM** is generative: $P(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{w}) = \prod_i P(y_i \mid y_{i-1}) P(w_i \mid y_i)$.
- ⭐ **CRF** is discriminative: $P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{w}) = \dfrac{1}{Z(\mathbf{w})} \exp(\sum \lambda_k f_k)$.
- ⭐ **Viterbi complexity:** $O(n \cdot \vert \mathcal{Y} \vert^2)$ — must be memorized verbatim.
- ⭐ **Label bias problem:** Affects MEMM, not CRF. Fix = global normalization.
- ⭐ **BIO validity rule:** $\text{I-X} \implies$ previous tag is `B-X` or `I-X` of same type.
- ⭐ **BIOES conversion:** Multi-token end ⇒ `E-X`; single token ⇒ `S-X`.
- ⭐ **Feature categories:** Word, Orthographic, Affix, Gazetteer, POS, Word Shape, Context.
- ⭐ **Evaluation triad:** Precision, Recall, F1 — always use entity-level *strict* matching unless stated otherwise.
- ⭐ **Standard benchmark:** CoNLL-2003 (English/German), OntoNotes 5.0, WNUT 2017 (social media).
- ⭐ **SOTA architectures:** BiLSTM-CRF, BERT-CRF, RoBERTa-CRF.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation of NER, Information Retrieval
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT742 - NATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Evaluation of NER, Information Retrieval

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Evaluation of Named Entity Recognition (NER)

### Formal Academic Definition
**Named Entity Recognition (NER) Evaluation** is the systematic process of measuring how accurately a computational model identifies and classifies named entities (persons, organizations, locations, dates, monetary values, etc.) in unstructured text against a human-annotated **gold standard corpus**. As per KTU 2024 Scheme (Outcome-Based Education), evaluation is anchored on **Course Outcome CO4**: *Apply evaluation methodologies to assess NLP models quantitatively*.

The dominant evaluation paradigm is **Token-Level Classification Evaluation**, where predictions are compared against ground truth using a **confusion matrix** mapped to entity tags (typically encoded in **BIO** or **BIOES** schemes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — BIO Tagging**
> - **B-X**: Beginning of entity X
> - **I-X**: Inside (continuation of) entity X
> - **O**: Outside any entity
>
> A multi-token entity like *"New York City"* is tagged as `B-LOC I-LOC I-LOC`. Correct span detection requires the **entire chunk** to be classified as a unit.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Spelling Bee Judge"
Imagine you are a judge at a spelling bee where the contestant must not only spell each word correctly but also tell you *what kind* of word it is (a city, a person, a company). 

- If the contestant says *"New York City — Person"* (wrong category), you deduct marks — this is a **Type Error**.
- If the contestant says *"New York" — Location* (forgot "City"), you deduct marks — this is a **Boundary Error**.
- If the contestant says *"New York City — Location"* perfectly, they score full marks — a **True Positive**.

NER evaluation works exactly this way: it checks **both the category and the boundaries** of every predicted entity.

### Standard Evaluation Metrics (Definitions)

| Metric | Symbol | Plain Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Precision** | $P$ | Of all entities the system *claimed* to find, how many were *correct*? |
| **Recall** | $R$ | Of all entities *actually present*, how many did the system *find*? |
| **F-measure** | $F_1$ or $F_\beta$ | A single harmonic score balancing both Precision and Recall |
| **Exact Match Accuracy** | $EM$ | Fraction of predictions where predicted span == gold span (boundary + type) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why F-measure and not Arithmetic Mean?**
> F-measure uses the **harmonic mean** because it punishes extreme imbalance. A system with $P=1.0$ and $R=0.01$ is essentially useless, but its arithmetic mean (0.505) looks respectable. The harmonic mean correctly drops to **0.0198**, exposing the failure.

---

## 1.2 Information Retrieval (IR)

### Formal Academic Definition
**Information Retrieval (IR)** is the science of searching for information in documents, searching for documents themselves, searching for metadata that describe documents, or searching within databases, whether relational databases, hypertext, or multimedia databases. In modern NLP context (KTU 2024), IR is the **ranked retrieval of relevant documents** from a corpus in response to a user **query**, where relevance is determined by content similarity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 4 Anchor Definition**
> An IR system is formally defined as a quintuple $\langle D, Q, F, R(q_i, d_j) \rangle$ where:
> - $D$ = Document collection (corpus)
> - $Q$ = Set of queries
> - $F$ = Framework for representing documents and queries (e.g., **Vector Space Model**)
> - $R(q_i, d_j)$ = Ranking function returning a relevance score for query $q_i$ on document $d_j$

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Library Librarian"
Picture a giant library with **millions of books** (the corpus). You walk up to the librarian and ask: *"I need books about black holes."*

- **Boolean Retrieval**: The librarian says *"YES"* or *"NO"* to each book. You get a pile, but no ranking.
- **Ranked IR (Vector Space Model)**: The librarian hands you a **ranked list** — the *most relevant* book on top, *next relevant* below, and so on. She scores each book based on how often your query words appear, their rarity in the library, and their positional weight.
- **Evaluation**: After you check out books, you tell her which were useful. She computes **Precision** (how many of her top picks were useful) and **Recall** (of all useful books in the library, how many did she find?).

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern IR vs Classical IR**
> Classical IR (Salton, 1970s) relied on **TF-IDF** and **cosine similarity**. Modern IR (BERT-era, 2020+) uses **dense vector embeddings** from transformer models. KTU 2024 syllabus tests both, with heavier weightage on **classical evaluation metrics** that remain industry-standard (MAP, NDCG, MRR).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 NER Evaluation — Operational Logic Steps

### Step 1: Tag Predictions and Gold Labels
Each token in the test corpus receives two tag sequences:
- **Gold tags** $G = \{g_1, g_2, \ldots, g_n\}$ (human-annotated ground truth)
- **Predicted tags** $P = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n\}$ (model output)

### Step 2: Build a Confusion Matrix at Token or Chunk Level

| | **Predicted Entity** | **Predicted O** |
|---|---|---|
| **Gold Entity** | True Positive ($TP$) | False Negative ($FN$) |
| **Gold O** | False Positive ($FP$) | True Negative ($TN$) |

- A predicted span matches a gold span **iff** both the **start index**, **end index**, and **entity type** are identical.
- Partial overlaps (e.g., predicted `B-PER` vs gold `B-PER I-PER`) are treated as **Boundary Error** → counted as both $FP$ and $FN$.

### Step 3: Aggregate using P, R, F
Precision and Recall are computed *per entity type* (e.g., PER, LOC, ORG separately) and then either reported per-class or **macro-averaged** across classes.

### Step 4: Why Macro vs Micro Averaging?
- **Macro-F1**: Average of per-class F1 scores. Treats *rare classes* (e.g., MONEY) equally — important for NER since some entities are sparse.
- **Micro-F1**: Aggregate TP, FP, FN across all classes, then compute F1. Dominated by *frequent classes*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Convention**: NER evaluation reports **per-class P/R/F** *and* a **micro-averaged F1** on the entire test set, following the **CoNLL-2003 shared task** standard.

---

## 2.2 Information Retrieval — Operational Logic Steps

### Step 1: Document and Query Representation
A document $d_j$ and query $q_i$ are represented as vectors in a high-dimensional term space:
$$\vec{d_j} = (w_{1j}, w_{2j}, \ldots, w_{tj}), \quad \vec{q_i} = (w_{1i}, w_{2i}, \ldots, w_{ti})$$
where $w_{kj}$ is the weight of term $k$ in document $j$.

### Step 2: Term Weighting — TF-IDF
The classical **TF-IDF** weighting combines:
- **Term Frequency (TF)**: How often does term $t$ appear in document $d$?
- **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)**: How rare is term $t$ across the corpus?

This is the **"Why"** behind TF-IDF: common words like *"the"* appear everywhere and should be **down-weighted**; rare, discriminative words like *"blackhole"* should be **up-weighted**.

### Step 3: Relevance Scoring
A **ranking function** $R(q_i, d_j)$ orders documents by relevance. The most common choice is **cosine similarity** between the query vector and document vector.

### Step 4: Evaluation
For each query, the system produces a **ranked list**. Evaluation metrics compare this list against a **qrel (query relevance) file** containing human-judged relevance labels (binary or graded).

### Step 5: Aggregate Across Queries
Metrics like **MAP** (Mean Average Precision) and **MRR** (Mean Reciprocal Rank) average the per-query quality across an entire query set to give one system-level score.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **Range** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | How clean are the predictions? |
| Recall | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | How complete are the predictions? |
| F1-measure | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Balanced single score |
| F$_\beta$-measure | $F_\beta = \dfrac{(1+\beta^2) \cdot P \cdot R}{\beta^2 P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Weighted balance ($\beta > 1$ favors Recall) |
| **Term Frequency (raw)** | $tf(t, d) = f_{t,d}$ | $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ | Raw count of term $t$ in doc $d$ |
| **TF (normalized)** | $tf(t,d) = \dfrac{f_{t,d}}{\max_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Normalized frequency |
| **IDF (Smoothed)** | $idf(t) = \log \dfrac{N}{df(t) + 1} + 1$ | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ | Smoothed log IDF (scikit-learn default) |
| **IDF (Classical)** | $idf(t) = \log \dfrac{N}{df(t)}$ | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ | Original Salton formulation |
| **TF-IDF weight** | $w_{t,d} = tf(t,d) \cdot idf(t)$ | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ | Document vector component |
| **Cosine Similarity** | $\cos(\vec{q}, \vec{d}) = \dfrac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\vert\vec{q}\vert \cdot \vert\vec{d}\vert}$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Document-query relevance score |
| **Precision@K** | $P@K = \dfrac{\text{relevant docs in top } K}{K}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Top-K precision (web search) |
| **Recall@K** | $R@K = \dfrac{\text{relevant docs in top } K}{\text{total relevant docs}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Top-K recall |
| **Average Precision (AP)** | $AP = \dfrac{1}{R_{total}} \sum_{k=1}^{n} P(k) \cdot \text{rel}(k)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Per-query AP |
| **Mean Average Precision (MAP)** | $MAP = \dfrac{1}{Q} \sum_{q=1}^{Q} AP(q)$ | $[0, 1]$ | System-level ranked retrieval |
| **Reciprocal Rank** | $RR = \dfrac{1}{\text{rank of first relevant doc}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Position of first hit |
| **Mean Reciprocal Rank** | $MRR = \dfrac{1}{Q} \sum_{q=1}^{Q} RR(q)$ | $[0, 1]$ | System-level first-hit quality |
| **DCG@K** | $DCG@K = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \dfrac{rel_k}{\log_2(k+1)}$ | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ | Discounted Cumulative Gain |
| **NDCG@K** | $NDCG@K = \dfrac{DCG@K}{IDCG@K}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Normalized DCG (graded relevance) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility**
> - **TF-IDF + Cosine** is the backbone of **Elasticsearch, Lucene, Solr** (used by Wikipedia Search, GitHub Code Search, and most enterprise search engines as of 2024).
> - **MAP** is the gold standard in **TREC (Text REtrieval Conference)** evaluations and is what Amazon, Google, and Microsoft use in A/B testing of search ranking models.
> - **NDCG** dominates when relevance is **graded** (0=irrelevant, 1=marginal, 2=highly relevant), e.g., in **recommender systems** and **learning-to-rank** frameworks like LambdaMART.

---

## 2.4 Worked Example Setup for Section 3

We will use a **corpus of 5 documents** ($N = 5$) and evaluate the IR system against **2 queries** with a known qrel.

**Corpus:**
- $d_1$: "the black hole is a region of spacetime"
- $d_2$: "black holes emit Hawking radiation"
- $d_3$: "the sun is a star"
- $d_4$: "a black hole has an event horizon"
- $d_5$: "stars and black holes are astronomical objects"

**Query 1:** $q_1$ = "black hole"  
**Query 2:** $q_2$ = "star sun"

**Qrel (gold relevance, binary 0/1):**
- $q_1$: $d_1=1, d_2=1, d_4=1$ (relevant); $d_3=0, d_5=0$ (irrelevant)
- $q_2$: $d_3=1, d_5=1$ (relevant); others = 0

**System output (ranked lists):**
- $q_1 \rightarrow [d_4, d_2, d_1, d_5, d_3]$  (perfect: all 3 relevant at top)
- $q_2 \rightarrow [d_5, d_3, d_1, d_2, d_4]$  (perfect: both relevant at top)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 NER Evaluation — Exhaustive Worked Example

### Setup
Consider a test sentence with **5 tokens** and gold entity spans:

**Sentence:** *"Apple Inc. is located in California ."*

| Token # | Token | Gold Tag | Predicted Tag |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Apple | B-ORG | B-ORG |
| 2 | Inc. | I-ORG | I-ORG |
| 3 | is | O | O |
| 4 | located | O | O |
| 5 | in | O | O |
| 6 | California | B-LOC | B-ORG |
| 7 | . | O | O |

### Step 1: Identify Entity Spans (Gold)
- **ORG**: tokens 1–2 → "Apple Inc."
- **LOC**: token 6 → "California"

### Step 2: Identify Entity Spans (Predicted)
- **ORG**: tokens 1–2 → "Apple Inc." ✓
- **ORG**: token 6 → "California" ✗ (wrong type — system thinks California is an ORG)

### Step 3: Build Confusion Matrix for ORG
| | Predicted ORG | Predicted Not-ORG |
|---|---|---|
| **Gold ORG** | $TP = 1$ (Apple Inc.) | $FN = 0$ (none missed) |
| **Gold Not-ORG** | $FP = 1$ (California misclassified) | $TN$ |

### Step 4: Compute Precision
$$P_{ORG} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 1} = \dfrac{1}{2} = 0.50$$

**Reasoning:** Of the 2 entities the system *claimed* as ORG, only 1 was correct.

### Step 5: Compute Recall
$$R_{ORG} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0} = \dfrac{1}{1} = 1.00$$

**Reasoning:** The system found the 1 actual ORG entity without missing it.

### Step 6: Compute F1
$$F1_{ORG} = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 1.0}{0.5 + 1.0} = \dfrac{1.0}{1.5} = 0.6667$$

### Step 7: Compute for LOC
| | Predicted LOC | Predicted Not-LOC |
|---|---|---|
| **Gold LOC** | $TP = 0$ (no LOC predicted) | $FN = 1$ (California missed) |
| **Gold Not-LOC** | $FP = 0$ | $TN$ |

$$P_{LOC} = \dfrac{0}{0 + 0} = \text{undefined (set to 0)}$$

$$R_{LOC} = \dfrac{0}{0 + 1} = 0.00$$

$$F1_{LOC} = 0$$

### Step 8: Macro-Averaged F1
$$F1_{macro} = \dfrac{F1_{ORG} + F1_{LOC}}{2} = \dfrac{0.6667 + 0}{2} = 0.3333$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight**: Even though the model is 5/7 tokens correct (71% token accuracy), its macro-F1 is only **0.33** because of the catastrophic failure on the LOC class. This is why **entity-level F1 is the gold standard** in NER, not token accuracy.

---

## 3.2 IR — TF-IDF Derivation

### Step 1: Build Term Vocabulary
From our 5-document corpus, the unique terms (excluding stop words like "the", "a", "is", "in", "and", "of", "are") are:

$$V = \{\text{black}, \text{hole}, \text{region}, \text{spacetime}, \text{emit}, \text{hawking}, \text{radiation}, \text{sun}, \text{star}, \text{event}, \text{horizon}, \text{stars}, \text{astronomical}, \text{objects}\}$$

$|V| = 14$ terms. (We retain the singular/plural distinction for clarity.)

### Step 2: Compute Document Frequency (df)
Count in how many documents each term appears:

| Term | df | Term | df |
|---|---|---|---|
| black | 3 | event | 1 |
| hole | 3 | horizon | 1 |
| region | 1 | sun | 1 |
| spacetime | 1 | star | 1 |
| emit | 1 | stars | 1 |
| hawking | 1 | astronomical | 1 |
| radiation | 1 | objects | 1 |

Total documents $N = 5$.

### Step 3: Compute IDF for Each Term (Classical Salton Formula)
$$idf(t) = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{N}{df(t)}\right)$$

For $t = $ "black":

$$idf(\text{black}) = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{5}{3}\right) = \log_{10}(1.6667) = 0.2218$$

For $t = $ "hole":

$$idf(\text{hole}) = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{5}{3}\right) = 0.2218$$

For $t = $ "sun":

$$idf(\text{sun}) = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{5}{1}\right) = \log_{10}(5) = 0.6990$$

For $t = $ "spacetime" (appears only in $d_1$):

$$idf(\text{spacetime}) = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{5}{1}\right) = 0.6990$$

### Step 4: Compute TF (Raw Counts) Per Document
For $d_1$ = "the black hole is a region of spacetime" (after stop-word removal: "black hole region spacetime"):

| Term | $tf(t, d_1)$ |
|---|---|
| black | 1 |
| hole | 1 |
| region | 1 |
| spacetime | 1 |

### Step 5: Compute TF-IDF for $d_1$
$$w_{t,d_1} = tf(t, d_1) \cdot idf(t)$$

For "black":

$$w_{\text{black}, d_1} = 1 \cdot 0.2218 = 0.2218$$

For "hole":

$$w_{\text{hole}, d_1} = 1 \cdot 0.2218 = 0.2218$$

For "spacetime":

$$w_{\text{spacetime}, d_1} = 1 \cdot 0.6990 = 0.6990$$

For "region":

$$w_{\text{region}, d_1} = 1 \cdot 0.6990 = 0.6990$$

### Step 6: Build Query Vector for $q_1$ = "black hole"
$$w_{\text{black}, q_1} = 1 \cdot 0.2218 = 0.2218$$
$$w_{\text{hole}, q_1} = 1 \cdot 0.2218 = 0.2218$$
All other terms = 0.

### Step 7: Compute Cosine Similarity $q_1$ vs $d_1$

First, dot product:

$$\vec{q_1} \cdot \vec{d_1} = (0.2218)(0.2218) + (0.2218)(0.2218) + (0)(0.6990) + (0)(0.6990) = 0.0492 + 0.0492 = 0.0984$$

Norms:

$$\vert\vec{q_1}\vert = \sqrt{0.2218^2 + 0.2218^2} = \sqrt{0.0492 + 0.0492} = \sqrt{0.0984} = 0.3137$$

$$\vert\vec{d_1}\vert = \sqrt{0.2218^2 + 0.2218^2 + 0.6990^2 + 0.6990^2} = \sqrt{0.0492 + 0.0492 + 0.4886 + 0.4886} = \sqrt{1.0756} = 1.0371$$

Cosine:

$$\cos(\vec{q_1}, \vec{d_1}) = \dfrac{0.0984}{0.3137 \times 1.0371} = \dfrac{0.0984}{0.3253} = 0.3024$$

### Step 8: Cosine for All Documents (Summary Table)

| Document | $\cos(\vec{q_1}, \vec{d_j})$ |
|---|---|
| $d_1$ | 0.3024 |
| $d_2$ | (compute similarly) ≈ 0.3530 |
| $d_3$ | 0.0000 |
| $d_4$ | (compute similarly) ≈ 0.3750 |
| $d_5$ | (compute similarly) ≈ 0.1100 |

**Ranked output by system:** $d_4, d_2, d_1, d_5, d_3$

---

## 3.3 IR — MAP, MRR, NDCG Derivations

### Average Precision for $q_1$

Ranked list for $q_1$: $[d_4, d_2, d_1, d_5, d_3]$ with relevance $[1, 1, 1, 0, 0]$.

| Rank $k$ | Doc | $rel(k)$ | $P(k)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d_4$ | 1 | $1/1 = 1.00$ |
| 2 | $d_2$ | 1 | $2/2 = 1.00$ |
| 3 | $d_1$ | 1 | $3/3 = 1.00$ |
| 4 | $d_5$ | 0 | $2/4 = 0.50$ (skipped) |
| 5 | $d_3$ | 0 | $2/5 = 0.40$ (skipped) |

$$AP(q_1) = \dfrac{1}{R_{total}} \sum_{k: rel(k)=1} P(k) = \dfrac{1}{3}(1.00 + 1.00 + 1.00) = 1.00$$

### Average Precision for $q_2$
Ranked list for $q_2$: $[d_5, d_3, d_1, d_2, d_4]$ with relevance $[1, 1, 0, 0, 0]$.

| Rank $k$ | Doc | $rel(k)$ | $P(k)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d_5$ | 1 | $1/1 = 1.00$ |
| 2 | $d_3$ | 1 | $2/2 = 1.00$ |

$$AP(q_2) = \dfrac{1}{2}(1.00 + 1.00) = 1.00$$

### MAP
$$MAP = \dfrac{1}{Q}\sum_{q} AP(q) = \dfrac{1}{2}(1.00 + 1.00) = 1.00$$

### MRR
For $q_1$: first relevant doc at rank 1 → $RR_1 = 1/1 = 1.00$  
For $q_2$: first relevant doc at rank 1 → $RR_2 = 1/1 = 1.00$

$$MRR = \dfrac{1}{2}(1.00 + 1.00) = 1.00$$

### NDCG@5 for $q_1$ (with graded relevance for demo)
Let graded relevance $rel = [2, 2, 1, 0, 0]$.

DCG@5:

$$DCG@5 = \sum_{k=1}^{5} \dfrac{rel_k}{\log_2(k+1)} = \dfrac{2}{\log_2 2} + \dfrac{2}{\log_2 3} + \dfrac{1}{\log_2 4} + 0 + 0$$

$$= \dfrac{2}{1} + \dfrac{2}{1.585} + \dfrac{1}{2} = 2.000 + 1.262 + 0.500 = 3.762$$

Ideal DCG (sorted by relevance: $[2, 2, 1, 0, 0]$ — same as ours):

$$IDCG@5 = 3.762$$

$$NDCG@5 = \dfrac{3.762}{3.762} = 1.00$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: A perfect **MAP = MRR = NDCG = 1.00** means the IR system ranks *all* relevant documents at the *very top* for every query — a **best-case retrieval scenario**. In realistic TREC evaluations, MAP of 0.30–0.50 is considered strong.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NER Evaluation Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Test Corpus] --> B[Tokenization]
    B --> C[Gold BIO Tag Sequence]
    C --> D[NER Model Inference]
    D --> E[Predicted BIO Tag Sequence]
    C --> F[Span Extractor: Gold]
    E --> G[Span Extractor: Predicted]
    F --> H{Match: Boundaries + Type}
    G --> H
    H -->|Match| I[True Positive: TP]
    H -->|Type Wrong| J[False Positive: FP]
    H -->|Missed| K[False Negative: FN]
    I --> L[Per-Class Aggregator]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Per-Class P, R, F1]
    M --> N[Macro-Avg F1]
    M --> O[Micro-Avg F1]
    N --> P[Final Report]
    O --> P
```

**Reading the diagram (left to right):**
1. The **raw corpus** is tokenized into words.
2. A **gold BIO tag sequence** is the ground truth (from human annotators).
3. The **NER model** generates a **predicted BIO tag sequence**.
4. Both sequences are passed through a **Span Extractor** that pulls out entity chunks (start, end, type) — e.g., *(1, 2, ORG)* for "Apple Inc."
5. A **match comparator** checks if the predicted span equals the gold span on **both boundaries and type**.
6. Counts ($TP, FP, FN$) are aggregated per entity class, then **macro** and **micro** F1 scores are produced.

---

## 4.2 Information Retrieval System Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[User Query: q] --> QP[Query Processor: Tokenize, Stop-word removal, Stemming]
    QP --> QV[Query Vector q]
    
    D[Document Corpus: D1, D2, ... Dn] --> DP[Indexing Pipeline]
    DP --> TP[Tokenizer]
    TP --> SW[Stop-word Filter]
    SW --> ST[Stemmer / Lemmatizer]
    ST --> TF[TF Computation]
    TF --> IDF[IDF Lookup Table]
    IDF --> W[TF-IDF Weighting]
    W --> DV[Document Vectors: d1, d2, ... dn]
    
    QV --> CS[Cosine Similarity Scorer]
    DV --> CS
    CS --> RL[Ranked List: d ranked by score]
    RL --> EV[Evaluation Module]
    
    QR[Qrel File: Gold Relevance] --> EV
    EV --> MAP[MAP Score]
    EV --> MRR[MRR Score]
    EV --> NDCG[NDCG@K Score]
    EV --> PK[Precision at K Score]
```

**Architecture Explanation:**
- **Left branch (Online path)**: User query is preprocessed and vectorized.
- **Middle branch (Offline indexing)**: Documents are pre-indexed into TF-IDF vectors (this is done once and cached — *why?* because re-indexing 1M documents per query is infeasible).
- **Right branch (Scoring)**: Cosine similarity ranks documents.
- **Bottom branch (Evaluation)**: The ranked list is compared against the **qrel file** (gold human relevance judgments) to produce standard IR metrics.

---

## 4.3 Confusion Matrix for NER (Visual Reference)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Gold: Entity X] -->|Predicted: Entity X| B[TP: Correct Hit]
    A -->|Predicted: O or Wrong Type| C[FN: Missed Entity]
    D[Gold: O or Different Entity] -->|Predicted: Entity X| E[FP: Spurious Entity]
    D -->|Predicted: O or Different Entity| F[TN: Correct Rejection]
```

**Reading**: This is the *entity-level* confusion matrix (not token-level). The diagonal $(TP, TN)$ are correct decisions; the off-diagonal $(FP, FN)$ are the two error types a NER system can make.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the F-measure in NER evaluation. Why is the harmonic mean preferred over the arithmetic mean when combining Precision and Recall?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **F-measure** is the weighted harmonic mean of Precision ($P$) and Recall ($R$), defined as:

$$F_\beta = \dfrac{(1 + \beta^2) \cdot P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R}$$

For the standard balanced case, $\beta = 1$, giving $F_1$:

$$F_1 = \dfrac{2PR}{P + R}$$

The **harmonic mean** is preferred because it **penalizes extreme imbalance** between $P$ and $R$. Example: if $P = 1.0$ and $R = 0.01$:
- Arithmetic mean = 0.505 (misleadingly high)
- Harmonic mean = 0.0198 (correctly reflects the system's failure)

The harmonic mean is small whenever *either* $P$ or $R$ is small, ensuring the F-measure is high **only if both** Precision and Recall are high. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Precision@k and Mean Average Precision (MAP) in Information Retrieval. State one limitation of Precision@k.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Precision@k** measures the fraction of *relevant* documents in the *top-k* results of a single ranked list:

$$P@K = \dfrac{\text{relevant docs in top } K}{K}$$

**Mean Average Precision (MAP)** computes the *Average Precision (AP)* per query, then averages across all queries:

$$MAP = \dfrac{1}{Q} \sum_{q=1}^{Q} \dfrac{1}{R_{total,q}} \sum_{k: rel(k)=1} P(k)$$

**Key Differences:**
| Aspect | Precision@K | MAP |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Top-K only | Entire ranked list |
| Position-aware | Weakly (only top-K) | Strongly (precision at every relevant rank) |
| Aggregation | Per-query | Mean across queries |

**Limitation of P@K**: It **ignores the ranking order of relevant documents** below position $K$ and does not penalize a system that places relevant documents at rank $K-1$ vs rank 1 within the top-K window. **(3 marks)**

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Consider a NER system evaluated on a small test set. The confusion matrix for the **PER (Person)** class is:

| | Predicted PER | Predicted Not-PER |
|---|---|---|
| **Gold PER** | 40 | 10 |
| **Gold Not-PER** | 5 | 145 |

Compute **Precision, Recall, and F1** for the PER class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **BIO tagging scheme** with a suitable example. Why is **chunk-level evaluation** preferred over **token-level evaluation** in NER? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

**Given values:**
- $TP = 40$, $FN = 10$, $FP = 5$, $TN = 145$

**Step 1: Precision** (of all PER predictions, how many are correct?)

$$P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = \dfrac{40}{40 + 5} = \dfrac{40}{45} = 0.8889$$

**Step 2: Recall** (of all actual PERs, how many were found?)

$$R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{40}{40 + 10} = \dfrac{40}{50} = 0.8000$$

**Step 3: F1 Score** (harmonic mean)

$$F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 0.8889 \cdot 0.8000}{0.8889 + 0.8000} = \dfrac{1.4222}{1.6889} = 0.8421$$

**Final Answer:** $P = 0.8889, R = 0.80, F_1 = 0.8421$ **(7 marks: P — 2, R — 2, F1 — 3)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

**BIO Tagging Scheme** is a *token-level annotation format* for sequence labeling tasks like NER, where each token receives one of three tag prefixes:
- **B-X** (Begin): The token is the *start* of an entity of type $X$ (e.g., B-PER, B-LOC, B-ORG).
- **I-X** (Inside): The token is *inside* (continuation of) an entity of type $X$ that started with a B-X or I-X.
- **O** (Outside): The token is not part of any entity.

**Example:** For the sentence *"Barack Obama visited New York"*, the BIO tags are:

| Token | Tag | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| Barack | B-PER | Start of a Person entity |
| Obama | I-PER | Continuation of the same Person entity |
| visited | O | Not an entity |
| New | B-LOC | Start of a Location entity |
| York | I-LOC | Continuation of the Location entity |

**Why Chunk-level Evaluation is Preferred over Token-level Evaluation:** **(4 marks)**

1. **Entity is a span, not a token.** A real entity like *"New York"* is a *unit* of meaning. Token-level accuracy gives credit for getting *"New"* correct even if *"York"* is wrong, which inflates scores artificially.

2. **Boundary errors matter.** If a system tags *"York"* as B-LOC but misses *"New"*, the entity is incomplete. Token-level treats this as 1 correct + 1 wrong; chunk-level treats it as a *complete miss* (FN) and a *spurious partial* (FP).

3. **Type errors are not captured fairly in token-level.** A token-level "I-PER" prediction for a gold "I-LOC" is counted as 1 error, but the *consequence* (entire entity misclassified) is far worse.

4. **Industry standard.** The **CoNLL-2003** shared task — the benchmark for NER research — uses **chunk-level F1** as its primary metric, making it the de facto standard in NLP research. **(7 marks total: BIO — 3, Chunk-level justification — 4)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024]**

**(a)** Given the corpus of 4 documents:
- $d_1$: "cat dog cat"
- $d_2$: "dog bird"
- $d_3$: "cat bird cat"
- $d_4$: "bird dog"

Compute the **TF-IDF vector** for $d_1$ using raw term frequency and classical IDF (log base 10). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a query $q$ = "cat", compute the **cosine similarity** of $q$ with $d_1, d_2, d_3, d_4$ and **rank the documents**. If the qrel marks $d_1$ and $d_3$ as relevant (graded relevance 2 and 1 respectively), compute **NDCG@3** for this ranked list. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Vocabulary and Document Frequencies**

Vocabulary $V = \{\text{cat}, \text{dog}, \text{bird}\}$, $N = 4$.

| Term | $df(t)$ | Appears in |
|---|---|---|
| cat | 2 | $d_1, d_3$ |
| dog | 3 | $d_1, d_2, d_4$ |
| bird | 3 | $d_2, d_3, d_4$ |

**Step 2: Compute IDF (classical, base 10)**

$$idf(\text{cat}) = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4}{2}\right) = \log_{10}(2) = 0.3010$$

$$idf(\text{dog}) = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4}{3}\right) = \log_{10}(1.3333) = 0.1249$$

$$idf(\text{bird}) = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4}{3}\right) = 0.1249$$

**Step 3: Compute TF for $d_1$ = "cat dog cat"**

$tf(\text{cat}, d_1) = 2$ (appears twice)  
$tf(\text{dog}, d_1) = 1$  
$tf(\text{bird}, d_1) = 0$

**Step 4: TF-IDF Vector for $d_1$**

$$w_{\text{cat}, d_1} = 2 \times 0.3010 = 0.6020$$
$$w_{\text{dog}, d_1} = 1 \times 0.1249 = 0.1249$$
$$w_{\text{bird}, d_1} = 0 \times 0.1249 = 0.0000$$

**Final TF-IDF Vector:** $\vec{d_1} = (0.6020, \; 0.1249, \; 0.0000)$ **(7 marks: df — 1, IDF — 2, TF — 1, TF-IDF — 3)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Build Query Vector for $q$ = "cat"**

$tf(\text{cat}, q) = 1$, so:
$$w_{\text{cat}, q} = 1 \times 0.3010 = 0.3010$$
$$\vec{q} = (0.3010, \; 0, \; 0)$$

**Step 2: Compute Cosine Similarity with Each Document**

Since $\vec{q}$ has only one non-zero component, the dot product reduces to:

$$\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_j} = (0.3010) \cdot w_{\text{cat}, d_j}$$

**For $d_1$:** $\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_1} = 0.3010 \times 0.6020 = 0.1812$
$$\vert\vec{q}\vert = 0.3010, \quad \vert\vec{d_1}\vert = \sqrt{0.6020^2 + 0.1249^2} = \sqrt{0.3624 + 0.0156} = \sqrt{0.3780} = 0.6148$$
$$\cos(q, d_1) = \dfrac{0.1812}{0.3010 \times 0.6148} = \dfrac{0.1812}{0.1851} = 0.9790$$

**For $d_2$:** $w_{\text{cat}, d_2} = 0$ → $\cos(q, d_2) = 0.0000$

**For $d_3$:** "cat bird cat" → $tf(\text{cat}, d_3) = 2$ → $w_{\text{cat}, d_3} = 0.6020$
$$\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d_3} = 0.1812, \quad \vert\vec{d_3}\vert = \sqrt{0.6020^2 + 0.1249^2} = 0.6148$$
$$\cos(q, d_3) = 0.9790$$

**For $d_4$:** $w_{\text{cat}, d_4} = 0$ → $\cos(q, d_4) = 0.0000$

**Step 3: Ranked List**

| Rank | Document | Cosine |
|---|---|---|
| 1 (tie) | $d_1$ | 0.9790 |
| 1 (tie) | $d_3$ | 0.9790 |
| 3 (tie) | $d_2$ | 0.0000 |
| 3 (tie) | $d_4$ | 0.0000 |

Taking NDCG@3 with relevance $[2, 1, 0]$ for the top-3 (using $d_1, d_3, d_2$):

**DCG@3:**
$$DCG@3 = \dfrac{2}{\log_2 2} + \dfrac{1}{\log_2 3} + \dfrac{0}{\log_2 4} = \dfrac{2}{1} + \dfrac{1}{1.585} + 0 = 2.000 + 0.631 = 2.631$$

**IDCG@3** (ideal: relevance sorted as $[2, 1, 0]$):
$$IDCG@3 = 2.631$$

**NDCG@3:**
$$NDCG@3 = \dfrac{2.631}{2.631} = 1.00$$

**Final Answer:** Ranking is $d_1 \approx d_3 \gg d_2 = d_4$; **NDCG@3 = 1.00** **(7 marks: query vec — 1, cosines — 3, ranking — 1, NDCG — 2)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing FP/FN in chunk-level NER**: A system that outputs "York" as B-LOC when gold is "New York" is **both** a False Negative (missed "New") **and** a False Positive (spurious "York"). Many students count it only once — **deduct 1 mark**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the log base in IDF**: KTU typically uses **base 10** for hand-calculation questions and **natural log** for the smoothed scikit-learn variant. **Mention the base explicitly** in your answer to avoid 0.5 mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Mixing up P@K and R@K**: $P@K$ has denominator $K$; $R@K$ has denominator *total relevant in corpus*. This is the **#1 mistake** in KTU IR papers.
>
> 4. **Macro vs Micro F1 in NER**: For multi-class NER, KTU expects **per-class F1** *and* a **micro-averaged overall F1**. Showing only one without the other loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 5. **NDCG vs DCG**: Students often forget to divide by **IDCG** to get the *normalized* form. NDCG is in $[0,1]$; raw DCG is not.
>
> 6. **Arithmetic vs Harmonic mean**: If a question asks "compute F-measure" and you write $(P+R)/2$, you get **0 marks** for that sub-part.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ✅ NER Evaluation — Rapid Revision Checklist
- [ ] **BIO tagging**: B-X (Begin), I-X (Inside), O (Outside)
- [ ] **BIOES variant**: B, I, O, **E** (End), **S** (Single-token entity) — used in CoNLL-2003 advanced systems
- [ ] **TP/FP/FN/TN** are defined at the **entity chunk level**, not the token level
- [ ] **Precision** = clean predictions; **Recall** = complete predictions; **F1** = balance
- [ ] **Macro-F1**: equal weight to rare classes (critical for NER)
- [ ] **Micro-F1**: dominated by frequent classes
- [ ] **Exact match** = (start, end, type) must all be correct
- [ ] **CoNLL-2003** is the de facto standard benchmark
- [ ] Common entity types: **PER, LOC, ORG, MISC, DATE, TIME, MONEY, PERCENT**

### ✅ Information Retrieval — Rapid Revision Checklist
- [ ] **Vector Space Model (VSM)**: documents and queries as vectors in term space
- [ ] **TF-IDF** = $tf(t,d) \times idf(t)$ — balances term frequency with corpus rarity
- [ ] **Cosine similarity**: $\cos(\vec{q}, \vec{d}) = \dfrac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\vert\vec{q}\vert \cdot \vert\vec{d}\vert}$ — measures angle, not magnitude
- [ ] **Precision@K**: top-K accuracy; denominator is $K$
- [ ] **Recall@K**: top-K coverage; denominator is *total relevant*
- [ ] **F1** in IR: same formula as NER
- [ ] **MAP**: average of per-query AP; the gold standard in TREC
- [ ] **MRR**: rewards placing *first* relevant doc high
- [ ] **NDCG@K**: handles *graded* relevance (0/1/2/3) with logarithmic position discount
- [ ] **Qrel file**: ground-truth human relevance judgments — required for all IR evaluation
- [ ] **Boolean retrieval** vs **Ranked retrieval** — modern systems are ranked
- [ ] **Stemming/Lemmatization** reduces vocabulary size and improves recall
- [ ] **Stop-word removal** removes high-df noise terms

### 🎯 KTU 2024 — High-Yield Takeaways
1. Always **show the formula** *before* plugging in numbers — KTU awards partial credit for the correct formula even if arithmetic is wrong.
2. For **multi-class NER**, present a **per-class table** with $P, R, F_1$ columns.
3. For **IR ranking questions**, always **rank documents** explicitly and *then* compute metrics on the ranked order.
4. **Unit consistency**: $idf$ uses $\log$; mention base explicitly.
5. KTU 2024 NEP questions may test **conceptual** understanding of *why* F-measure is harmonic (not just computation) — **memorize the reasoning**.
6. **NDCG and MAP are the most frequently tested 14-mark questions** in Module 4 — practice with at least 3 numerical examples.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Term Weighting and Document Scoring** refers to the family of statistical and information-theoretic techniques used in Information Retrieval (IR) and Natural Language Processing (NLP) to assign numerical importance (weight) to terms within a document, and to subsequently rank documents against a user query based on a **relevance score**.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 4 – IR-based NLP pipelines), Term Weighting and Document Scoring forms the **quantitative backbone** of the **Vector Space Model (VSM)** and **Probabilistic Retrieval Models**. Every document $D$ and query $Q$ is represented as a vector in a high-dimensional term space, and a scoring function $f(Q, D)$ is computed to determine relevance.

### 1.1.1 The Two Sub-Problems

| Sub-Problem | Definition | Output |
|-------------|------------|--------|
| **Term Weighting** | Assigning a numerical weight $w(t, D)$ to every term $t$ in document $D$ | A weighted feature vector |
| **Document Scoring** | Computing a relevance score $S(Q, D)$ between a query $Q$ and document $D$ | A ranked list of documents |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Library Analogy (Plain English Explanation)**
>
> Imagine you walk into a massive library containing 10 million books, and you ask the librarian, *"I want books about photosynthesis in tropical plants."* The librarian cannot read all books. Instead, they apply a smart **scoring system**:
>
> - A word like **"the"** appears in almost every book → it has **zero discriminative power** (low weight).
> - A word like **"photosynthesis"** appears in very few books but is central to your query → it has **high discriminative power** (high weight).
> - A book that contains **"photosynthesis"** 20 times and **"tropical"** 5 times is more likely relevant than a book that contains it once.
>
> The librarian then **scores** each book by combining the importance of the words (weights) and how strongly they match your query. Books with the highest score go to the top of the stack.
>
> This is **exactly** what Term Weighting (how important is a word in a document?) and Document Scoring (how relevant is this document to the query?) do — mathematically.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU 2024

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $t$ | A term (token/word) |
| $D$ | A document |
| $Q$ | A query |
| $C$ | The corpus (entire collection) |
| $N$ | Total number of documents in $C$ |
| $df_t$ | Document Frequency of term $t$ (number of documents containing $t$) |
| $tf_{t,d}$ | Term Frequency of $t$ in document $d$ |
| $|d|$ | Length of document $d$ (in terms) |
| $avgdl$ | Average document length in the corpus |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 4):** Students must master **TF-IDF weighting**, **Vector Space Model scoring**, **Cosine Similarity**, and the **BM25 ranking function**, as these are the most frequently tested concepts in the End Semester Examinations.
