# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define Precision and Recall. Why are they often in tension with each other?

**Model Answer:**

**Precision (P)** is the fraction of retrieved documents that are relevant:

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

**Recall (R)** is the fraction of relevant documents that are retrieved:

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**Tension:** They are in tension because increasing the number of retrieved documents (e.g., lowering the relevance threshold) generally improves recall but pulls in more irrelevant documents, decreasing precision. Conversely, restricting retrieval to only the most confident matches raises precision but may miss relevant documents, lowering recall. This **Precision-Recall trade-off** is fundamental to IR system design.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] What is the F-measure? Why is the harmonic mean used instead of the arithmetic mean?

**Model Answer:**

The **F-measure (F1-score)** is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, providing a single balanced metric:

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$$

**Why harmonic mean?** The harmonic mean **penalizes extreme values**. If either P = 0 or R = 0, then F1 = 0 (the system has failed on at least one dimension). In contrast, the arithmetic mean of (P=1, R=0) would be 0.5, falsely suggesting a moderately good system. The harmonic mean correctly reflects that a system must perform well on **both** precision and recall to achieve a high F1.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer ONE full question (with sub-parts) from a choice of two.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Cranfield evaluation methodology** for Information Retrieval systems. Describe the components of a test collection and the role of relevance judgments.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a given query, a search engine returns the following 10 documents in rank order. The ground truth says documents D2, D4, D5, D8 are relevant. Compute **Precision, Recall, F1, and Average Precision (AP)**. Assume there are exactly 6 relevant documents in the collection.

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Cranfield Methodology

The **Cranfield methodology**, pioneered by Cyril Cleverdon in 1966, is the standard laboratory-style framework for IR evaluation. It enables reproducible, comparative experiments by fixing all variables except the system being tested.

**The three fixed components of a test collection are:**

1. **Document Collection (D):** A static, representative corpus (e.g., TREC disks, CACM, Cranfield 1400 abstracts). It is held constant so that all systems are evaluated on identical input.

2. **Query Set (Q):** A benchmark set of information needs called "topics" or "queries." Modern benchmarks (TREC, CLEF) use 50–300 queries with full descriptions of the information need.

3. **Relevance Judgments (Qrels):** Human-assessed, query-document pairs marked as relevant (or graded). For large collections, **pooling** is used: only the top-k results from all participating systems are judged.

**Role of Relevance Judgments:** They provide the **ground truth** against which system output is compared. Without qrels, retrieval quality is unmeasurable. The reliability of qrels is itself measured using **Cohen's Kappa** to quantify inter-annotator agreement.

**Strengths:** Reproducible, quantitative, comparable across systems.
**Weaknesses:** Static collections age; user interaction is abstracted away.

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Numerical Computation

**Given data:**
- Retrieved set (in order): D1, D2, D3, D4, D5, D6, D7, D8, D9, D10
- Relevant (in collection): D2, D4, D5, D8, Dx, Dy → total = 6
- Relevant retrieved (TP) = 4 (D2, D4, D5, D8)
- Retrieved not relevant (FP) = 10 − 4 = 6
- Relevant not retrieved (FN) = 6 − 4 = 2

**Step 1: Precision**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{4}{4 + 6} = \frac{4}{10} = 0.400
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Recall**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{4}{4 + 2} = \frac{4}{6} \approx 0.667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: F1-Score**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.400 \cdot 0.667}{0.400 + 0.667} = \frac{0.534}{1.067} \approx 0.500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Average Precision**

Relevant documents are at ranks **2, 4, 5, 8**. Compute $P@k$ at each:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P@2 &= \frac{1}{2} = 0.500 \\
P@4 &= \frac{2}{4} = 0.500 \\
P@5 &= \frac{3}{5} = 0.600 \\
P@8 &= \frac{4}{8} = 0.500
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum and divide by total relevant (6):

$$
\begin{aligned}
AP &= \frac{1}{6} \cdot (0.500 + 0.500 + 0.600 + 0.500) = \frac{2.100}{6} = 0.350
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> 1. **Common Error 1:** Forgetting to use *total relevant in collection* (6) in the recall formula, instead using *retrieved relevant* (4). This yields an incorrect R = 1.0 and loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Common Error 2:** Dividing AP by the *number of retrieved relevant* (4) instead of *total relevant in collection* (6). The correct denominator for AP is $R_{\text{total}}$, the total number of relevant documents in the *entire collection*, not just those retrieved.
> 3. **Common Error 3:** Confusing the AP denominator with the MAP denominator. AP uses $R_{\text{total}}$; MAP averages AP across queries.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain **MAP, MRR, and NDCG** evaluation metrics. State one practical application scenario where each is most appropriate.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A search engine returns 5 documents with graded relevance scores `[2, 0, 3, 1, 2]` at ranks 1 to 5. Compute **DCG@5 and NDCG@5**. Comment on the system's ranking quality.

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Three Advanced Metrics

**1. Mean Average Precision (MAP):**
MAP is the mean of Average Precision (AP) scores across a set of queries. For each query, AP captures the area under its precision-recall curve. MAP is **single-valued, stable, and widely used for comparing ranking algorithms**.

$$MAP = \frac{1}{\vert Q \vert} \sum_{q=1}^{\vert Q \vert} \frac{1}{R_q} \sum_{k=1}^{n} P_q(k) \cdot \text{rel}_q(k)$$

**Best application:** Comparing two ranking algorithms (e.g., BM25 vs. TF-IDF) on a benchmark like TREC. It is the *de facto* standard in academic IR papers.

**2. Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR):**
MRR measures how early the system returns the *first* relevant document, averaged across queries.

$$MRR = \frac{1}{\vert Q \vert} \sum_{i=1}^{\vert Q \vert} \frac{1}{\text{rank}_i}$$

**Best application:** Question Answering systems, navigational queries ("Facebook login"), and factoid retrieval where a single correct answer suffices.

**3. Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG):**
NDCG handles **graded relevance** (multi-level judgments). It applies a logarithmic discount to lower ranks, so that a relevant document at rank 1 contributes much more than the same document at rank 10. Normalization by the ideal ranking (IDCG) yields a [0, 1] score.

$$NDCG_p = \frac{DCG_p}{IDCG_p}, \quad DCG_p = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \frac{rel_i}{\log_2(i+1)}$$

**Best application:** Web search and recommendation systems where relevance is multi-graded and the user's attention is concentrated at the top of the list.

#### Model Solution — Part (b): DCG & NDCG Computation

**Given:** Graded relevance at ranks 1–5: `[2, 0, 3, 1, 2]`.

**Step 1: Compute DCG@5**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DCG_5 &= \sum_{i=1}^{5} \frac{rel_i}{\log_2(i+1)} \\
      &= \frac{2}{\log_2 2} + \frac{0}{\log_2 3} + \frac{3}{\log_2 4} + \frac{1}{\log_2 5} + \frac{2}{\log_2 6} \\
      &= \frac{2}{1.000} + \frac{0}{1.585} + \frac{3}{2.000} + \frac{1}{2.322} + \frac{2}{2.585} \\
      &= 2.000 + 0.000 + 1.500 + 0.431 + 0.774 \\
      &= 4.705
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute IDCG@5 (Ideal Ranking)**

Ideal order sorts by relevance descending: `[3, 2, 2, 1, 0]`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
IDCG_5 &= \frac{3}{\log_2 2} + \frac{2}{\log_2 3} + \frac{2}{\log_2 4} + \frac{1}{\log_2 5} + \frac{0}{\log_2 6} \\
       &= \frac{3}{1.000} + \frac{2}{1.585} + \frac{2}{2.000} + \frac{1}{2.322} + \frac{0}{2.585} \\
       &= 3.000 + 1.262 + 1.000 + 0.431 + 0.000 \\
       &= 5.693
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute NDCG@5**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NDCG_5 &= \frac{DCG_5}{IDCG_5} = \frac{4.705}{5.693} \approx 0.826
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on Ranking Quality:** NDCG@5 = 0.826 indicates **good ranking quality**. The system placed the most relevant document (relevance = 3) at rank 3 instead of rank 1, costing some score. The logarithmic discount model penalizes this misplacement moderately (since the discount at rank 3 is only $\log_2 4 = 2$, not a steep penalty). To improve, the system should rank by *predicted relevance score* in descending order before outputting.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> 1. **Common Error 1:** Using natural logarithm $\ln$ instead of $\log_2$ in the DCG formula. Always use **base-2 logarithm** for the standard DCG formulation. Using $\log_{10}$ or $\ln$ yields a wrong numerical answer and forfeits 2 marks.
> 2. **Common Error 2:** Forgetting to compute IDCG using the *ideal* sorted ranking, not the system's actual ranking. The ideal ranking is obtained by sorting the relevance vector in descending order.
> 3. **Common Error 3:** Dividing DCG by something other than IDCG. A common mistake is normalizing by the maximum possible sum (e.g., $\sum rel_i$) — this is **incorrect**; IDCG is the only valid normalizer.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cranfield Paradigm** is the foundation of modern IR evaluation; it fixes documents, queries, and qrels.
- **TREC** is the most influential real-world implementation of the Cranfield paradigm (since 1992).
- **Precision (P)** = TP / (TP + FP) — purity of results.
- **Recall (R)** = TP / (TP + FN) — completeness of results.
- **F1-Score** = $2PR / (P + R)$ — harmonic mean, penalizes weak performance in either P or R.
- **Fβ-Score** — use $\beta > 1$ when recall matters more (e.g., legal/medical); $\beta < 1$ when precision matters more (e.g., web search top-k).
- **P@k** — precision at rank cut-off `k`; **MAP** averages AP across queries.
- **MRR** = mean reciprocal rank — best for QA, navigational queries.
- **DCG/NDCG** — handles graded (multi-level) relevance; NDCG is normalized to [0, 1].
- **Cohen's Kappa** measures inter-annotator agreement on qrels; $\kappa > 0.8$ is considered reliable.
- **Pooling** is the practical method for generating qrels in massive collections.
- **Trade-off**: Always state the use case — there is no single "best" metric. P-R trade-off, MAP vs. NDCG vs. MRR — each serves a different engineering goal.
- **Use base-2 logarithm** in all DCG computations; use $R_{\text{total}}$ (not retrieved) as the AP denominator.
- **KTU 2024 high-yield**: Numerical AP/MAP and DCG/NDCG problems appear almost every semester — practice the worked examples above.
